Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bodo (code=brx, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Chirang.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Bodo.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Salt Reclamation Worker

professional_persona: An aspiring senior salt reclamation technician, Kenshing Paul combines hands‑on expertise in brine management and pump maintenance with a vivid imagination for low‑cost solar‑assisted drying, and they meticulously plan each shift with detailed checklists.
linguistic_persona: Linguistically adept, Kenshing Paul speaks their mother tongue fluently and writes it in Devanagari, reads and writes Hindi at a functional level, converses comfortably in Assamese for marketplace interactions, and picks up basic English phrases from church sermons, allowing them to switch languages as the situation demands.
sports_persona: A keen cricket enthusiast, Kenshing Paul plays weekly street matches with friends, avidly follows the Assam cricket team and the Delhi Capitals in the IPL, and they keep fit by jogging through the salt fields during early mornings.
arts_persona: A creative soul, Kenshing Paul spends afternoons sketching the salt ponds and surrounding fields, drawing inspiration from regional folk singer Lunjor Mingi’s melodies and Bollywood vocalist Arijit Singh’s emotive ballads, while sharpening techniques through tutorials on the YouTube channel ‘Art of Sketching’.
travel_persona: A modest traveler, Kenshing Paul enjoys weekend trips to nearby attractions like Kaziranga National Park and Shillong, dreams of a pilgrimage to Sikkim's Buddhist monasteries, carefully plans each journey to stay within a tight budget, and they prefer traveling with family during festivals but treasure solitary walks in the fields for inspiration.
culinary_persona: A food enthusiast, Kenshing Paul relishes Assamese staple maasor tenga, regional pitha and bamboo‑shoot curries seasoned with mustard oil, often savors a cup of chai with jaggery after sketching, and while they can whip up simple dal‑roti at home, they prefer exploring street stalls for fresh fish fry and mango‑based desserts.
persona: Kenshing Paul is a meticulous 18‑year‑old salt‑pond technician who blends artistic imagination with a lingering worry about the future, often juggling sketchbooks, cricket bat, and a nervous habit of checking equipment twice.
cultural_background: Kenshing Paul belongs to the Bodo community, an indigenous tribal group of Assam, and lives in the rural village of Chirang. As a Christian, his family’s faith blends with Bodo customs, celebrating both church festivals and traditional Bodo rites. Growing up in a salt‑rich area, he is familiar with the region’s agricultural and salt reclamation practices, while also being exposed to Assamese and Hindi cultural elements through schooling and community interactions.
linguistic_background: Kenshing grew up speaking Bodo at home and is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing Bodo using the Devanagari script. His schooling in a government school taught him Hindi, where he achieved functional literacy, enabling him to read newspapers, official documents, and simple literature. He also learned Assamese as a third language, primarily for oral communication with neighbors and market interactions; his reading and writing skills in Assamese are rudimentary. Additionally, he has basic exposure to English through church services and occasional media.
skills_and_expertise: Kenshing is proficient in salt reclamation processes, including brine management, evaporation, and crystallization techniques. He operates and maintains simple mechanical equipment such as pumps, filters, and drying racks used in the salt ponds. His middle‑school education equips him with basic arithmetic and record‑keeping abilities for tracking production volumes and labor hours. He practices safety protocols, conducts routine equipment checks, and provides basic first‑aid assistance on site. Additionally, his imaginative side fosters a talent for sketching and visualising process improvements, and his organized nature makes him reliable in planning daily work schedules and coordinating with fellow workers.

hobbies_and_interests: Kenshing enjoys sketching rural landscapes and salt pond scenes, reading Hindi novels and Christian literature, listening to traditional Bodo folk songs, playing cricket with friends, and experimenting with simple DIY projects at home. He also likes taking solitary walks in the fields, gardening, and learning new art techniques through online videos whenever internet access permits.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Sketching rural landscapes", "Reading Hindi novels and Christian literature", "Listening to Bodo folk music", "Playing cricket", "DIY home projects", "Solitary walks in fields", "Gardening", "Learning new art techniques online"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Kenshing aspires to become a senior technician or supervisor in the salt reclamation sector, mastering advanced evaporation and crystallisation methods. He plans to enroll in a vocational training program to acquire certifications in mechanical maintenance and environmental safety. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

बेरामीया 18 बोसोरनि हौवासा, भंगाबारी गामिनि चिरांग जिलायाव थानाय साल्ट रिक्लेमेसन मावग्रा। बिथाङा 3 हप्तायै खोखोननाय, बेलासेनि खम लोमजानाय आरो दानसेयाव 5 केजि ओजोन खमनायनि जेंनाजों फैदों। बिथाङा हरनि लोमजानाय आरो उखैनाय खमनायनि बाथ्रा बुंदों। आनजाद खालामनायाव, बिथाङा सांग्रां, साननाय-हनाय थि आरो लोमजानाय गैया। हाबनाय-ओंखारनाय आनजादाव आगदा गोजौ बाहागोआव हाबनाय-ओंखारनाय सोदोब खमनाय आरो माब्लाबा माब्लाबा रन्क' (rhonchi) सोदोब मोननाय जादों। बिथांनि भिटेलस (vitals) फोरा थि दं। बिथांनि देहायाव पल्मोनारि टिबि (pulmonary tuberculosis) नि निदान खालामनाय जादों। आनजाद खालामनायाव स्पुटाम (sputum) नि GeneXpert MTB/RIF आरो चेस्ट X-ray खालामनो थिननाय जादों। बिथांखौ DOTS नि सिङाव मानारि एन्टि-टिबि (anti-tubercular) थेरापि जागायनाय जादों। [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Basumatary]] आ बिथांखौ थोंजोङै नायबिजिरनानै फाहामथाय होगोन। बिथांनि [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-AS-2023-0045]] आरो [[VOTER_ID|BR-1234567]] नि बिवरखौ आनजाद खालामनाय जादों। बिथांनि [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] खौ बिथांनि सावस्रि रेकर्डजों फोनांजाबनाय जादों। कन्ट्याक्ट नम्बर [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] खौ थि खालामनाय जादों। उननि आनजादखौ मोनसे हप्तानि उनाव खालामनाय जागोन।
generated_text_en:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Bhangbari]] Village [[VILLAGE|Bhangbari]] District [[DISTRICT|Chirang]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ranjit Boro]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]] | Cough for 3 weeks, weight loss, evening fever

Patient is an 18-year-old male salt reclamation worker from Bhangbari village, Chirang district. He presents with a productive cough for three weeks, associated with evening low-grade fever and a 5 kg weight loss over the past month. He reports night sweats and decreased appetite. On examination, he is conscious, oriented, and afebrile. Respiratory examination reveals reduced breath sounds in the right upper zone with occasional rhonchi. His vitals are stable. A provisional diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis is made. Investigations ordered include sputum for GeneXpert MTB/RIF and a chest X-ray. He has been started on the standard anti-tubercular therapy regimen under DOTS. The [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Basumatary]] will be providing directly observed treatment. Patient's [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-AS-2023-0045]] and [[VOTER_ID|BR-1234567]] details are verified. His [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] is linked to his health record. Contact number [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] is confirmed. Follow-up scheduled in one week.
judge_reasoning: Bodo Devanagari prose correct; medical Latin terms allowed; all TYPEs in allow-list; male 18 TB case fits persona/domain; no script shifts or length issues.

professional_persona: An aspiring senior salt reclamation technician, Kenshing Paul combines hands‑on expertise in brine management and pump maintenance with a vivid imagination for low‑cost solar‑assisted drying, and they meticulously plan each shift with detailed checklists.
linguistic_persona: Linguistically adept, Kenshing Paul speaks their mother tongue fluently and writes it in Devanagari, reads and writes Hindi at a functional level, converses comfortably in Assamese for marketplace interactions, and picks up basic English phrases from church sermons, allowing them to switch languages as the situation demands.
sports_persona: A keen cricket enthusiast, Kenshing Paul plays weekly street matches with friends, avidly follows the Assam cricket team and the Delhi Capitals in the IPL, and they keep fit by jogging through the salt fields during early mornings.
arts_persona: A creative soul, Kenshing Paul spends afternoons sketching the salt ponds and surrounding fields, drawing inspiration from regional folk singer Lunjor Mingi’s melodies and Bollywood vocalist Arijit Singh’s emotive ballads, while sharpening techniques through tutorials on the YouTube channel ‘Art of Sketching’.
travel_persona: A modest traveler, Kenshing Paul enjoys weekend trips to nearby attractions like Kaziranga National Park and Shillong, dreams of a pilgrimage to Sikkim's Buddhist monasteries, carefully plans each journey to stay within a tight budget, and they prefer traveling with family during festivals but treasure solitary walks in the fields for inspiration.
culinary_persona: A food enthusiast, Kenshing Paul relishes Assamese staple maasor tenga, regional pitha and bamboo‑shoot curries seasoned with mustard oil, often savors a cup of chai with jaggery after sketching, and while they can whip up simple dal‑roti at home, they prefer exploring street stalls for fresh fish fry and mango‑based desserts.
persona: Kenshing Paul is a meticulous 18‑year‑old salt‑pond technician who blends artistic imagination with a lingering worry about the future, often juggling sketchbooks, cricket bat, and a nervous habit of checking equipment twice.
cultural_background: Kenshing Paul belongs to the Bodo community, an indigenous tribal group of Assam, and lives in the rural village of Chirang. As a Christian, his family’s faith blends with Bodo customs, celebrating both church festivals and traditional Bodo rites. Growing up in a salt‑rich area, he is familiar with the region’s agricultural and salt reclamation practices, while also being exposed to Assamese and Hindi cultural elements through schooling and community interactions.
linguistic_background: Kenshing grew up speaking Bodo at home and is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing Bodo using the Devanagari script. His schooling in a government school taught him Hindi, where he achieved functional literacy, enabling him to read newspapers, official documents, and simple literature. He also learned Assamese as a third language, primarily for oral communication with neighbors and market interactions; his reading and writing skills in Assamese are rudimentary. Additionally, he has basic exposure to English through church services and occasional media.
skills_and_expertise: Kenshing is proficient in salt reclamation processes, including brine management, evaporation, and crystallization techniques. He operates and maintains simple mechanical equipment such as pumps, filters, and drying racks used in the salt ponds. His middle‑school education equips him with basic arithmetic and record‑keeping abilities for tracking production volumes and labor hours. He practices safety protocols, conducts routine equipment checks, and provides basic first‑aid assistance on site. Additionally, his imaginative side fosters a talent for sketching and visualising process improvements, and his organized nature makes him reliable in planning daily work schedules and coordinating with fellow workers.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Salt reclamation techniques", "Operation and maintenance of pumps and filters", "Basic arithmetic and record-keeping", "Safety protocol compliance", "Basic first aid", "Process sketching and visualisation", "Work schedule planning", "Team coordination"]
hobbies_and_interests: Kenshing enjoys sketching rural landscapes and salt pond scenes, reading Hindi novels and Christian literature, listening to traditional Bodo folk songs, playing cricket with friends, and experimenting with simple DIY projects at home. He also likes taking solitary walks in the fields, gardening, and learning new art techniques through online videos whenever internet access permits.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Sketching rural landscapes", "Reading Hindi novels and Christian literature", "Listening to Bodo folk music", "Playing cricket", "DIY home projects", "Solitary walks in fields", "Gardening", "Learning new art techniques online"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Kenshing aspires to become a senior technician or supervisor in the salt reclamation sector, mastering advanced evaporation and crystallisation methods. He plans to enroll in a vocational training program to acquire certifications in mechanical maintenance and environmental safety. In the long term, he wishes to introduce low‑cost, solar‑assisted salt drying techniques that improve productivity for his village’s cooperative. He also aims to secure a stable income that supports his young family, while continuing to nurture his creative side by documenting process innovations through sketches and simple reports.
persona_clinical_summary: A 18‑year‑old male salt reclamation worker from rural Chirang, Assam, currently married, with a middle school education. He speaks Bodo at home and Hindi in school, and works as a salt‑pond technician. Clinically, he presents with a nervous habit of equipment checking and a worry about the future. He is a keen cricket player and an aspiring senior technician with interest in solar‑assisted drying methods.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bodo (code=brx, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Chirang.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Bodo.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Salt Reclamation Worker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Chirang
- first_language: Bodo
- clinical_persona_summary: A 18‑year‑old male salt reclamation worker from rural Chirang, Assam, currently married, with a middle school education. He speaks Bodo at home and Hindi in school, and works as a salt‑pond technician. Clinically, he presents with a nervous habit of equipment checking and a worry about the future. He is a keen cricket player and an aspiring senior technician with interest in solar‑assisted drying methods.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

She also has proficiency in Hindi and basic Punjabi.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]

  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Reasi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Dogri.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 20
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Reasi
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 20‑year‑old female from Reasi, Jammu & Kashmir, with a Dogri‑speaking background, holds a secondary education and is unmarried. She works as a homemaker and lives in an urban setting. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, embroidery, and modest savings, with occasional engagement in Bollywood entertainment. She also has proficiency in Hindi and basic Punjabi.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
--- सत्र ---
मरीज [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanti Devi]] [[AGE|20]] [[GENDER|Female]]
अपॉइंटमेंट [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] पोर्टल [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
क्लाइंट IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] डिवाइस IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] फोन [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419123456]]
ईमेल [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|shanti.devi.reasi@example.com]] अस्पताल [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]]
--- चैट ---
मरीज: नमस्ते डॉक्टर साहब, मैं [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanti Devi]] रीयसी से बोल रही हूं। पिछले तीन दिनों से मुझे लगातार सिरदर्द हो रहा है। यह एक हल्का दर्द है, ज्यादातर मेरे सिर के दाहिने हिस्से में, और जब मैं झुकती हूं तो यह और बढ़ जाता है। मुझे कभी-कभी थोड़ा जी मिचलाने जैसा भी महसूस होता है। मैंने मेडिकल स्टोर से कुछ पैरासिटामोल ली थी, लेकिन उससे ज्यादा आराम नहीं मिल रहा है।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: नमस्ते शांति देवी। संपर्क करने के लिए धन्यवाद। मैं समझता हूं कि आपको तीन दिनों से सिरदर्द है, जी मिचलाने के साथ, और पैरासिटामोल से भी आराम नहीं मिल रहा है। क्या आप मुझे बता सकती हैं कि आपको बुखार है, या आपकी दृष्टि में कोई बदलाव आया है? इसके अलावा, क्या आपको हाल ही में सिर में कोई चोट लगी है?
मरीज: नहीं डॉक्टर, बुखार नहीं है। मेरी दृष्टि सामान्य है। मैंने अपने सिर को चोट नहीं पहुंचाई है। दर्द बस बना हुआ है, एक लगातार दबाव जैसा। जब मैं बहुत जल्दी खड़ी होती हूं तो मुझे थोड़ा चक्कर भी आता है।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: मैं समझ गया। खड़े होने पर चक्कर आना एक ऐसी बात है जिसे हमें नोट करना चाहिए। आपके लक्षणों के आधार पर, यह तनाव-प्रकार का सिरदर्द या माइग्रेन का एक प्रकार हो सकता है। चूंकि आपको जी मिचलाना और चक्कर आना है, इसलिए मैं सावधान रहना चाहता हूं। मैं आपको त्वरित जांच के लिए [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]] आने की सलाह दे रहा हूं। हम एक बुनियादी न्यूरोलॉजिकल जांच कर सकते हैं और आपका रक्तचाप जांच सकते हैं। कृपया कल सुबह आएं यदि संभव हो। आपका [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] पुष्ट हो गया है।
मरीज: ठीक है डॉक्टर। मैं कल आने की कोशिश करूंगी। इस बीच मुझे क्या करना चाहिए?
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: अभी के लिए, कृपया झुकने से बचें और शांत, अंधेरे कमरे में आराम करने की कोशिश करें। आप पैरासिटामोल लेना जारी रख सकती हैं, लेकिन दैनिक सीमा से अधिक न लें। पर्याप्त पानी पिएं। यदि सिरदर्द गंभीर हो जाए या आपको उल्टी होने लगे, तो आपको तुरंत अस्पताल आना चाहिए। हम कल आपसे मिलेंगे। अपना ख्याल रखें।
generated_text_en:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanti Devi]] [[AGE|20]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419123456]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|shanti.devi.reasi@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor saheb, I am [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanti Devi]] from Reasi. I have been having a persistent headache for the past three days. It's a dull ache, mostly on the right side of my head, and it gets worse when I bend down. I also feel a bit nauseous sometimes. I took some paracetamol from the medical shop, but it's not helping much.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: Hello Shanti Devi. Thank you for reaching out. I understand you are having a headache for three days, with nausea, and it's not responding to paracetamol. Can you tell me if you have any fever, or if your vision has changed? Also, have you had any recent head injury?
Patient: No fever, doctor. My vision is normal. I haven't bumped my head or anything. The pain is just there, a constant pressure. I also feel a little dizzy when I stand up too quickly.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: I see. The dizziness on standing is something we should note. Based on your symptoms, it could be a tension-type headache or a migraine variant. Since you have nausea and dizziness, I want to be cautious. I am advising you to come to the [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]] for a quick check-up. We can do a basic neurological examination and check your blood pressure. Please come tomorrow morning if possible. Your [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] is confirmed.
Patient: Okay doctor. I will try to come tomorrow. What should I do in the meantime?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: For now, please avoid bending down and try to rest in a quiet, dark room. You can continue taking paracetamol, but do not exceed the daily limit. Drink plenty of water. If the headache becomes severe or you start vomiting, you must come to the hospital immediately. We will see you tomorrow. Take care.
judge_reasoning: Prose in standard Hindi; expected Dogri language in Devanagari.

arts_persona: Ritu Devi immerses herself in Dogri folk music by artists like Rattan Kaur, reads Punjabi poetry by Amrita Pritam, and enjoys Bollywood films starring Alia Bhatt, often weaving these cultural threads into her embroidered creations.

cultural_background: Ritu hails from the Dogri‑speaking community of Reasi in Jammu & Kashmir. Her cultural milieu blends the traditional folk customs of the Dogra region—such as local festivals, folk music, and handcrafted embroidery—with the broader pan‑Indian Hindu rituals observed in urban settings.

Additionally, she understands Punjabi and can hold basic conversations, though her literacy in Punjabi is limited to reading simple texts.
skills_and_expertise: She is adept at managing household chores, preparing traditional Dogri dishes, budgeting family expenses, basic embroidery, and has rudimentary computer skills for online banking and communication.

hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys reading Punjabi poetry, listening to Dogri folk songs, practicing yoga in the mornings, gardening, and creating embroidered wall hangings for her home. Occasionally, she watches Bollywood movies with close friends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Punjabi poetry', 'Listening to Dogri folk music', 'Morning yoga', 'Gardening', 'Embroidery wall hangings', 'Bollywood movies with friends']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Ritu aspires to complete her higher secondary education and eventually enroll in a diploma course for early childhood education, aiming to become a community school teacher. She also envisions turning her embroidery hobby into a small cottage industry that supplies handcrafted items to local markets.
persona_clinical_summary: Ritu Devi is a 20‑year‑old female who has never married, resides in an urban area of Jammu & Kashmir, and has completed matriculation. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation and speaks Dogri as her first language, with proficiency in Hindi. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, embroidery, and modest savings, with occasional engagement in Bollywood entertainment.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Reasi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Dogri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 20
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Reasi
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: Ritu Devi is a 20‑year‑old female who has never married, resides in an urban area of Jammu & Kashmir, and has completed matriculation. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation and speaks Dogri as her first language, with proficiency in Hindi. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, embroidery, and modest savings, with occasional engagement in Bollywood entertainment.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ritu Devi]] [[AGE|20]] [[GENDER|Female]] [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]] [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]] [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0915]] [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]] [[BED_NUMBER|05]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0915-004]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-99876]]
ऑपरेशन नोट
प्रक्रिया / निष्कर्ष: मरीज़ 10 हफ़्तों की गर्भधारण के दौरान पूर्ण गर्भपात के साथ आई, जिसमें भारी योनि रक्तस्राव और ऐंठन थी। जांच पर, वह हेमोडायनामिक रूप से अस्थिर थी, जिसकी नाड़ी 110 bpm और रक्तचाप 90/60 mmHg था। सामान्य एनेस्थीसिया के तहत गर्भाशय का आपातकालीन सक्शन इवैक्यूएशन किया गया। प्रक्रिया जटिलता रहित थी, जिसमें अनुमानित रक्त हानि 400 ml थी। प्रक्रिया के बाद, मरीज़ को स्थिर किया गया और रिकवरी वार्ड में स्थानांतरित कर दिया गया।
generated_text_en:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ritu Devi]] [[AGE|20]] [[GENDER|Female]] [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]] [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]] [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0915]] [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]] [[BED_NUMBER|05]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0915-004]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-99876]]
Operative Note
Procedure / Findings: Patient presented with a complete miscarriage at 10 weeks gestation, with heavy vaginal bleeding and cramping. On examination, she was hemodynamically unstable with a pulse of 110 bpm and blood pressure of 90/60 mmHg. An emergency suction evacuation of the uterus was performed under general anesthesia. The procedure was uncomplicated, with estimated blood loss of 400 ml. Post-procedure, the patient was stabilized and transferred to the recovery ward.
judge_reasoning: Clinical prose is standard Hindi; Dogri (Devanagari) required. All tags allowed, persona/domain fit, length ok.

professional_persona: Ritu Devi is an aspiring early‑childhood teacher who blends practical budgeting skills with a gentle, structured classroom style, using their love for routine and flexibility to create a calm learning environment, while their knack for embroidery adds a creative touch to teaching aids.
linguistic_persona: Ritu Devi is fluent in Dogri and Hindi with solid reading and writing abilities, can hold simple conversations in Punjabi and uses basic online tools for communication, reflecting a practical approach to language that balances comfort with occasional need for quick translation.
sports_persona: Ritu Devi follows the Jammu & Kashmir cricket team and JK United FC, plays badminton at the local community center, and maintains fitness through daily yoga, preferring gentle exercise over high‑intensity sports.
arts_persona: Ritu Devi immerses herself in Dogri folk music by artists like Rattan Kaur, reads Punjabi poetry by Amrita Pritam, and enjoys Bollywood films starring Alia Bhatt, often weaving these cultural threads into her embroidered creations.
travel_persona: Ritu Devi loves weekend getaways to nearby hill stations such as Shimla and Manali, dreams of trekking through Leh‑Ladakh, and plans trips with friends using budget-friendly trains and shared rides, balancing adventure with modest expenses.
culinary_persona: Ritu Devi relishes traditional Dogri dishes like madra and kalari, loves a steaming plate of rajma with rice, enjoys fresh paneer tikka and sweet lassi, and while she occasionally helps in the kitchen, she prefers savoring meals at home over frequent dining out.
persona: Ritu Devi is a 20‑year‑old who blends a love for structured routines with a laid‑back approach to obligations, filling her days with yoga, embroidery, modest savings, and the occasional binge of Bollywood movies on a disorganized but cozy study desk.
cultural_background: Ritu hails from the Dogri‑speaking community of Reasi in Jammu & Kashmir. Her cultural milieu blends the traditional folk customs of the Dogra region—such as local festivals, folk music, and handcrafted embroidery—with the broader pan‑Indian Hindu rituals observed in urban settings.
linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Dogri, which she reads and writes fluently. She is also proficient in Hindi, both spoken and written, as it is the state language and medium of education. Additionally, she understands Punjabi and can hold basic conversations, though her literacy in Punjabi is limited to reading simple texts.
skills_and_expertise: She is adept at managing household chores, preparing traditional Dogri dishes, budgeting family expenses, basic embroidery, and has rudimentary computer skills for online banking and communication.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Basic cooking (Dogri cuisine)', 'Family budgeting', 'Embroidery', 'Basic computer literacy']
hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys reading Punjabi poetry, listening to Dogri folk songs, practicing yoga in the mornings, gardening, and creating embroidered wall hangings for her home. Occasionally, she watches Bollywood movies with close friends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Punjabi poetry', 'Listening to Dogri folk music', 'Morning yoga', 'Gardening', 'Embroidery wall hangings', 'Bollywood movies with friends']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Ritu aspires to complete her higher secondary education and eventually enroll in a diploma course for early childhood education, aiming to become a community school teacher. She also envisions turning her embroidery hobby into a small cottage industry that supplies handcrafted items to local markets.
persona_clinical_summary: Ritu Devi is a 20‑year‑old female who has never married, living in an urban area of Jammu & Kashmir. She has a secondary education and is currently a homemaker, with a Dogri-speaking background from Reasi. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, embroidery, and modest savings, with a diet rich in traditional Dogri dishes. She is proficient in Hindi and Dogri, and her routine is structured yet flexible.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Reasi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Dogri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 20
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Reasi
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: Ritu Devi is a 20‑year‑old female who has never married, living in an urban area of Jammu & Kashmir. She has a secondary education and is currently a homemaker, with a Dogri-speaking background from Reasi. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, embroidery, and modest savings, with a diet rich in traditional Dogri dishes. She is proficient in Hindi and Dogri, and her routine is structured yet flexible.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):

MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riya Banerjee]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

persona: Vishwanath Hota is a 40-year-old hands-on bricklayer who blends curiosity with a laid-back vibe, improvises with jugaad, loves folk music and festivals, and occasionally procrastinates on paperwork.

He often employs ‘jugaad’ – creative problem‑solving with limited resources – to fix equipment or improvise temporary structures. He also maintains basic records of daily wages and material usage.
