system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

GOOD:

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):

professional_persona: Vishwanath Hota is a seasoned manual labourer and bricklayer with strong masonry and carpentry skills, who blends hands-on expertise with a curious, flexible mindset, often improvising solutions on site using creative jugaad while they gently juggle supervisory aspirations and a relaxed approach to planning.
linguistic_persona: Vishwanath Hota reads and writes English confidently for news and official paperwork, speaks Hindi fluently for everyday communication, understands spoken Bengali and can recognize familiar words in print, while they have limited formal Odia literacy.
sports_persona: Vishwanath Hota enjoys playing informal cricket on weekends with neighbourhood friends, follows the local state cricket team and the Indian Premier League, occasionally joins a community kabaddi club during monsoon, and stays active with morning walks and light jogging to maintain fitness.
arts_persona: Vishwanath Hota regularly tunes into Sambalpuri folk music, especially songs by Jitendra Narayan, enjoys watching classic Odia films by Sabyasachi Mohapatra and popular Bengali cinema by Satyajit Ray, and occasionally attends local Jhumar dance performances during festivals.
travel_persona: Vishwanath Hota prefers short road trips to nearby pilgrimage sites like Puri and Konark, enjoys weekend getaways to Kolkata during Durga Puja, dreams of exploring the hill station of Darjeeling and the coastal charm of Goa, but balances travel with family responsibilities and limited budget.
culinary_persona: Vishwanath Hota enjoys cooking traditional Odia dishes such as dalma, pakhala bhata, and chhena poda, relishes the tangy flavor of mustard seeds and sesame oil, and frequently prepares these meals for family gatherings, though they rely more on ready-made mixes for occasional convenience.
persona: Vishwanath Hota is a 40-year-old hands-on bricklayer who blends curiosity with a laid-back vibe, improvises with jugaad, loves folk music and festivals, and occasionally procrastinates on paperwork.
cultural_background: Vishwanath Hota belongs to the urban working‑class community of Sambalpur, Odisha. His family’s cultural roots blend mainstream Odia traditions with the vibrant Sambalpuri folk culture, known for Jhumar dance, hand‑loom Sambalpuri sarees and the harvest festival Nuakhai. Growing up in a Hindu household, he observes pan‑Indian rituals as well as local worship of the goddess Samaleswari. His daily life is shaped by the practical ethos of a labourer, but his curiosity drives him to engage with diverse ideas through his multilingual abilities in English, Hindi and Bengali.
linguistic_background: Vishwanath is literate and can read and write English with confidence, using it for daily news, official forms and occasional correspondence. His Hindi is functional – he speaks fluently, reads simple texts and can fill out basic documents. Bengali is his third language: he understands spoken Bengali well and can read familiar words, though his writing ability is limited. Growing up in Sambalpur, he also picks up everyday phrases in Odia, but his formal literacy in Odia remains minimal.
skills_and_expertise: Vishwanath’s skill set is shaped by years of manual work and his practical mindset. He is adept at bricklaying, basic masonry, and simple carpentry, which he uses on construction sites across Sambalpur. He can operate common hand tools such as drills, hammers, and cement mixers safely. His physical fitness enables him to handle heavy lifting and prolonged outdoor work. He often employs ‘jugaad’ – creative problem‑solving with limited resources – to fix equipment or improvise temporary structures. As a team player, he coordinates with fellow labourers, ensures smooth workflow, and communicates effectively in English, Hindi and Bengali. He also maintains basic records of daily wages and material usage.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Bricklaying", "Basic Masonry", "Simple Carpentry", "Operation of Hand Tools (Drill, Hammer, Cement Mixer)", "Physical Fitness for Heavy Lifting", "Jugaad Problem Solving", "Team Coordination", "Multilingual Communication (English, Hindi, Bengali)", "Basic Wage and Material Record Keeping", "Safety Awareness on Construction Sites"]
hobbies_and_interests: Vishwanath likes listening to Sambalpuri folk songs and attending local cultural programmes, especially during festivals. He enjoys watching Odia movies and Bengali cinema on satellite TV, and reads English newspapers each morning to stay informed. In his free time, he plays cards with neighbours, helps organise community gatherings, and often tinkers with simple DIY repairs around his house, reflecting his practical curiosity. He also enjoys cooking traditional Odia dishes, which he shares with his family and friends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Sambalpuri folk music", "Watching Odia movies", "Reading English newspapers", "Playing cards with neighbours", "Participating in community festivals", "DIY home repairs", "Cooking traditional Odia dishes", "Following Bengali cinema and music"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: He hopes to transition from day‑to‑day manual labour to a supervisory role, such as foreman, which will provide a steadier income and allow him to support his family more comfortably. He plans to take a short vocational training in modern construction techniques or safety management, leveraging his curiosity and practical nature. In the longer term, he aspires to start a small hardware or construction‑material shop in Sambalpur, creating a modest but stable business that can also provide occasional work for his relatives.
persona_clinical_summary: A 40-year-old male labourer in Sambalpur, Odisha, currently married, with a literate education level. He works as a manual labourer and is fluent in English, with functional Hindi. His lifestyle includes occasional cricket and a preference for traditional Odia cuisine.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

arts_persona: Vishwanath Hota regularly tunes into Sambalpuri folk music, especially songs by Jitendra Narayan, enjoys watching classic Odia films by Sabyasachi Mohapatra and popular Bengali cinema by Satyajit Ray, and occasionally attends local Jhumar dance performances during festivals.
travel_persona: Vishwanath Hota prefers short road trips to nearby pilgrimage sites like Puri and Konark, enjoys weekend getaways to Kolkata during Durga Puja, dreams of exploring the hill station of Darjeeling and the coastal charm of Goa, but balances travel with family responsibilities and limited budget.
culinary_persona: Vishwanath Hota enjoys cooking traditional Odia dishes such as dalma, pakhala bhata, and chhena poda, relishes the tangy flavor of mustard seeds and sesame oil, and frequently prepares these meals for family gatherings, though they rely more on ready-made mixes for occasional convenience.
persona: Vishwanath Hota is a 40-year-old hands-on bricklayer who blends curiosity with a laid-back vibe, improvises with jugaad, loves folk music and festivals, and occasionally procrastinates on paperwork.
cultural_background: Vishwanath Hota belongs to the urban working‑class community of Sambalpur, Odisha. His family’s cultural roots blend mainstream Odia traditions with the vibrant Sambalpuri folk culture, known for Jhumar dance, hand‑loom Sambalpuri sarees and the harvest festival Nuakhai. Growing up in a Hindu household, he observes pan‑Indian rituals as well as local worship of the goddess Samaleswari. His daily life is shaped by the practical ethos of a labourer, but his curiosity drives him to engage with diverse ideas through his multilingual abilities in English, Hindi and Bengali.
linguistic_background: Vishwanath is literate and can read and write English with confidence, using it for daily news, official forms and occasional correspondence. Bengali is his third language: he understands spoken Bengali well and can read familiar words, though his writing ability is limited. skills_and_expertise: Vishwanath’s skill set is shaped by years of manual work and his practical mindset. He is adept at bricklaying, basic masonry, and simple carpentry, which he uses on construction sites across Sambalpur. He can operate common hand tools such as drills, hammers, and cement mixers safely. His physical fitness enables him to handle heavy lifting and prolonged outdoor work. He often employs ‘jugaad’ – creative problem‑solving with limited resources – to fix equipment or improvise temporary structures. As a team player, he coordinates with fellow labourers, ensures smooth workflow, and communicates effectively in English, Hindi and Bengali. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into English (code=en, script=Latin). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Sambalpur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Latin for English.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 40
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Labourer, Other
- education_level: Literate without education level
- zone: Urban
- state: Odisha
- district: Sambalpur
- first_language: English
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old male labourer from Sambalpur, Odisha, currently married, with an urban occupation and literate English proficiency. He is a hands-on bricklayer with strong masonry skills, often using improvisation on site. His lifestyle includes listening to Odia folk music and occasional cricket, with no significant travel or culinary restrictions noted.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

linguistic_persona: Paramar Patel speaks Gujarati fluently for every daily interaction and reads and writes it with ease, while their Hindi is functional enough to understand basic signs and instructions, and they have no formal training in any other language.
sports_persona: Paramar Patel enjoys early morning walks across the fields, follows the Saurashtra cricket team on television, and they occasionally join a village Kabaddi match during festivals.
arts_persona: Paramar Patel cherishes folk tales of Dhumketu, listens to Kirtidan Gadhvi’s traditional songs and bhajans by Harshad Rao while tending his garden, and they occasionally attend local folk music gatherings featuring the dhol and dandiya.
travel_persona: Paramar Patel prefers budget train travel, having visited Somnath for a shrine pilgrimage and Mount Abu for a weekend retreat, and they hope to explore the ghats of Varanasi next year.
culinary_persona: Paramar Patel favors hearty vegetarian staples such as dhokla, undhiyu, thepla, and sweet ghari, enjoys a glass of chaas after their morning walks, and they occasionally prepare simple dal-rice for their family, though they often prefer the comforting flavors of the local dhaba.
persona: Paramar Patel blends steadfast dedication to preserving traditional funeral customs with a quiet curiosity for modest digital tools, walks the countryside at dawn, prefers solitude, and they habitually check their blood pressure while humming folk bhajans.
cultural_background: Paramar Patel belongs to a traditional Gujarati farming community in the rural district of Banas Kantha, Gujarat. His upbringing was steeped in local customs, folk music, and the communal rituals surrounding life and death. As a caretaker of burial sites and cremation grounds, he plays a vital role in preserving Hindu funeral traditions while also adapting to modest modern improvements. His cultural identity blends the pragmatic ethos of rural Gujarat with a deep respect for age‑old rites, and his daily life is guided by the values of service, humility, and a quiet sense of duty to his neighbors.
linguistic_background: Paramar's mother tongue is Gujarati, which he speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication, reading and writing. With only primary schooling, his literacy is strong in Gujarati but basic in Hindi, allowing him to understand common signs and simple instructions. He does not have formal proficiency in any other language.
skills_and_expertise: The core of Paramar's expertise lies in managing burial sites and cremation grounds. He is proficient in coordinating Hindu funeral rites, maintaining the sanctity and cleanliness of the grounds, and handling logistical aspects such as scheduling services and preparing the pyre. His practical skills include basic carpentry for repairs, record‑keeping of burial plots, liaising with families and local priests, and ensuring compliance with municipal regulations. He also possesses conflict‑resolution abilities, mediating disputes over plot allocations, and provides basic first‑aid support when needed.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Funeral rites coordination", "Ground sanitation and maintenance", "Basic carpentry repairs", "Record keeping of burial plots", "Family and priest liaison", "Municipal compliance management", "Conflict resolution", "Basic first aid provision"]
hobbies_and_interests: Paramar enjoys solitary pursuits such as reading Gujarati folk stories and listening to devotional bhajans. He tends his modest garden, walks the countryside in the early mornings, and occasionally plays a round of cards with a few close friends. He also takes interest in traditional Gujarati folk music and occasionally assists in temple festivals, though always in a low‑key, behind‑the‑scenes role.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Gujarati folk stories", "Listening to devotional bhajans", "Gardening", "Morning countryside walks", "Playing cards with close friends", "Enjoying Gujarati folk music", "Assisting in temple festivals (behind the scenes)"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Paramar aspires to modernize the cremation and burial facilities under his care while preserving their religious sanctity. He aims to introduce systematic record‑keeping using simple digital tools, improve ground sanitation with eco‑friendly practices, and train a younger assistant to ensure continuity. Over the next five years, he hopes to be recognized by the district administration as a model caretaker, possibly overseeing multiple sites and advocating for better funding for maintenance.
occupation: Caretaker, Burial Places and Cremation Grounds
persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Paramar Patel, a 50-year-old male from rural Gujarat, works as a caretaker for burial and cremation grounds while being married. He has primary education and speaks Gujarati as his first language, with limited Hindi literacy. His lifestyle includes early morning walks, occasional blood pressure checks, and a preference for solitude.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Gujarati (code=gu, script=Gujarati). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Gujarat, district=Banas Kantha.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gujarati for Gujarati.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 50
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Caretaker, Burial Places and Cremation Grounds
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Gujarat
- district: Banas Kantha
- first_language: Gujarati
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Paramar Patel, a 50-year-old male from rural Gujarat, works as a caretaker for burial and cremation grounds while being married. He has primary education and speaks Gujarati as his first language, with limited Hindi literacy. His lifestyle includes early morning walks, occasional blood pressure checks, and a preference for solitude.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|Mr. Paramar Patel]]: તમારો ઓન્કોલોજી ફોલો-અપ 21-મે ના રોજ [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Banas Kantha]] ખાતે [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Mehta]] સાથે [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] છે. કૃપા કરીને [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]] દ્વારા પુષ્ટિ કરો. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]].
generated_text_en: [[PATIENT_NAME|Mr. Paramar Patel]]: your oncology follow-up is [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] on 21-May at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Banas Kantha]] with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Mehta]]. Please confirm via [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]].
judge_reasoning: Gujarati prose correct; all TYPEs allowed; persona/domain fit; no length or script issues.

professional_persona: Paramar Patel is a seasoned caretaker of burial and cremation grounds, adept at coordinating traditional funeral rites, maintaining meticulous records, and performing basic carpentry, and they balance a practical mindset with an inquisitive willingness to adopt modest digital tools.
linguistic_persona: Paramar Patel speaks Gujarati fluently for every daily interaction and reads and writes it with ease, while their Hindi is functional enough to understand basic signs and instructions, and they have no formal training in any other language.
sports_persona: Paramar Patel enjoys early morning walks across the fields, follows the Saurashtra cricket team on television, and they occasionally join a village Kabaddi match during festivals.
arts_persona: Paramar Patel cherishes folk tales of Dhumketu, listens to Kirtidan Gadhvi’s traditional songs and bhajans by Harshad Rao while tending his garden, and they occasionally attend local folk music gatherings featuring the dhol and dandiya.
travel_persona: Paramar Patel prefers budget train travel, having visited Somnath for a shrine pilgrimage and Mount Abu for a weekend retreat, and they hope to explore the ghats of Varanasi next year.
culinary_persona: Paramar Patel favors hearty vegetarian staples such as dhokla, undhiyu, thepla, and sweet ghari, enjoys a glass of chaas after their morning walks, and they occasionally prepare simple dal-rice for their family, though they often prefer the comforting flavors of the local dhaba.
persona: Paramar Patel blends steadfast dedication to preserving traditional funeral customs with a quiet curiosity for modest digital tools, walks the countryside at dawn, prefers solitude, and they habitually check their blood pressure while humming folk bhajans.
cultural_background: Paramar Patel belongs to a traditional Gujarati farming community in the rural district of Banas Kantha, Gujarat. His upbringing was steeped in local customs, folk music, and the communal rituals surrounding life and death. As a caretaker of burial sites and cremation grounds, he plays a vital role in preserving Hindu funeral traditions while also adapting to modest modern improvements. His cultural identity blends the pragmatic ethos of rural Gujarat with a deep respect for age‑old rites, and his daily life is guided by the values of service, humility, and a quiet sense of duty to his neighbors.
linguistic_background: Paramar's mother tongue is Gujarati, which he speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication, reading and writing. With only primary schooling, his literacy is strong in Gujarati but basic in Hindi, allowing him to understand common signs and simple instructions. He does not have formal proficiency in any other language.
skills_and_expertise: The core of Paramar's expertise lies in managing burial sites and cremation grounds. He is proficient in coordinating Hindu funeral rites, maintaining the sanctity and cleanliness of the grounds, and handling logistical aspects such as scheduling services and preparing the pyre. His practical skills include basic carpentry for repairs, record‑keeping of burial plots, liaising with families and local priests, and ensuring compliance with municipal regulations. He also possesses conflict‑resolution abilities, mediating disputes over plot allocations, and provides basic first‑aid support when needed.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Funeral rites coordination", "Ground sanitation and maintenance", "Basic carpentry repairs", "Record keeping of burial plots", "Family and priest liaison", "Municipal compliance management", "Conflict resolution", "Basic first aid provision"]
hobbies_and_interests: Paramar enjoys solitary pursuits such as reading Gujarati folk stories and listening to devotional bhajans. He tends his modest garden, walks the countryside in the early mornings, and occasionally plays a round of cards with a few close friends. He also takes interest in traditional Gujarati folk music and occasionally assists in temple festivals, though always in a low‑key, behind‑the‑scenes role.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Gujarati folk stories", "Listening to devotional bhajans", "Gardening", "Morning countryside walks", "Playing cards with close friends", "Enjoying Gujarati folk music", "Assisting in temple festivals (behind the scenes)"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Paramar aspires to modernize the cremation and burial facilities under his care while preserving their religious sanctity. He aims to introduce systematic record‑keeping using simple digital tools, improve ground sanitation with eco‑friendly practices, and train a younger assistant to ensure continuity. Over the next five years, he hopes to be recognized by the district administration as a model caretaker, possibly overseeing multiple sites and advocating for better funding for maintenance.
occupation: Caretaker, Burial Places and Cremation Grounds
persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Paramar Patel, a 50-year-old male caretaker in rural Banas Kantha, Gujarat, is married with primary education and speaks Gujarati. He works maintaining burial and cremation grounds, a role involving traditional rituals and basic record-keeping. His lifestyle includes early morning walks, occasional blood pressure checks, and a preference for solitude, with limited literacy in Hindi.

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

દર્દી ત્રણ મહિનાથી સતત ઓછી મૂડ, એનહેડોનિયા (anhedonia), અને ઊંઘમાં ખલેલ સાથે રજૂ થયા. તે પોતાની જાતને નકામી માને છે અને કામ પર ધ્યાન કેન્દ્રિત કરવામાં મુશ્કેલી અનુભવે છે. ભૂતકાળના તબીબી ઇતિહાસમાં હાયપરટેન્શન (hypertension) નોંધપાત્ર છે, જે દવાઓ પર સારી રીતે નિયંત્રિત છે. પારિવારિક ઇતિહાસ બિન-યોગદાનકારી છે. સામાજિક ઇતિહાસ દર્શાવે છે કે તે સ્થાનિક સ્મશાનમાં સંભાળ રાખનાર છે અને તેની પત્ની સાથે રહે છે. તપાસમાં સાયકોમોટર રિટાર્ડેશન (psychomotor retardation), ફ્લેટ અફેક્ટ (flat affect), અને કોઈ સ્પષ્ટ આત્મહત્યાના વિચારો નથી. નિદાન: મેજર ડિપ્રેસિવ ડિસઓર્ડર, રિકરન્ટ, મધ્યમ. યોજના: સર્ટાલાઇન (Sertraline) 50 mg દૈનિક શરૂ કરો, કાઉન્સેલિંગ રેફરલ પ્રદાન કરો, અને બે અઠવાડિયામાં ફોલો-અપ શેડ્યૂલ કરો.

Patient presented with chief complaint of persistent low mood, anhedonia, and sleep disturbances for the past three months. He reports feelings of worthlessness and difficulty concentrating at work. Past medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled on medication. Family history is non-contributory. Social history reveals he is a caretaker at a local cremation ground and lives with his spouse. Examination reveals psychomotor retardation, flat affect, and no overt suicidal ideation. Diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate. Plan: Initiate Sertraline 50 mg daily, provide counseling referral, and schedule follow-up in two weeks.

professional_persona: Paramar Patel is a seasoned caretaker of burial and cremation grounds, adept at coordinating traditional funeral rites, maintaining meticulous records, and performing basic carpentry, and they balance a practical mindset with an inquisitive willingness to adopt modest digital tools.
linguistic_persona: Paramar Patel speaks Gujarati fluently for every daily interaction and reads and writes it with ease, while their Hindi is functional enough to understand basic signs and instructions, and they have no formal training in any other language.
sports_persona: Paramar Patel enjoys early morning walks across the fields, follows the Saurashtra cricket team on television, and they occasionally join a village Kabaddi match during festivals.
arts_persona: Paramar Patel cherishes folk tales of Dhumketu, listens to Kirtidan Gadhvi’s traditional songs and bhajans by Harshad Rao while tending his garden, and they occasionally attend local folk music gatherings featuring the dhol and dandiya.
travel_persona: Paramar Patel prefers budget train travel, having visited Somnath for a shrine pilgrimage and Mount Abu for a weekend retreat, and they hope to explore the ghats of Varanasi next year.
culinary_persona: Paramar Patel favors hearty vegetarian staples such as dhokla, undhiyu, thepla, and sweet ghari, enjoys a glass of chaas after their morning walks, and they occasionally prepare simple dal-rice for their family, though they often prefer the comforting flavors of the local dhaba.
persona: Paramar Patel blends steadfast dedication to preserving traditional funeral customs with a quiet curiosity for modest digital tools, walks the countryside at dawn, prefers solitude, and they habitually check their blood pressure while humming folk bhajans.
cultural_background: Paramar Patel belongs to a traditional Gujarati farming community in the rural district of Banas Kantha, Gujarat. His upbringing was steeped in local customs, folk music, and the communal rituals surrounding life and death. As a caretaker of burial sites and cremation grounds, he plays a vital role in preserving Hindu funeral traditions while also adapting to modest modern improvements. His cultural identity blends the pragmatic ethos of rural Gujarat with a deep respect for age‑old rites, and his daily life is guided by the values of service, humility, and a quiet sense of duty to his neighbors.
linguistic_background: Paramar's mother tongue is Gujarati, which he speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication, reading and writing. With only primary schooling, his literacy is strong in Gujarati but basic in Hindi, allowing him to understand common signs and simple instructions. He does not have formal proficiency in any other language.
skills_and_expertise: The core of Paramar's expertise lies in managing burial sites and cremation grounds. He is proficient in coordinating Hindu funeral rites, maintaining the sanctity and cleanliness of the grounds, and handling logistical aspects such as scheduling services and preparing the pyre. His practical skills include basic carpentry for repairs, record‑keeping of burial plots, liaising with families and local priests, and ensuring compliance with municipal regulations. He also possesses conflict‑resolution abilities, mediating disputes over plot allocations, and provides basic first‑aid support when needed.

hobbies_and_interests: Paramar enjoys solitary pursuits such as reading Gujarati folk stories and listening to devotional bhajans. He tends his modest garden, walks the countryside in the early mornings, and occasionally plays a round of cards with a few close friends. He also takes interest in traditional Gujarati folk music and occasionally assists in temple festivals, though always in a low‑key, behind‑the‑scenes role.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Gujarati folk stories", "Listening to devotional bhajans", "Gardening", "Morning countryside walks", "Playing cards with close friends", "Enjoying Gujarati folk music", "Assisting in temple festivals (behind the scenes)"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Paramar aspires to modernize the cremation and burial facilities under his care while preserving their religious sanctity. He aims to introduce systematic record‑keeping using simple digital tools, improve ground sanitation with eco‑friendly practices, and train a younger assistant to ensure continuity. Over the next five years, he hopes to be recognized by the district administration as a model caretaker, possibly overseeing multiple sites and advocating for better funding for maintenance.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain General Medicine.

CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

His lifestyle includes early morning walks, occasional blood pressure checks, and a preference for solitude.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:

Address district [[DISTRICT|...]] PIN [[PIN_CODE|110001]]

professional_persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a seasoned hotel steward known for their vivid storytelling, adept housekeeping, and inventive 'jugaad' solutions that make every guest feel at home.
linguistic_persona: Deepmala Tiwari speaks fluent Hindi enriched with the Awadhi dialect, understands basic spoken English through gestures and context, and excels at oral storytelling and memorising routines, despite being unable to read or write; they rely on vivid verbal expression to connect with guests.
sports_persona: Deepmala Tiwari follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Lucknow Super Giants, participates in weekend walking clubs along the Gomti River, and they practice yoga each morning to ease joint discomfort, reflecting a balanced approach to fitness.
arts_persona: Deepmala Tiwari revels in Awadhi folk music, devotional bhajans, and Ramlila performances, often humming tunes of Ustad Rashid Khan and Shreya Ghoshal while they stitch intricate Chikankari designs inspired by traditional motifs, showcasing a deep love for cultural arts.
travel_persona: Deepmala Tiwari enjoys weekend trips to Lucknow's Bara Imambara and the historic Taj Mahal in Agra, plans occasional pilgrimages to Varanasi, and they dream of a cultural tour of Jaipur's heritage hotels, balancing careful itinerary planning with spontaneous day-trips.
culinary_persona: Deepmala Tiwari delights in preparing Mishri ka halwa, richly spiced kebabs, and aromatic biryani, blending saffron and cardamom, and they often cook these dishes for guests, though they prefer to follow cherished family recipes rather than experiment.
persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a talkative, imaginative 42-year-old hospitality professional who blends traditional storytelling with practical 'jugaad', dreams of a heritage guesthouse, and spices life with folk music, embroidery, and generous hospitality; they radiate warmth wherever they go.
cultural_background: Deepmala hails from the Awadhi cultural milieu of the rural outskirts of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh. She grew up in a joint family where traditional North Indian customs, such as celebrating Diwali, Holi, and Navratri, were central. Her community places a high value on hospitality and sharing food, which later influenced her choice of a career in the hotel sector. Even without formal schooling, she inherited a rich oral tradition of folk songs, proverbs and the craft of Chikankari embroidery passed down from her mother and neighbours. Her cultural identity is a blend of rustic village life and the cosmopolitan exposure she gets daily from interacting with guests from diverse backgrounds.
linguistic_background: Deepmala's mother‑tongue is Hindi, and she commonly uses the Awadhi dialect in daily conversation. Being illiterate, she cannot read or write Hindi or English fluently, but she can recognise familiar symbols, hotel signage, and basic numerals. She understands simple spoken English from guests and manages communication through gestures, body language, and contextual cues. Her linguistic strengths lie in oral storytelling, memorising routines, and using vivid, imaginative vocabulary when interacting with guests, which helps her create a warm and engaging atmosphere.
skills_and_expertise: Deepmala excels in hospitality operations, especially in guest relations and housekeeping. She is adept at cleaning rooms, managing linens, serving food and beverages, and ensuring that guests feel welcomed. Her strong oral communication allows her to convey instructions and assist guests without relying on written material. She employs practical "jugaad" solutions to solve on‑the‑spot problems, and she is known for her storytelling ability, which adds a personal touch to guest interactions. Additionally, she possesses good knowledge of local attractions in Lucknow, basic first aid, and the ability to coordinate short‑term tasks among her team members.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Guest relations", "Housekeeping", "Room cleaning", "Linen management", "Food and beverage service", "Storytelling", "Problem solving (jugaad)", "Team coordination", "Knowledge of local attractions", "Basic first aid"]
hobbies_and_interests: Deepmala enjoys listening to traditional Awadhi folk music and devotional bhajans, attending Ramlila performances during festivals, and exploring Lucknow’s historic sites such as the Bara Imambara and Chota Imambara. She loves cooking and experimenting with regional recipes, especially Mishri ka halwa and kebabs. In her spare time she practices Chikankari embroidery, shares stories with neighbours, sings bhajans, and organises small gatherings where she can interact with friends and guests, reflecting her sociable nature.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to folk music", "Attending Ramlila performances", "Cooking regional dishes", "Chikankari embroidery", "Storytelling with neighbours", "Singing devotional bhajans", "Exploring historic sites in Lucknow", "Socialising with guests and friends"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Deepmala aspires to progress from her current steward role to a supervisory position within the hotel, eventually managing a small team and overseeing guest services. She envisions opening a boutique heritage guesthouse in Lucknow that showcases Awadhi culture, Chikankari crafts and traditional cuisine. In parallel, she wishes to enroll in adult literacy programmes to acquire basic reading and writing skills in Hindi, which will help her maintain records and communicate more effectively with management.
persona_clinical_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she has limited literacy but can recognize symbols and basic signage, and she practices yoga for joint discomfort.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Lucknow.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Steward, Hotel
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Lucknow
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she has limited literacy but can recognize symbols and basic signage, and she practices yoga for joint discomfort.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
