विषय: [[PATIENT_NAME|सविता देवी]], [[AGE|42]] / [[GENDER|Female]], जिला [[DISTRICT|लखनऊ]]

कारण: बाएं निचले पैर पर एक निरंतर, न भरने वाले अल्सर का मूल्यांकन, जिसमें घातक परिवर्तन (malignant transformation) का संदेह है, जिसके लिए ऑन्कोलॉजिकल मूल्यांकन और प्रबंधन की आवश्यकता है।

रोगी [[PATIENT_NAME|सविता देवी]] लखनऊ, उत्तर प्रदेश के ग्रामीण क्षेत्र की 42 वर्षीय महिला हैं, जो पिछले आठ महीनों से अपने बाएं पैर पर एक पुराने अल्सर के साथ आई हैं। यह घाव, जिसे शुरू में एक साधारण घाव समझा गया था, सामयिक उपचारों के बावजूद लगातार बिगड़ता गया है। रोगी रुक-रुक कर दर्द और कभी-कभी रक्तस्राव की रिपोर्ट करती हैं। उन्हें टाइप 2 मधुमेह और उच्च रक्तचाप का इतिहास है। उनका [[MRN|MRN-2024-LKO-0891]] फाइल में है। वह अपने पति, [[RELATIVE_NAME|रमेश कुमार]] के साथ आई हैं, जो उनकी प्राथमिक देखभाल करने वाले हैं।

परीक्षण पर, बाएं निचले पैर के मध्य पहलू पर 4x3 सेमी का अल्सर देखा गया, जिसमें उभरे हुए, मुड़े हुए किनारे और एक नेक्रोटिक आधार है। क्षेत्रीय लिम्फैडेनोपैथी (regional lymphadenopathy) है। नैदानिक संदेह स्क्वैमस सेल कार्सिनोमा (squamous cell carcinoma) को देखते हुए, बायोप्सी की सिफारिश की जाती है। रोगी के [[PHONE_NUMBER|9454321098]] और [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP28 20100012345]] संपर्क के लिए सत्यापित किए गए हैं। उनकी [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P8765432]] भविष्य की यात्रा संबंधी प्रक्रियाओं के लिए उपलब्ध है।

कृपया एक व्यापक ऑन्कोलॉजिकल वर्कअप की व्यवस्था करें, जिसमें इमेजिंग और हिस्टोपैथोलॉजिकल पुष्टि शामिल हो। हम स्टेजिंग और निश्चित उपचार योजना पर आपके विशेषज्ञ की राय का अनुरोध करते हैं। रोगी का [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] राष्ट्रीय स्वास्थ्य प्रणाली में पंजीकृत है। किसी भी वित्तीय सहायता संबंधी प्रश्नों के लिए उनकी [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|30912345678901]] का विवरण फाइल में है। हम इस जटिल मामले पर आपके त्वरित ध्यान की प्रतीक्षा कर रहे हैं।
generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-LKO-001]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|डॉ. राम मनोहर लोहिया अस्पताल]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|डॉ. प्रिया नायर]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|सविता देवी]], [[AGE|42]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|लखनऊ]]
Reason: Evaluation of a persistent, non-healing ulcer on the left lower leg with suspected malignant transformation, requiring oncological assessment and management.

Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|सविता देवी]] is a 42-year-old female from a rural area of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, who presents with a chronic ulcer on her left leg for the past eight months. The lesion, initially thought to be a simple wound, has progressively worsened despite topical treatments. The patient reports intermittent pain and occasional bleeding. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Her [[MRN|MRN-2024-LKO-0891]] is on file. She is accompanied by her husband, [[RELATIVE_NAME|रमेश कुमार]], who is her primary caregiver.

On examination, a 4x3 cm ulcer is noted on the medial aspect of the left lower leg, with raised, rolled edges and a necrotic base. There is regional lymphadenopathy. Given the clinical suspicion of squamous cell carcinoma, a biopsy is recommended. The patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9454321098]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP28 20100012345]] have been verified for contact. Her [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P8765432]] is available for any future travel-related procedures.

Please arrange for a comprehensive oncological workup, including imaging and histopathological confirmation. We request your expert opinion on the staging and definitive treatment plan. The patient's [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] is registered in the national health system. Her [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|30912345678901]] details are on file for any financial assistance queries. We look forward to your prompt attention to this complex case.

professional_persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a seasoned hotel steward known for their vivid storytelling, adept housekeeping, and inventive 'jugaad' solutions that make every guest feel at home.
linguistic_persona: Deepmala Tiwari speaks fluent Hindi enriched with the Awadhi dialect, understands basic spoken English through gestures and context, and excels at oral storytelling and memorising routines, despite being unable to read or write; they rely on vivid verbal expression to connect with guests.

travel_persona: Deepmala Tiwari enjoys weekend trips to Lucknow's Bara Imambara and the historic Taj Mahal in Agra, plans occasional pilgrimages to Varanasi, and they dream of a cultural tour of Jaipur's heritage hotels, balancing careful itinerary planning with spontaneous day-trips.
culinary_persona: Deepmala Tiwari delights in preparing Mishri ka halwa, richly spiced kebabs, and aromatic biryani, blending saffron and cardamom, and they often cook these dishes for guests, though they prefer to follow cherished family recipes rather than experiment.
persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a talkative, imaginative 42-year-old hospitality professional who blends traditional storytelling with practical 'jugaad', dreams of a heritage guesthouse, and spices life with folk music, embroidery, and generous hospitality; they radiate warmth wherever they go.
cultural_background: Deepmala hails from the Awadhi cultural milieu of the rural outskirts of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh. She grew up in a joint family where traditional North Indian customs, such as celebrating Diwali, Holi, and Navratri, were central. Her community places a high value on hospitality and sharing food, which later influenced her choice of a career in the hotel sector. Even without formal schooling, she inherited a rich oral tradition of folk songs, proverbs and the craft of Chikankari embroidery passed down from her mother and neighbours. Her cultural identity is a blend of rustic village life and the cosmopolitan exposure she gets daily from interacting with guests from diverse backgrounds.
linguistic_background: Deepmala's mother‑tongue is Hindi, and she commonly uses the Awadhi dialect in daily conversation. Being illiterate, she cannot read or write Hindi or English fluently, but she can recognise familiar symbols, hotel signage, and basic numerals. She understands simple spoken English from guests and manages communication through gestures, body language, and contextual cues. Her linguistic strengths lie in oral storytelling, memorising routines, and using vivid, imaginative vocabulary when interacting with guests, which helps her create a warm and engaging atmosphere.
skills_and_expertise: Deepmala excels in hospitality operations, especially in guest relations and housekeeping. She is adept at cleaning rooms, managing linens, serving food and beverages, and ensuring that guests feel welcomed. Her strong oral communication allows her to convey instructions and assist guests without relying on written material. She employs practical "jugaad" solutions to solve on‑the‑spot problems, and she is known for her storytelling ability, which adds a personal touch to guest interactions. Additionally, she possesses good knowledge of local attractions in Lucknow, basic first aid, and the ability to coordinate short‑term tasks among her team members.

hobbies_and_interests: Deepmala enjoys listening to traditional Awadhi folk music and devotional bhajans, attending Ramlila performances during festivals, and exploring Lucknow’s historic sites such as the Bara Imambara and Chota Imambara. She loves cooking and experimenting with regional recipes, especially Mishri ka halwa and kebabs. In her spare time she practices Chikankari embroidery, shares stories with neighbours, sings bhajans, and organises small gatherings where she can interact with friends and guests, reflecting her sociable nature.

career_goals_and_ambitions: Deepmala aspires to progress from her current steward role to a supervisory position within the hotel, eventually managing a small team and overseeing guest services. She envisions opening a boutique heritage guesthouse in Lucknow that showcases Awadhi culture, Chikankari crafts and traditional cuisine. In parallel, she wishes to enroll in adult literacy programmes to acquire basic reading and writing skills in Hindi, which will help her maintain records and communicate more effectively with management.

She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi dialect, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she may have limited health literacy, potential for hypertension due to dietary habits, and requires communication support for health instructions. Her lifestyle includes traditional storytelling, folk music, and limited physical activity, with possible joint discomfort.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Lucknow.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Steward, Hotel
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Lucknow
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi dialect, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she may have limited health literacy, potential for hypertension due to dietary habits, and requires communication support for health instructions. Her lifestyle includes traditional storytelling, folk music, and limited physical activity, with possible joint discomfort.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

रेडियोलॉजी रिपोर्ट — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Lucknow]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Devi]] [[AGE|42]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]] | रिपोर्ट किया गया डॉ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]] द्वारा

[[DISTRICT|Lucknow]] | [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] | [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]] | [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

professional_persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a seasoned hotel steward known for their vivid storytelling, adept housekeeping, and inventive 'jugaad' solutions that make every guest feel at home.
linguistic_persona: Deepmala Tiwari speaks fluent Hindi enriched with the Awadhi dialect, understands basic spoken English through gestures and context, and excels at oral storytelling and memorising routines, despite being unable to read or write; they rely on vivid verbal expression to connect with guests.
sports_persona: Deepmala Tiwari follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Lucknow Super Giants, participates in weekend walking clubs along the Gomti River, and they practice yoga each morning to ease joint discomfort, reflecting a balanced approach to fitness.
arts_persona: Deepmala Tiwari revels in Awadhi folk music, devotional bhajans, and Ramlila performances, often humming tunes of Ustad Rashid Khan and Shreya Ghoshal while they stitch intricate Chikankari designs inspired by traditional motifs, showcasing a deep love for cultural arts.
travel_persona: Deepmala Tiwari enjoys weekend trips to Lucknow's Bara Imambara and the historic Taj Mahal in Agra, plans occasional pilgrimages to Varanasi, and they dream of a cultural tour of Jaipur's heritage hotels, balancing careful itinerary planning with spontaneous day-trips.
culinary_persona: Deepmala Tiwari delights in preparing Mishri ka halwa, richly spiced kebabs, and aromatic biryani, blending saffron and cardamom, and they often cook these dishes for guests, though they prefer to follow cherished family recipes rather than experiment.
persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a talkative, imaginative 42-year-old hospitality professional who blends traditional storytelling with practical 'jugaad', dreams of a heritage guesthouse, and spices life with folk music, embroidery, and generous hospitality; they radiate warmth wherever they go.
cultural_background: Deepmala hails from the Awadhi cultural milieu of the rural outskirts of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh. She grew up in a joint family where traditional North Indian customs, such as celebrating Diwali, Holi, and Navratri, were central. Her community places a high value on hospitality and sharing food, which later influenced her choice of a career in the hotel sector. Even without formal schooling, she inherited a rich oral tradition of folk songs, proverbs and the craft of Chikankari embroidery passed down from her mother and neighbours. Her cultural identity is a blend of rustic village life and the cosmopolitan exposure she gets daily from interacting with guests from diverse backgrounds.
linguistic_background: Deepmala's mother‑tongue is Hindi, and she commonly uses the Awadhi dialect in daily conversation. Being illiterate, she cannot read or write Hindi or English fluently, but she can recognise familiar symbols, hotel signage, and basic numerals. She understands simple spoken English from guests and manages communication through gestures, body language, and contextual cues. Her linguistic strengths lie in oral storytelling, memorising routines, and using vivid, imaginative vocabulary when interacting with guests, which helps her create a warm and engaging atmosphere.
skills_and_expertise: Deepmala excels in hospitality operations, especially in guest relations and housekeeping. She is adept at cleaning rooms, managing linens, serving food and beverages, and ensuring that guests feel welcomed. Her strong oral communication allows her to convey instructions and assist guests without relying on written material. She employs practical "jugaad" solutions to solve on‑the‑spot problems, and she is known for her storytelling ability, which adds a personal touch to guest interactions. Additionally, she possesses good knowledge of local attractions in Lucknow, basic first aid, and the ability to coordinate short‑term tasks among her team members.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Guest relations", "Housekeeping", "Room cleaning", "Linen management", "Food and beverage service", "Storytelling", "Problem solving (jugaad)", "Team coordination", "Knowledge of local attractions", "Basic first aid"]
hobbies_and_interests: Deepmala enjoys listening to traditional Awadhi folk music and devotional bhajans, attending Ramlila performances during festivals, and exploring Lucknow’s historic sites such as the Bara Imambara and Chota Imambara. She loves cooking and experimenting with regional recipes, especially Mishri ka halwa and kebabs. In her spare time she practices Chikankari embroidery, shares stories with neighbours, sings bhajans, and organises small gatherings where she can interact with friends and guests, reflecting her sociable nature.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to folk music", "Attending Ramlila performances", "Cooking regional dishes", "Chikankari embroidery", "Storytelling with neighbours", "Singing devotional bhajans", "Exploring historic sites in Lucknow", "Socialising with guests and friends"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Deepmala aspires to progress from her current steward role to a supervisory position within the hotel, eventually managing a small team and overseeing guest services. She envisions opening a boutique heritage guesthouse in Lucknow that showcases Awadhi culture, Chikankari crafts and traditional cuisine. In parallel, she wishes to enroll in adult literacy programmes to acquire basic reading and writing skills in Hindi, which will help her maintain records and communicate more effectively with management.
persona_clinical_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she has limited literacy but can recognize basic signage and numbers. Her lifestyle includes traditional storytelling, folk music, and embroidery, with a preference for home-cooked meals.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]

  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Lucknow.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Steward, Hotel
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Lucknow
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she has limited literacy but can recognize basic signage and numbers. Her lifestyle includes traditional storytelling, folk music, and embroidery, with a preference for home-cooked meals.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
आपातकालीन विभाग ट्राइएज — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Lucknow]] मुठभेड़ [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Devi]] [[AGE|42]] [[GENDER|Female]] वार्ड [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] बेड [[BED_NUMBER|03]]
एम्बुलेंस द्वारा पहुँचे [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP32AB1234]] (सटीक रूप से एक बार)।
रिश्तेदार [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] फोन [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Singh]]
वाइटल्स / गंभीरता: रोगी सचेत, सतर्क, उन्मुख है। रक्तचाप 140/90 mmHg, नाड़ी 110/मिनट, श्वसन दर 22/मिनट, तापमान 37.8°C, कमरे की हवा में SpO2 98%। पेट में कोमलता मौजूद है, फंडस की ऊँचाई 36 सप्ताह। भ्रूण की हृदय गति 145/मिनट। मध्यम योनि रक्तस्राव, 2 पैड भीग गए। रोगी पिछले 4 घंटों से गंभीर निचले पेट दर्द और भ्रूण की हलचल में कमी की रिपोर्ट करता है। प्रारंभिक मूल्यांकन संभावित प्लेसेंटल एब्रप्शन (अपरा विलगन) के साथ समयपूर्व प्रसव का सुझाव देता है। IV लाइन स्थापित की गई, 2 यूनिट O-नेगेटिव रक्त क्रॉसमैच किया गया। निरंतर भ्रूण निगरानी शुरू की गई। आपातकालीन सिजेरियन सेक्शन के संबंध में रोगी को परामर्श दिया गया। ऑपरेशन थिएटर की तैयारी जारी है।

ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Lucknow]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Devi]] [[AGE|42]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|03]]
Arrived by ambulance [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP32AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Singh]]
Vitals / acuity: Patient conscious, alert, oriented. BP 140/90 mmHg, pulse 110/min, respiratory rate 22/min, temperature 37.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Abdominal tenderness present, fundal height 36 weeks. Fetal heart rate 145/min. Moderate vaginal bleeding, soaked 2 pads. Patient reports severe lower abdominal pain and decreased fetal movements for the past 4 hours. Initial assessment suggests possible placental abruption with preterm labor. IV line established, 2 units of O-negative blood crossmatched. Continuous fetal monitoring initiated. Patient counseled regarding emergency cesarean section. Preparation for OR ongoing.
judge_reasoning: Hindi Devanagari prose correct; all TYPEs allowed and culturally plausible; maternal 42F fits; Latin tags and clinical terms per rules; minor verb agreement not triggering listed flags.

professional_persona: Mani, a resilient waste collector who expertly sorts recyclables using hand‑cart tricks and rallies neighbours with spontaneous community clean‑up drives, they dream of formal training and a women‑run recycling centre that will dignify their work.
linguistic_persona: Mani, a fluent Kannada speaker who understands Hindi but cannot read or write either language, they rely on oral instructions and visual cues to navigate daily tasks.
sports_persona: Mani, an avid cricket enthusiast who follows matches on the village radio, roots for Royal Challengers Bangalore and cheers for the local Andhra women’s cricket team, occasionally joins neighbourhood kabaddi games for fun, and walks briskly to keep fit, they rarely visit a formal gym.
arts_persona: Mani, a passionate lover of folk music who sings along to Kannada legends like Kuttur Mallappa and Telugu troupe Kinnera, joins village bhajan circles, dances to traditional dandiya whenever festivals arrive, and keeps a collection of cassette tapes from her youth, they rarely have time for formal music lessons.
travel_persona: Mani, a modest traveler who takes occasional bus trips to the nearest market town of Ananthapur, the pilgrimage site of Tirumala, and once visited Hyderabad by train to see her niece, they plan journeys spontaneously with family, prefer budget seats and stays with relatives, and dream of a future weekend getaway to the beaches of Visakhapatnam.
culinary_persona: Mani, a home‑cooking enthusiast who prepares ragi mudde with spicy sambar, pulihora, fresh coconut chutney, and tangy mango pickle for neighbours, enjoys seasonal mangoes in salads and desserts, and while she effortlessly whips up these traditional dishes, they rarely experiment beyond the familiar pantry staples.
persona: Mani, a 40‑year‑old waste‑collector who blends spontaneous community‑jugaad with a deep love for folk songs, juggling chronic back ache and a dream of a women‑run recycling hub, they occasionally splurge on festival sweets despite a cash‑only lifestyle.
cultural_background: Mani belongs to a Kannada-speaking community that migrated to the rural outskirts of Anantapur in Andhra Pradesh. Her family has roots in the traditionally agricultural villages of northern Karnataka, but over generations they have settled in the agrarian hinterland of Andhra, adapting to local customs while preserving Kannada folk traditions, especially in food, festivals like Ugadi, and oral storytelling.
linguistic_background: Mani's mother tongue is Kannada, which she speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with neighbours and fellow workers. She also understands and converses in Hindi, the lingua franca of the region, though her literacy in both languages is limited due to illiteracy. She cannot read or write Kannada or Hindi, relying on oral instructions and visual cues for her work.
skills_and_expertise: Mani is adept at manual waste collection, efficiently gathering household garbage from narrow lanes using a hand‑cart. She can quickly sort recyclable materials (plastic, metal, paper) from non‑recyclables, applying simple segregation techniques. Her physical stamina enables her to work long hours in hot weather. She is skilled at community mobilisation, encouraging neighbours to keep streets clean, and uses practical ‘jugaad’ solutions to handle overflowing bins. She also knows basic first‑aid for minor injuries that occur on the job.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Garbage collection", "Waste segregation", "Recycling", "Physical stamina", "Community mobilisation", "Jugaad solutions", "Basic first aid"]
hobbies_and_interests: Mani loves listening to folk songs in Kannada and Telugu, especially those sung during village festivals, and often joins community dance circles. She enjoys gardening a small patch of vegetables near her house, tending to mango saplings, and cooking traditional meals for neighbours. In her free moments she watches cricket matches on a communal radio, participates in nightly bhajan sessions, and takes pride in experimenting with simple recycling crafts like making reusable cloth bags.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Folk singing", "Traditional dancing", "Vegetable gardening", "Cooking", "Watching cricket on radio", "Community bhajan sessions", "DIY recycling crafts"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Mani aspires to improve the dignity and safety of waste workers in her village. She hopes to receive formal training in waste management and eventually start a small community-run recycling centre that can generate modest income and provide employment to other widows and marginalised women. She also wishes to secure a pension or government welfare benefits for widows, ensuring stable financial support in her later years. Long‑term, she dreams of seeing her village cleaner, with less litter and better waste segregation practices.
persona_clinical_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from a rural area in Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed and speaks Kannada as her first language. She has an illiterate education level and works in the informal sector, experiencing chronic back pain. Her lifestyle includes a love for folk songs and occasional festival sweets, with limited formal education.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Anantapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: Garbage Collectors, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Anantapur
- first_language: Kannada
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from a rural area in Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed and speaks Kannada as her first language. She has an illiterate education level and works in the informal sector, experiencing chronic back pain. Her lifestyle includes a love for folk songs and occasional festival sweets, with limited formal education.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ಔಪಚಾರಿಕ ದಾಖಲೆ | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Anantapur]] | ಐಡಿ [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-AN-001]]
ರೋಗಿ: [[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi]] | ಜನ್ಮ ದಿನಾಂಕ [[DOB|1984-07-22]] | ವಯಸ್ಸು: [[AGE|40]] | ಲಿಂಗ: [[GENDER|Female]]
ವೃತ್ತಿ: [[OCCUPATION|Garbage Collector]] | ಎಂಆರ್‌ಎನ್: [[MRN|OPD-AN-2024-089]] | ವೈದ್ಯರು: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Reddy]]
ಸಂಬಂಧಿ: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Savitri]] | ಫೋನ್: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9440012345]]
ನೋಂದಣಿ ಉದ್ಯೋಗಿ ಐಡಿ: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-AN-042]] | ಜಿಲ್ಲೆ: [[DISTRICT|Anantapur]]
ಮುಖ್ಯ ದೂರು: 2 ತಿಂಗಳಿಂದ ಕೆಳಬೆನ್ನು ನೋವು ಮತ್ತು ಕಾಲಿನ ಊತ; ಗರ್ಭಧಾರಣೆಯ ಶಂಕೆ ಇದೆ. ಕೊನೆಯ ಮುಟ್ಟಿನ ಅವಧಿ (LMP) ಸುಮಾರು 8 ವಾರಗಳು, ಅಲ್ಟ್ರಾಸೌಂಡ್ (USG) ವರದಿಯು 12 ವಾರಗಳ ಗರ್ಭಧಾರಣೆಯನ್ನು ತೋರಿಸುತ್ತದೆ. ಯೋಜನೆ: ಕಬ್ಬಿಣದ ಅಂಶ ಮತ್ತು ಫೋಲಿಕ್ ಆಸಿಡ್ ಮಾತ್ರೆಗಳು, ಶ್ರೋಣಿ ವಿಶ್ರಾಂತಿ (pelvic rest), ಫಿಸಿಯೋಥೆರಪಿ ಶಿಫಾರಸು, 4 ವಾರಗಳ ನಂತರ ಮರುಪರಿಶೀಲನೆ.

judge_reasoning: Kannada prose correct; all TYPEs allowed; Latin tags expected; age 40 female maternal content plausible; no script/dialect or length issues.

professional_persona: Mani, a resilient waste collector who expertly sorts recyclables using hand‑cart tricks and rallies neighbours with spontaneous community clean‑up drives, they dream of formal training and a women‑run recycling centre that will dignify their work.
linguistic_persona: Mani, a fluent Kannada speaker who understands Hindi but cannot read or write either language, they rely on oral instructions and visual cues to navigate daily tasks.
sports_persona: Mani, an avid cricket enthusiast who follows matches on the village radio, roots for Royal Challengers Bangalore and cheers for the local Andhra women’s cricket team, occasionally joins neighbourhood kabaddi games for fun, and walks briskly to keep fit, they rarely visit a formal gym.
arts_persona: Mani, a passionate lover of folk music who sings along to Kannada legends like Kuttur Mallappa and Telugu troupe Kinnera, joins village bhajan circles, dances to traditional dandiya whenever festivals arrive, and keeps a collection of cassette tapes from her youth, they rarely have time for formal music lessons.
travel_persona: Mani, a modest traveler who takes occasional bus trips to the nearest market town of Ananthapur, the pilgrimage site of Tirumala, and once visited Hyderabad by train to see her niece, they plan journeys spontaneously with family, prefer budget seats and stays with relatives, and dream of a future weekend getaway to the beaches of Visakhapatnam.
culinary_persona: Mani, a home‑cooking enthusiast who prepares ragi mudde with spicy sambar, pulihora, fresh coconut chutney, and tangy mango pickle for neighbours, enjoys seasonal mangoes in salads and desserts, and while she effortlessly whips up these traditional dishes, they rarely experiment beyond the familiar pantry staples.
persona: Mani, a 40‑year‑old waste‑collector who blends spontaneous community‑jugaad with a deep love for folk songs, juggling chronic back ache and a dream of a women‑run recycling hub, they occasionally splurge on festival sweets despite a cash‑only lifestyle.
cultural_background: Mani belongs to a Kannada-speaking community that migrated to the rural outskirts of Anantapur in Andhra Pradesh. Her family has roots in the traditionally agricultural villages of northern Karnataka, but over generations they have settled in the agrarian hinterland of Andhra, adapting to local customs while preserving Kannada folk traditions, especially in food, festivals like Ugadi, and oral storytelling.
linguistic_background: Mani's mother tongue is Kannada, which she speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with neighbours and fellow workers. She also understands and converses in Hindi, the lingua franca of the region, though her literacy in both languages is limited due to illiteracy. She cannot read or write Kannada or Hindi, relying on oral instructions and visual cues for her work.
skills_and_expertise: Mani is adept at manual waste collection, efficiently gathering household garbage from narrow lanes using a hand‑cart. She can quickly sort recyclable materials (plastic, metal, paper) from non‑recyclables, applying simple segregation techniques. Her physical stamina enables her to work long hours in hot weather. She is skilled at community mobilisation, encouraging neighbours to keep streets clean, and uses practical ‘jugaad’ solutions to handle overflowing bins. She also knows basic first‑aid for minor injuries that occur on the job.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Garbage collection", "Waste segregation", "Recycling", "Physical stamina", "Community mobilisation", "Jugaad solutions", "Basic first aid"]
hobbies_and_interests: Mani loves listening to folk songs in Kannada and Telugu, especially those sung during village festivals, and often joins community dance circles. She enjoys gardening a small patch of vegetables near her house, tending to mango saplings, and cooking traditional meals for neighbours. In her free moments she watches cricket matches on a communal radio, participates in nightly bhajan sessions, and takes pride in experimenting with simple recycling crafts like making reusable cloth bags.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Folk singing", "Traditional dancing", "Vegetable gardening", "Cooking", "Watching cricket on radio", "Community bhajan sessions", "DIY recycling crafts"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Mani aspires to improve the dignity and safety of waste workers in her village. She hopes to receive formal training in waste management and eventually start a small community-run recycling centre that can generate modest income and provide employment to other widows and marginalised women. She also wishes to secure a pension or government welfare benefits for widows, ensuring stable financial support in her later years. Long‑term, she dreams of seeing her village cleaner, with less litter and better waste segregation practices.
persona_clinical_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from rural Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed and has an illiterate education, speaks Kannada as her first language and understands Hindi. She lives in a rural zone and works in the informal sector, experiencing chronic back pain. Her lifestyle includes community-oriented activities and a cultural affinity for folk music.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Anantapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: Garbage Collectors, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Anantapur
- first_language: Kannada
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from rural Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed and has an illiterate education, speaks Kannada as her first language and understands Hindi. She lives in a rural zone and works in the informal sector, experiencing chronic back pain. Her lifestyle includes community-oriented activities and a cultural affinity for folk music.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ಪ್ರಾಥಮಿಕ ಆರೋಗ್ಯ ಕೇಂದ್ರದ ದಾಖಲೆ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Anantapur]] ಗ್ರಾಮ [[VILLAGE|Kandukur]] ಜಿಲ್ಲೆ [[DISTRICT|Anantapur]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi]] [[AGE|40]] [[GENDER|Female]] | ಕ್ಷಯರೋಗ ತಪಾಸಣೆ ಮತ್ತು ಅಸಂಕ್ರಾಮಿಕ ರೋಗಗಳ (NCD) ವಿಮರ್ಶೆ

ರೋಗಿ ಲಕ್ಷ್ಮಿ, 40 ವರ್ಷದ ವಿಧವೆ, [[VILLAGE|Kandukur]] ಗ್ರಾಮದವರು, ತಮ್ಮ ಮಾಸಿಕ ಡಾಟ್ಸ್ (DOTS) ಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆ ಮತ್ತು ನಿಯಮಿತ ಅಸಂಕ್ರಾಮಿಕ ರೋಗಗಳ (NCD) ತಪಾಸಣೆಗಾಗಿ ಬಂದಿದ್ದರು. ಅವರು ಕಳೆದ ಮೂರು ವಾರಗಳಿಂದ ನಿರಂತರ ಒಣ ಕೆಮ್ಮು ಇರುವುದಾಗಿ ವರದಿ ಮಾಡಿದ್ದಾರೆ, ಇದು ಕಳೆದ ಒಂದು ವಾರದಿಂದ ಉಲ್ಬಣಗೊಂಡಿದೆ. ಅವರಿಗೆ ಸೌಮ್ಯವಾದ, ಮಧ್ಯಂತರ ಎದೆ ನೋವು ಮತ್ತು ಕಳೆದ ಎರಡು ತಿಂಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ಸುಮಾರು 3 ಕಿಲೋಗ್ರಾಂ ತೂಕ ಇಳಿಕೆಯಾಗಿರುವುದಾಗಿ ದೂರುತ್ತಾರೆ. ಅವರಿಗೆ ಅಧಿಕ ರಕ್ತದೊತ್ತಡದ ಇತಿಹಾಸವಿದೆ, ಇದಕ್ಕಾಗಿ ಅವರು ಪ್ರತಿದಿನ 5 ಮಿಲಿಗ್ರಾಂ ಅಮ್ಲೋಡಿಪೈನ್ (Amlodipine) ತೆಗೆದುಕೊಳ್ಳುತ್ತಾರೆ. ಪರೀಕ್ಷೆಯ ಸಮಯದಲ್ಲಿ, ಅವರ ಪ್ರಮುಖ ಲಕ್ಷಣಗಳು ಸ್ಥಿರವಾಗಿವೆ: ಬಿಪಿ 130/85 mmHg, ನಾಡಿಮಿಡಿತ ನಿಮಿಷಕ್ಕೆ 88, ಉಸಿರಾಟದ ದರ ನಿಮಿಷಕ್ಕೆ 18, ತಾಪಮಾನ 98.4°F. ಎದೆಯ ಆಸ್ಕಲ್ಟೇಶನ್ (Chest auscultation) ಪರೀಕ್ಷೆಯು ಬಲ ಮೇಲ್ಭಾಗದ ವಲಯದಲ್ಲಿ ಉಸಿರಾಟದ ಶಬ್ದಗಳು ಕಡಿಮೆಯಾಗಿರುವುದನ್ನು ಬಹಿರಂಗಪಡಿಸಿದೆ. ಅವರ ರಕ್ತದ ಸಕ್ಕರೆಯ ಉಪವಾಸದ ಮಟ್ಟವು 108 mg/dL ಇತ್ತು. ಜೀನ್ ಎಕ್ಸ್‌ಪರ್ಟ್ (GeneXpert) ಪರೀಕ್ಷೆಗೆ ಮಾದರಿಯನ್ನು ಸಂಗ್ರಹಿಸಲಾಯಿತು. ರೋಗಿಗೆ ಕ್ಷಯರೋಗ ವಿರೋಧಿ ಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ಪೂರ್ಣ ಕೋರ್ಸ್ ಅನ್ನು ಪೂರ್ಣಗೊಳಿಸುವ ಪ್ರಾಮುಖ್ಯತೆಯ ಬಗ್ಗೆ ಸಲಹೆ ನೀಡಲಾಯಿತು ಮತ್ತು ತಮ್ಮ ರಕ್ತದೊತ್ತಡ ವಿರೋಧಿ ಔಷಧಿಯನ್ನು ಮುಂದುವರಿಸಲು ಸೂಚಿಸಲಾಯಿತು. ಹೆಚ್ಚಿನ ಮೌಲ್ಯಮಾಪನ ಮತ್ತು ನಿರ್ವಹಣೆಗಾಗಿ ಅವರನ್ನು ಜಿಲ್ಲಾ ಆಸ್ಪತ್ರೆಗೆ ಕಳುಹಿಸಲಾಯಿತು.

[[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-AP-345678-9012]]
[[VOTER_ID|AP123456789]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
generated_text_en:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Anantapur]] Village [[VILLAGE|Kandukur]] District [[DISTRICT|Anantapur]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi]] [[AGE|40]] [[GENDER|Female]] | TB screening and NCD review

Patient Lakshmi, a 40-year-old widowed female from Kandukur village, presented for her monthly DOTS therapy and routine NCD screening. She reports persistent dry cough for the past three weeks, which has worsened over the last week. She also complains of mild, intermittent chest pain and progressive weight loss of approximately 3 kilograms in the last two months. She has a known history of hypertension, for which she takes Amlodipine 5mg daily. On examination, her vitals are stable: BP 130/85 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Respiratory Rate 18/min, Temperature 98.4°F. Chest auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds in the right upper zone. Her blood sugar fasting level was 108 mg/dL. A sputum sample was collected for GeneXpert testing. The patient was counseled on the importance of completing the full course of anti-tubercular therapy and was advised to continue her antihypertensive medication. She was referred to the District Hospital for further evaluation and management of her TB.

[[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-AP-345678-9012]]
[[VOTER_ID|AP123456789]]

persona: Mani, a 40‑year‑old waste‑collector who blends spontaneous community‑jugaad with a deep love for folk songs, juggling chronic back ache and a dream of a women‑run recycling hub, they occasionally splurge on festival sweets despite a cash‑only lifestyle.
cultural_background: Mani belongs to a Kannada-speaking community that migrated to the rural outskirts of Anantapur in Andhra Pradesh. Her family has roots in the traditionally agricultural villages of northern Karnataka, but over generations they have settled in the agrarian hinterland of Andhra, adapting to local customs while preserving Kannada folk traditions, especially in food, festivals like Ugadi, and oral storytelling.
linguistic_background: Mani's mother tongue is Kannada, which she speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with neighbours and fellow workers. She also understands and converses in Hindi, the lingua franca of the region, though her literacy in both languages is limited due to illiteracy. She cannot read or write Kannada or Hindi, relying on oral instructions and visual cues for her work.
skills_and_expertise: Mani is adept at manual waste collection, efficiently gathering household garbage from narrow lanes using a hand‑cart. She can quickly sort recyclable materials (plastic, metal, paper) from non‑recyclables, applying simple segregation techniques. Her physical stamina enables her to work long hours in hot weather. She is skilled at community mobilisation, encouraging neighbours to keep streets clean, and uses practical ‘jugaad’ solutions to handle overflowing bins. She also knows basic first‑aid for minor injuries that occur on the job.

hobbies_and_interests: Mani loves listening to folk songs in Kannada and Telugu, especially those sung during village festivals, and often joins community dance circles. hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Folk singing", "Traditional dancing", "Vegetable gardening", "Cooking", "Watching cricket on radio", "Community bhajan sessions", "DIY recycling crafts"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Mani aspires to improve the dignity and safety of waste workers in her village. She hopes to receive formal training in waste management and eventually start a small community-run recycling centre that can generate modest income and provide employment to other widows and marginalised women. Long‑term, she dreams of seeing her village cleaner, with less litter and better waste segregation practices.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
