user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Anantapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: Garbage Collectors, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Anantapur
- first_language: Kannada
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from a rural, illiterate, Kannada-speaking background in Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed. She works in the informal sector and has limited formal education, relying on community-based solutions for daily challenges. Her lifestyle includes chronic back pain and a cultural affinity for folk songs, with occasional consumption of festival sweets. She demonstrates resilience in her occupation and expresses aspirations for a women-run recycling hub.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

arts_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, an avid follower of Goan folk legends like Remo Fernandes and Lorna, revels in qawwali evenings featuring vocalist Firoz Ali, and spends spare time capturing sunrise silhouettes at Miramar through his DSLR, often drawing inspiration from exhibitions of local painter Antonio Demétrio.
travel_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a road‑trip enthusiast, enjoys weekend getaways to nearby beaches like Colva and Diu, has trekked the Western Ghats in Mahabaleshwar, and dreams of a cultural tour of Lisbon to soak up the Portuguese legacy, always balancing careful budgeting with a love for spontaneous coastal drives.
culinary_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a modest home cook, loves preparing Goan fish curry with kokum and coconut milk, sizzling prawn balchão, and savoring freshly caught pomfret, while often sprinkling extra chilies that sometimes leave his palate burning.
persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a diligent trucker with a photographer’s eye, blends disciplined routes with a love for coastal art, occasional spice‑overload, and a steady plan to turn his wheels into a future logistics empire.
cultural_background: Mohamad Sheikh belongs to the small but vibrant Muslim community in South Goa, a region where Konkani is the mother tongue and the Portuguese colonial legacy blends with Indian traditions. Growing up in an urban coastal town, he has been exposed to a mix of Goan Catholic and Muslim cultural practices, enjoys local festivals, seafood cuisine and the multilingual ambience of the area.
linguistic_background: Mohamad is fluent in Konkani, his first language, and can comfortably read and write it using the Devanagari script. He also speaks Marathi fluently, communicating effortlessly in daily interactions and can read Marathi with moderate proficiency. Hindi, learned as his third language, is well understood for spoken communication and basic reading, though his writing skills are limited. Due to his occupation, he possesses functional English literacy sufficient for reading road signs, basic forms, and simple instructions.
skills_and_expertise: Mohamad has extensive expertise in operating heavy trucks and lorries, including maneuvering large vehicles through congested urban roads and coastal highways. He is proficient in vehicle inspection, routine maintenance, and troubleshooting mechanical issues on the spot. His knowledge of route planning, logistics coordination, and compliance with transport regulations ensures timely deliveries. He also possesses strong communication skills in Konkani, Marathi, and basic Hindi, enabling effective interaction with diverse clients and dispatchers.

hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Mohamad enjoys listening to Goan folk music and qawwali, visiting local art exhibitions, and capturing coastal scenery through photography. He loves cooking traditional Goan seafood dishes, playing cricket with friends, and reading short stories in Konkani. career_goals_and_ambitions: As a truck driver, Mohamad aspires to become a senior fleet manager, overseeing a team of drivers and eventually launching his own logistics enterprise in Goa. He plans to obtain advanced driver certifications, master modern fleet management software, and increase his earnings to secure financial stability for a future family. GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Clinical Summary:
Chief Complaint: तीन सप्तकां सावन सतत खोकली, मधून मधून उण्या प्रमाणांत जोर आनी रातचो घाम येवप. Patient-ाक वाडपी थकवो आनी फाटल्या म्हयन्यांत सुमार पांच किलो वजन उणें जाल्ल्याचें सांगता. ताणें हीं लक्षणां आपल्या जड ट्रक ड्रायव्हिंग कामाक आनी अनियमित न्हिदेच्या पद्धतीक लागून जाल्यात अशें मानलां.

Mohamad Sheikh, एक 30 वर्सांचो दादलो जड ट्रक ड्रायव्हर, ताका तीन सप्तकां सावन खोकली येता, जी सुरवेक सुकी आशिल्ली आनी मागीर उण्या प्रमाणांत कफ येवपाक लागली. ताका सांजवेळार उण्या प्रमाणांत जोर येता आनी रातचो घाम येता. ताणें वाडपी थकवो आनी पांच किलो वजन उणें जाल्ल्याचें सांगलां. ताणें रगत येवप, छातींत दूख वा स्वास घेवपाक त्रास जावप हाका न्हयकारला. ताणें फाटल्या धा वर्सां सावन दिसाक सुमार धा बिडी ओडपाची सवय आशिल्ल्याचें सांगलां.

Past Medical History:
खंयचीच वळखिल्ली जुनी दुयेंसां नात. पयलीं खंयचेंय hospitalization जावंक ना. खंयचीच वळखिल्ली allergies नात.

Investigations:
Chest X-ray: दाव्या वयरल्या भागांत infiltrate आनी cavitation दिसता, जें pulmonary tuberculosis-चें सुचोवणें दिता.

Complete Blood Count: Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, WBC count 8,000/cumm with neutrophilia.
Liver and Kidney Function Tests: Normal limits-च्या भितर आसात.

Diagnosis:
Pulmonary Tuberculosis, नव्यान निदान जाल्लें, दावो वयरलो भाग.

Treatment Plan:
National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme guidelines प्रमाणें Category I DOTS regimen सुरू केलां.
Isoniazid 300 mg daily, Rifampicin 450 mg daily, Pyrazinamide 1.5 g daily, Ethambutol 750 mg daily दोन म्हयन्यां खातीर, मागीर Isoniazid आनी Rifampicin चार म्हयन्यां खातीर.
Patient-ाक वखदां घेवपाची आनी side effects-ची म्हायती दिली.
Directly observed therapy-खातीर District TB Officer-ा कडेन धाडला.

Prognosis:
बरें, जर वखदां घेवपाची पद्धत पाळ्ळी तर.

Billing Summary:
दोन म्हयन्यांच्या anti-tubercular therapy आनी सुरवेच्या तपासणी खातीर एकूण पंदरा हजार रुपयांचो खर्च आयला. हो claim policy-च्या अंतर्गत reimbursement-खातीर सादर केला.

Clinical Summary:
Chief Complaint: Persistent cough for three weeks with intermittent low-grade fever and night sweats. Patient reports increasing fatigue and weight loss of approximately five kilograms over the past month. He attributes the symptoms to his heavy-duty driving job and irregular sleep patterns.

History of Present Illness:
Mohamad Sheikh, a 30-year-old male heavy truck driver, presents with a three-week history of a productive cough, initially dry and later with scanty sputum. He experiences low-grade fever in the evenings and drenching night sweats. He reports progressive fatigue and a five-kilogram weight loss. He denies hemoptysis, chest pain, or shortness of breath. He mentions a history of smoking bidis for the past ten years, approximately ten per day.

Past Medical History:
No known chronic illnesses. No prior hospitalizations. No known allergies.

Physical Examination:
General: Thin, cachectic male, appears fatigued.
Vital Signs: BP 120/80 mmHg, Pulse 98/min, Respiratory Rate 18/min, Temperature 37.8°C, SpO2 96% on room air.
Chest: Bilateral breath sounds with fine inspiratory crackles noted in the left upper zone. No wheezing or rhonchi. No signs of clubbing.

Investigations:
Chest X-ray: Shows a left upper lobe infiltrate with cavitation, suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis.
Sputum for AFB: Positive.
Sputum for GeneXpert: Positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex, resistant to none of the first-line drugs.
Complete Blood Count: Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, WBC count 8,000/cumm with neutrophilia.
Liver and Kidney Function Tests: Within normal limits.

Diagnosis:
Pulmonary Tuberculosis, newly diagnosed, left upper lobe.

Treatment Plan:
Started on Category I DOTS regimen as per National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme guidelines.
Isoniazid 300 mg daily, Rifampicin 450 mg daily, Pyrazinamide 1.5 g daily, Ethambutol 750 mg daily for two months, followed by Isoniazid and Rifampicin for four months.
Patient counseled on treatment adherence and side effects.
Referred to the District TB Officer for directly observed therapy.

Prognosis:
Good, provided treatment adherence is maintained.

Billing Summary:
Total estimated cost for two months of anti-tubercular therapy and initial investigations is fifteen thousand rupees. The claim is submitted for reimbursement under the policy.

I hereby declare that the above information is true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

professional_persona: Siraya Begam, a 25‑year‑old deep‑sea fisherwoman, expertly navigates the Arabian Sea, maintains and repairs large fishing nets and boat engines, and they leverage their talkative, sociable nature to coordinate crew and negotiate fair prices for the catch.
linguistic_persona: Siraya Begam speaks fluently in Kashmiri, handles everyday conversations in Hindi, grasps basic English nautical terms, and despite being illiterate, they excel at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses, and recognising simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
sports_persona: Siraya Begam keeps fit by swimming in Dal Lake, jogging along its promenade, playing occasional cricket with friends at the local Srinagar United Cricket Club, and they cheer for Real Kashmir FC during football season, while also practicing yoga for flexibility.
arts_persona: Siraya Begam loves listening to the soulful Sufi qawwalis of Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan and the melodious Kashmiri folk ballads of Mahajla, attends local cultural evenings where the sarangi is played, and they spend evenings carving wooden toys inspired by the designs of local artisan craftsmen.
travel_persona: Siraya Begam enjoys spontaneous weekend getaways, often exploring nearby hill stations like Gulmarg and Pahalgam, has taken a short trip to Delhi’s fish market to source new varieties, and they plan to voyage to Kerala’s coastal towns and Goa’s beaches to learn sustainable fishing practices, while preferring budget-friendly buses and shared rides over luxury travel.
culinary_persona: Siraya Begam relishes the rich flavors of their homemade Kashmiri fish curry, simmered with mustard oil, fennel seeds, dry red chilies, and a pinch of garam masala, delights in sipping kahwa infused with saffron and almonds, and they often enjoy crunchy pakoras made from fresh eggplant, though they rarely cook for themselves, preferring to share meals with the crew.
persona: Siraya Begam is a charismatic 25‑year‑old fisherwoman who blends a love for sea‑borne storytelling with a restless curiosity for sustainable tech, and they juggle nightly prayers while indulging in an occasional weakness for scented candles.
cultural_background: Siraya Begam hails from the vibrant Kashmiri community of Srinagar, an urban hub in the scenic valleys of Jammu & Kashmir. As a Muslim woman from the traditional fisherfolk of the Jhelum river and nearby seas, her cultural identity is woven from the rich tapestry of Kashmiri language, Sufi music, Pashmina weaving motifs, and the collective ethos of the local fishing cooperatives. Growing up amidst the lilting thaats of Kashmiri folk songs and the bustle of the local fish market, Siraya embodies the blend of traditional Kashmiri hospitality, modesty rooted in Islamic values, and the pragmatic resilience that characterises the deep‑sea fishing community.
linguistic_background: Linguistically, Siraya’s mother tongue is Kashmiri, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication within her family and the fishing crew. She has functional listening and speaking skills in Hindi, picked up through market interactions, and can understand basic English nautical terms such as ‘net’, ‘boat’, and ‘weather’, often heard from fellow fishermen or radio broadcasts. Siraya is illiterate, meaning she cannot read or write in any language, but she excels at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses and trade instructions, and she can recognise simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
skills_and_expertise: Siraya possesses hands‑on expertise in deep‑sea fishing operations. She is adept at navigating the open waters of the Arabian Sea using traditional knowledge of currents, tides, and weather patterns. She skillfully casts, repairs and maintains large fishing nets, and can identify a wide range of fish species common to the region. Her technical abilities extend to maintaining boat engines, handling rope and rigging, and ensuring safety protocols are followed on board. Moreover, her strong interpersonal skills enable effective coordination with crew members, quick decision‑making during emergencies, and community outreach to market the catch.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Deep sea navigation', 'Net casting and repair', 'Marine weather forecasting', 'Fish species identification', 'Boat engine maintenance', 'Rope and rigging handling', 'Safety protocol enforcement', 'Crew coordination', 'Community market outreach', 'Oral storytelling']
hobbies_and_interests: Siraya enjoys exploring various forms of art and culture. She often visits local tea houses to listen to Kashmiri folk songs and Sufi qawwalis, and she practices the traditional “Ropar” dance during festivals. In her downtime, she loves crafting simple wooden toys for children, cooking aromatic fish curries, and sharing oral stories of sea voyages with her peers. She is also fascinated by photography, using a basic mobile camera to capture sunrise over Dal Lake and the vibrant life aboard the fishing boat.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kashmiri folk music', 'Dancing Ropar during festivals', 'Crafting wooden toys', 'Cooking fish curries', 'Sharing oral sea stories', 'Mobile photography of sunrise and boats', 'Visiting tea houses', 'Listening to Sufi qawwalis']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Siraya aspires to modernise her deep‑sea fishing practices by learning sustainable techniques and acquiring basic literacy to manage catch records and market transactions. She aims to establish a women‑led fishing cooperative that empowers fellow female fishers, negotiates better prices, and promotes eco‑friendly gear. Over the next five years, she hopes to obtain a certification in marine safety, upgrade her boat with a more efficient engine, and travel to coastal ports in Kerala and Goa to learn from other fishing communities. Ultimately, she envisions becoming a mentor who blends traditional Kashmiri maritime heritage with contemporary practices, while also supporting local art initiatives.
persona_clinical_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, works as a deep-sea fisherwoman with an illiterate education level. She is never married, speaks Kashmiri as her first language, and lives in an urban zone. Her lifestyle includes swimming in Dal Lake and practicing Sufi qawwali.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 25
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Fisherman, Deep Sea
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, works as a deep-sea fisherwoman with an illiterate education level. She is never married, speaks Kashmiri as her first language, and lives in an urban zone. Her lifestyle includes swimming in Dal Lake and practicing Sufi qawwali.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

linguistic_background: Linguistically, Siraya’s mother tongue is Kashmiri, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication within her family and the fishing crew. She has functional listening and speaking skills in Hindi, picked up through market interactions, and can understand basic English nautical terms such as ‘net’, ‘boat’, and ‘weather’, often heard from fellow fishermen or radio broadcasts. Siraya is illiterate, meaning she cannot read or write in any language, but she excels at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses and trade instructions, and she can recognise simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
skills_and_expertise: Siraya possesses hands‑on expertise in deep‑sea fishing operations. She is adept at navigating the open waters of the Arabian Sea using traditional knowledge of currents, tides, and weather patterns. She skillfully casts, repairs and maintains large fishing nets, and can identify a wide range of fish species common to the region. Her technical abilities extend to maintaining boat engines, handling rope and rigging, and ensuring safety protocols are followed on board. Moreover, her strong interpersonal skills enable effective coordination with crew members, quick decision‑making during emergencies, and community outreach to market the catch.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Deep sea navigation', 'Net casting and repair', 'Marine weather forecasting', 'Fish species identification', 'Boat engine maintenance', 'Rope and rigging handling', 'Safety protocol enforcement', 'Crew coordination', 'Community market outreach', 'Oral storytelling']
hobbies_and_interests: Siraya enjoys exploring various forms of art and culture. She often visits local tea houses to listen to Kashmiri folk songs and Sufi qawwalis, and she practices the traditional “Ropar” dance during festivals. In her downtime, she loves crafting simple wooden toys for children, cooking aromatic fish curries, and sharing oral stories of sea voyages with her peers. She is also fascinated by photography, using a basic mobile camera to capture sunrise over Dal Lake and the vibrant life aboard the fishing boat.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kashmiri folk music', 'Dancing Ropar during festivals', 'Crafting wooden toys', 'Cooking fish curries', 'Sharing oral sea stories', 'Mobile photography of sunrise and boats', 'Visiting tea houses', 'Listening to Sufi qawwalis']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Siraya aspires to modernise her deep‑sea fishing practices by learning sustainable techniques and acquiring basic literacy to manage catch records and market transactions. She aims to establish a women‑led fishing cooperative that empowers fellow female fishers, negotiates better prices, and promotes eco‑friendly gear. Over the next five years, she hopes to obtain a certification in marine safety, upgrade her boat with a more efficient engine, and travel to coastal ports in Kerala and Goa to learn from other fishing communities. Ultimately, she envisions becoming a mentor who blends traditional Kashmiri maritime heritage with contemporary practices, while also supporting local art initiatives.
persona_clinical_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is never married and works as a deep-sea fisherwoman with an illiterate education. Her first language is Kashmiri, and she has functional Hindi skills. She lives in an urban zone and may have limited access to formal healthcare.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

BAD → instruction_drift:

GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 25
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Fisherman, Deep Sea
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is never married and works as a deep-sea fisherwoman. She is illiterate, speaks Kashmiri as her first language, and has functional Hindi skills. Her lifestyle includes swimming in Dal Lake and occasional cricket, with a cultural background rooted in the Kashmiri Muslim fisherfolk community.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|کۆتھہ]], District [[DISTRICT|بڈگام]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]], [[AGE|25]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Shabnam Bano]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Visit findings: Patient reports persistent cough with fever for three days. She feels weakness and body ache. No known allergies. Mother [[RELATIVE_NAME|Zainab Begum]] accompanies her. BPL card [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-JK-2024-001234]] is presented. Voter ID [[VOTER_ID|JK1234567]] verified. Religion [[RELIGION|Muslim]].

ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|Dal Lake]], District [[DISTRICT|Srinagar]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]], [[AGE|25]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Shabnam Bano]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Visit findings: Patient reports persistent cough with fever for three days. She feels weakness and body ache. No known allergies. Mother [[RELATIVE_NAME|Zainab Begum]] accompanies her. BPL card [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-JK-2024-001234]] is presented. Voter ID [[VOTER_ID|JK1234567]] verified. Religion [[RELIGION|Muslim]].
judge_reasoning: Narrative prose entirely in English instead of required Kashmiri Arabic script; minor district mismatch but language is primary failure.

professional_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, a seasoned loader and unloader with over two decades of experience, combines meticulous cargo arrangement skills with a curious eye for valuable Madhubani artwork, and they are regarded as a reliable yet innovative asset in the local logistics hub.
linguistic_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, whose mother tongue is Maithili, speaks fluent spoken Hindi for daily trade, learns new vocabulary via radio and community storytelling, and they can decipher common symbols and signs despite being illiterate.
sports_persona: Subhash Chaudhary follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Kolkata Knight Riders and the Bihar cricket team on the communal TV, and they maintain their fitness by taking early morning walks along the Kosi River banks.
arts_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, an avid admirer of Madhubani art, often discusses intricate motifs with local artist Shanti Devi, listens to Maithili folk singer Sharda Sinha, and they occasionally help select canvases that could fetch higher prices during transport.
travel_persona: Subhash Chaudhary prefers short, affordable trips, having visited the historic city of Varanasi and the serene banks of the Ganga, while they dream of a weekend pilgrimage to Puri and a future road trip to the Himalayan foothills.
culinary_persona: Subhash Chaudhary enjoys hearty meals of litti‑chokha, sattu paratha, and fish curry with mustard seeds, prefers the tangy flavor of raw mango pickle, and they often dine at the local dhaba where they also chat about art and logistics.
persona: Subhash Chaudhary is a diligent logistics worker with a quiet love for Madhubani art, modest savings, and a regular habit of watching cricket, yet they occasionally splurge on a new TV set, reflecting their blend of practicality and quiet indulgence.
cultural_background: Subhash belongs to the Maithil community of Supaul in rural Bihar. His daily life is shaped by the agrarian rhythms, close‑knit family ties, and the vibrant Mithila heritage that includes folk songs, seasonal festivals, and the famous Madhubani art form. He participates in village gatherings, observes local customs around marriage and harvest, and values the community’s emphasis on hospitality and collective support.
linguistic_background: Subhash speaks Maithili as his mother tongue and is fluent in spoken Hindi, which he uses for daily transactions and communicating with traders. Being illiterate, he cannot read or write in any language, but he can recognize common symbols and signs, and he learns new words through listening to radio programmes and community storytelling.
skills_and_expertise: With more than twenty years of experience as a loader and unloader, Subhash is adept at heavy lifting, arranging cargo for optimal weight distribution, and coordinating with truck drivers to ensure timely loading. He has practical knowledge of basic mechanical troubleshooting for equipment, understands local market schedules, and uses his curiosity for art to identify valuable items during transport. His reliability and organized approach make him a trusted worker in the supply chain.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Heavy lifting", "Cargo weight distribution", "Coordination with truck drivers", "Basic mechanical troubleshooting", "Local market schedule knowledge", "Madhubani art appreciation"]
hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Subhash enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs, watching cricket matches on the village’s shared TV, and walking along the banks of the Kosi River. He has a keen eye for Madhubani patterns and often spends evenings discussing the designs with local artisans. He also likes gardening, growing seasonal vegetables, and reading aloud community newsletters circulated by the panchayat.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Maithili folk songs", "Watching cricket on village TV", "Walking by the Kosi River", "Discussing Madhubani designs with artisans", "Gardening seasonal vegetables", "Reading aloud community newsletters"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Subhash aspires to move from manual loading to a supervisory role in the local logistics hub, eventually overseeing a small team of loaders. He wishes to acquire basic literacy through an adult education program, enabling him to keep simple records and read work orders. In the longer term, he dreams of starting a modest transport service that can support his family and contribute to community development, while also promoting Mithila art by sponsoring local Madhubani workshops.
persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Subhash Chaudhary, a 45-year-old male loader and unloader, is married and resides in a rural zone of Supaul, Bihar. He is illiterate, speaks Maithili as his first language, and has limited education. His lifestyle includes a modest income, a habit of watching cricket, and occasional purchases of household items.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

generated_text:
प्रयोगशाला रिपोर्ट — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Supaul District Hospital]] जिला [[DISTRICT|Supaul]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-BI-2024-0912]] रोगी पहचान [[PATIENT_ID|PID-BI-5432]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Subhash Chaudhary]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Male]] फोन [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431023456]]
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Amit Kumar]] द्वारा आदेशित
रोगी तीन सप्ताहक इतिहासक संग उत्पादक खाँसी, कम बुखार, आ वजन कम होयबाक शिकायत कयलनि। सूक्ष्मदर्शी आ GeneXpert MTB/RIF परीक्षणक लेल थूकक नमूना एकत्रित कयल गेल। रोगीकेँ उच्च रक्तचाप (hypertension) क इतिहास अछि आ ओ नियमित औषधि सेवन कऽ रहल छथि।

परिणाम:
थूक सूक्ष्मदर्शी (Ziehl-Neelsen रंजक): 2+ क्षेत्रमे एसिड-फास्ट बेसिली (AFB) देखल गेल।
थूक GeneXpert MTB/RIF: माइकोबैक्टीरियम ट्यूबरकुलोसिस कॉम्प्लेक्सक पता लागल, रिफैम्पिसिन प्रतिरोधक पता नहि चलल।
थूक संवर्धन (Culture): लंबित।
पूर्ण रक्त गणना (CBC): हीमोग्लोबिन 11.2 g/dL, कुल ल्यूकोसाइट गणना 7,800 कोशिका/µL।
यकृत कार्य परीक्षण (LFT): SGOT 45 U/L, SGPT 52 U/L, अल्कलाइन फॉस्फेट 78 U/L।
वृक्क कार्य परीक्षण (RFT): सीरम क्रिएटिनिन 0.9 mg/dL, रक्त यूरिया नाइट्रोजन 18 mg/dL।
रक्तचाप: 145/90 mmHg।
रक्त शर्करा (उपवास): 110 mg/dL।

व्याख्या:
निष्कर्ष फुफ्फुसीय तपेदिक (Pulmonary Tuberculosis) क अनुरूप अछि। रोगीकेँ उच्च रक्तचाप आ हल्का रक्ताल्पता (anemia) सेहो देखल गेल अछि। संशोधित राष्ट्रीय तपेदिक नियंत्रण कार्यक्रम (RNTCP) क दिशानिर्देशानुसार हुनका एंटी-ट्यूबरकुलर थेरेपी शुरू करबाक सलाह देल जाइत अछि।

योजना:
1. चारि-औषधि पद्धति शुरू करू: आइसोनियाज़िड, रिफैम्पिसिन, पाइराज़िनामाइड, आ एथमब्यूटोल।
2. उच्च रक्तचाप विरोधी औषधि जारी राखू।
3. पोषण संबंधी परामर्श आ सहायता प्रदान करू।
4. उपचारक प्रतिक्रिया आ औषधिक सहनशीलताक निगरानीक लेल दू सप्ताहमे अनुवर्ती (follow-up) जाँचक समय निर्धारित करू।
5. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sita Devi]] सँ DOTS सहायताक लेल संपर्क करू।
generated_text_en:
Lab Report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Supaul District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Supaul]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-BI-2024-0912]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-BI-5432]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Subhash Chaudhary]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Male]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431023456]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Amit Kumar]]
Patient presented with a three-week history of productive cough, low-grade fever, and weight loss. Sputum sample was collected for microscopy and GeneXpert MTB/RIF assay. The patient has a history of hypertension and is on regular medication.

Results:
Sputum Microscopy (Ziehl-Neelsen stain): Acid-fast bacilli (AFB) seen in 2+ fields.
Sputum GeneXpert MTB/RIF: Detected Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex, Rifampicin resistance not detected.
Sputum Culture: Pending.
Complete Blood Count (CBC): Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Total Leukocyte Count 7,800 cells/µL.
Liver Function Test (LFT): SGOT 45 U/L, SGPT 52 U/L, Alkaline Phosphatase 78 U/L.
Renal Function Test (RFT): Serum Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, Blood Urea Nitrogen 18 mg/dL.
Blood Pressure: 145/90 mmHg.
Blood Glucose (Fasting): 110 mg/dL.

Interpretation:
The findings are consistent with Pulmonary Tuberculosis. The patient is also noted to have hypertension and mild anemia. He is advised to start anti-tubercular therapy as per the Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme (RNTCP) guidelines.

Plan:
1. Initiate Four-Drug Regimen: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol.
2. Continue antihypertensive medication.
3. Provide nutritional counseling and support.
4. Schedule follow-up in two weeks for monitoring of treatment response and drug tolerance.
5. Contact [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sita Devi]] for DOTS support.
