system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Kannur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Market-Oriented Crop and Animal Producers, Other
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Kannur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old male farmer from rural Kerala, currently married, with higher secondary education, who speaks Malayalam and has a competitive, stress-prone personality. He works in market-oriented crop and animal production, managing integrated farming systems while dealing with worry-driven stress. His lifestyle includes organizing local cricket matches and following Malayalam cinema.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | MRN [[MRN|...]]
Address district [[DISTRICT|...]] PIN [[PIN_CODE|110001]]
Ambulance / parking vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PIN_CODE — ZIP / Postal Code (PIN Code) e.g. [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- TAX_ID — Tax Identification Number (TIN/GSTIN) e.g. [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
- TELEPHONE_LANDLINE — Telephone Number (Landline) e.g. [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

He does not have proficiency in any other Indian language.

His business acumen includes market price analysis, trade negotiation, farm accounting, and navigating government subsidy schemes. - Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Kannur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Market-Oriented Crop and Animal Producers, Other
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural

His first language is Malayalam, and he works in agriculture while managing stress related to farming. He resides in Kannur district and is part of the Malayali Christian community.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Kannur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Market-Oriented Crop and Animal Producers, Other
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Kannur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old male farmer from rural Kerala, currently married, with a market-oriented crop and animal production occupation and higher secondary education. He speaks Malayalam and has limited English proficiency, and his lifestyle involves agronomic planning and livestock management. He experiences stress related to market fluctuations and agricultural uncertainties.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
റഫറൽ [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0912]] [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kannur District Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anil Menon]]
വിഷയം: [[PATIENT_NAME|Kunjikannan]] [[AGE|45]] / [[GENDER|Male]], ജില്ല [[DISTRICT|Kannur]]
കാരണം: വിട്ടുമാറാത്ത ചുമ, ഭാരക്കുറവ്, രാത്രികാലങ്ങളിൽ വിയർക്കൽ എന്നിവയുമായി ബന്ധപ്പെട്ട സമഗ്രമായ വിലയിരുത്തൽ ആവശ്യമാണ്. ശ്വാസകോശ ക്ഷയരോഗം (Pulmonary tuberculosis) നിർണ്ണയിക്കുന്നതിനും അതോടൊപ്പം മറ്റ് പകർച്ചേതര രോഗങ്ങളുടെ (non-communicable diseases) പരിശോധനയ്ക്കും ഇത് ആവശ്യമാണ്.

പ്രിയപ്പെട്ട സർ/മാഡം,

ഞങ്ങൾ [[PATIENT_NAME|Kunjikannan]] എന്ന രോഗിയെ, 45 വയസ്സുള്ള ഒരു കർഷകനെ, കൂടുതൽ പരിശോധനകൾക്കും ചികിത്സയ്ക്കുമായി നിങ്ങളുടെ അടുത്തേക്ക് അയക്കുന്നു. രോഗിക്ക് കഴിഞ്ഞ മൂന്ന് മാസമായി ചുമയുണ്ട്, ഇടവിട്ടുള്ള പനി, ഏകദേശം 8 കിലോ ഭാരക്കുറവ് എന്നിവ അനുഭവപ്പെടുന്നു. കൂടാതെ രാത്രികാലങ്ങളിൽ വിയർക്കുന്നതായും കഠിനമായ ജോലികൾ ചെയ്യുമ്പോൾ ശ്വാസംമുട്ടുന്നതായും അദ്ദേഹം പരാതിപ്പെടുന്നു. അദ്ദേഹത്തിന്റെ മുൻകാല രോഗചരിത്രം പരിശോധിച്ചാൽ, രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം (hypertension) ഉണ്ടെന്ന് കാണാം. ഇതിനായി അദ്ദേഹം കൃത്യമായ മരുന്നുകൾ കഴിക്കുന്നില്ല.

പരിശോധനയിൽ, രോഗി വളരെ ക്ഷയിച്ച അവസ്ഥയിലാണ് (cachectic), അദ്ദേഹത്തിന്റെ ബി.എം.ഐ (BMI) 17.5 kg/m2 ആണ്. രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം 150/95 mmHg-ഉം, നാഡിമിടിപ്പ് മിനിറ്റിൽ 98 തവണയുമാണ്. ശ്വാസകോശ പരിശോധനയിൽ (Chest auscultation) വലതുവശത്തെ മുകൾ ഭാഗത്ത് ശ്വസനശബ്ദം കുറവാണെന്നും ഇടയ്ക്കിടെ ശ്വാസകോശത്തിലെ ശബ്ദങ്ങൾ (crepitations) കേൾക്കുന്നുണ്ടെന്നും കണ്ടെത്തി. പ്രാഥമികമായി ശ്വാസകോശ ക്ഷയരോഗവും അതോടൊപ്പം രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദവും ഉണ്ടെന്നാണ് നിഗമനം.

ഞങ്ങളുടെ ആശുപത്രിയിൽ നടത്തിയ പരിശോധനകളിൽ, GeneXpert MTB/RIF പരിശോധനയ്ക്കായി കഫം ശേഖരിച്ചിട്ടുണ്ട്, അതിന്റെ ഫലം വരാനിരിക്കുകയാണ്. നെഞ്ചിന്റെ എക്സ്-റേ (Chest X-ray) പരിശോധനയിൽ വലതുവശത്തെ മുകൾ ഭാഗത്ത് ഇൻഫിൽട്രേറ്റും (infiltrate) കാവിറ്റേഷനും (cavitation) കാണപ്പെടുന്നു. രക്തപരിശോധനയിൽ റാൻഡം ബ്ലഡ് ഗ്ലൂക്കോസ് 140 mg/dL-ഉം, HbA1c 7.2%-ഉം ആണ്.

ക്ഷയരോഗത്തിനുള്ള മരുന്നുകൾ (anti-tubercular therapy) നൽകി രോഗിയെ ചികിത്സിക്കാനും, അദ്ദേഹത്തിന്റെ രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദവും പ്രമേഹവും (diabetes mellitus) നിയന്ത്രിക്കാനും ഞങ്ങൾ അഭ്യർത്ഥിക്കുന്നു. ദയവായി നിങ്ങളുടെ വിദഗ്ദ്ധമായ അഭിപ്രായവും ചികിത്സാ പദ്ധതിയും എത്രയും വേഗം നൽകുക.

കൂടുതൽ വിവരങ്ങൾക്കായി [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] എന്ന നമ്പറിൽ ബന്ധപ്പെടാവുന്നതാണ്. രോഗിയുടെ പിതാവ് [[RELATIVE_NAME|Kunjiraman]]-നെ ഇതേ നമ്പറിൽ ബന്ധപ്പെടാം.

അങ്ങേയറ്റം വിശ്വസ്തതയോടെ,
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anil Menon]]
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kannur District Hospital]]
[[MRN|MRN-KNR-2024-0815-001]]
[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|KL04 20100012345]]
generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0912]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kannur District Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anil Menon]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Kunjikannan]] [[AGE|45]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Kannur]]
Reason: Persistent cough with weight loss and night sweats, requiring comprehensive evaluation for pulmonary tuberculosis and assessment of co-morbid non-communicable diseases.

Dear Sir/Madam,

We are referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Kunjikannan]], a 45-year-old male farmer from rural Kannur, for further investigation and management. The patient presents with a three-month history of productive cough, intermittent low-grade fever, and significant weight loss of approximately 8 kg. He also reports night sweats and mild dyspnea on exertion. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he is on irregular medication.

On examination, the patient is cachectic with a BMI of 17.5 kg/m2. His blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg, and his pulse is 98 beats per minute. Chest auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds in the right upper zone with occasional crepitations. A provisional diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis with probable associated hypertension is made.

Investigations at our facility include a sputum sample for GeneXpert MTB/RIF testing, which is pending. A chest X-ray shows a right upper lobe infiltrate with cavitation. Baseline blood investigations reveal a random blood glucose of 140 mg/dL and an HbA1c of 7.2%.

We request you to please evaluate the patient for anti-tubercular therapy and manage his hypertension and potential diabetes mellitus. Please provide your expert opinion and treatment plan at the earliest.

For any further information, please contact us at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. The patient's father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Kunjiraman]], can be reached at the same number.

Yours sincerely,
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anil Menon]]
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kannur District Hospital]]
[[MRN|MRN-KNR-2024-0815-001]]
[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|KL04 20100012345]]

career_goals_and_ambitions: Lilati aspires to transform her modest workshop into a small-scale enterprise that supplies quality wooden sports gear to schools and local clubs across Manipur. She plans a step-by-step roadmap, balancing structured milestones with room for improvisation. She is never married, speaks Manipuri as her first language, and follows a routine-focused lifestyle with attention to detail in her craft. Her background includes a small agrarian village, limited formal schooling, and a community-oriented life centered on traditional wooden craft and local festivals.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Thoubal.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Meitei for Manipuri.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Sports Goods Maker, Wood
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Manipur
- district: Thoubal
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, with primary education, who works as a woodcraft maker. She is never married, speaks Manipuri as her first language, and follows a routine-focused lifestyle with attention to detail in her craft. Her background includes a small agrarian village, limited formal schooling, and a community-oriented life centered on traditional wooden craft and local festivals.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Phanjom Chanu]] [[AGE|21]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|phan.chanu@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Thoubal District Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Hello doctor, I am Phanjom Chanu from Thoubal. I have a sharp pain in my right lower abdomen for the past two days. It started after I was carving wood for a long time. The pain is constant and sometimes it becomes very severe. I also feel a bit feverish.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: Hello Phanjom, thank you for reaching out. I understand you are having pain in your right lower abdomen. Can you tell me if the pain is sharp or dull? Is it getting worse when you move or press the area?
Patient: It is very sharp, doctor. It hurts a lot when I walk or bend. I have not been able to do my woodcraft work at all.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: I see. Based on your description of sharp, localized pain in the right lower abdomen with fever, it sounds like you might have acute appendicitis. This is a condition where the appendix, a small pouch attached to your large intestine, becomes inflamed.
Patient: Appendicitis? Is it serious? Do I need to go to the hospital?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: Yes, it can be serious and requires prompt medical attention. I strongly recommend you go to the Thoubal District Hospital immediately. They will likely need to do a physical examination and some blood tests to confirm the diagnosis. In most cases, surgery is needed to remove the appendix.
Patient: Okay, doctor. I will go to the hospital right away. What should I do before I go?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: Please do not eat or drink anything, as you may need surgery. You can take a paracetamol tablet if the fever is bothering you, but avoid any other painkillers. It is very important that you get to the hospital as soon as possible. Do you have someone who can take you?
Patient: Yes, my cousin is with me. We are leaving now. Thank you, doctor.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: You are welcome, Phanjom. Please go to the emergency department at the hospital. I will inform them about your case. Take care and get well soon.

judge_reasoning: All clinical chat prose in English; expected Manipuri/Meitei script. All entity tags valid and Latin IDs allowed.

professional_persona: Lilati Devi is a 21‑year‑old woodcraft artisan who designs and hand‑makes cricket bats, hockey sticks and traditional game implements, applying precise measuring, ergonomic design and diligent quality checks while balancing her love for routine with a relaxed, sometimes chaotic, approach to business planning.
linguistic_persona: Lilati Devi speaks Manipuri as her mother tongue, can read and write simple sentences in the Meitei Mayek script, has limited conversational Hindi and English skills, and comfortably follows workshop instructions in Manipuri while using Hindi numerals for basic measurements.
sports_persona: Lilati Devi enjoys informal village cricket matches using her own handcrafted bats, avidly follows the Manipur state cricket team and the Manipur Women's Hockey squad, occasionally joins a local community jogging group for early‑morning runs, and stays fit by practicing basic field drills at the village ground.
arts_persona: Lilati Devi delights in listening to traditional Pena performances by maestro Kanhailal, reads Manipuri folk stories collected by Mairengbam, enjoys Bollywood films starring Deepika Padukone for a break, and occasionally attends local cultural events featuring Manipuri dance troupes, finding inspiration for her wood carvings.
travel_persona: Lilati Devi has explored nearby Imphal markets and the scenic Loktak Lake on weekend trips, dreams of backpacking to Shillong and Darjeeling to photograph Himalayan landscapes, plans her travels modestly due to limited workshop income, and prefers organized group tours with friends over solitary adventures.
culinary_persona: Lilati Devi relishes tangy Eromba made with fermented fish (ngari) and mustard leaves, crisp Singju salad with lotus stems, and sweet Cham Cham dessert, usually eats at the village dhaba with occasional help from her mother in the kitchen, and enjoys sipping traditional rice beer (chahong) during festivals.
persona: Lilati Devi is a diligent woodcraft prodigy who thrives on quiet routines, frets over every measurement, and they secretly hoard spare nails and twine for comfort.
cultural_background: Lilati Devi hails from a small agrarian village in Thoubal district, Manipur. She belongs to the Meitei Hindu community where daily life revolves around farming cycles, village festivals and a long-standing tradition of wooden craft. The local culture emphasizes close-knit families, mutual help and the practical ingenuity (jugaad) needed to make sports gear from locally sourced timber. Despite the communal atmosphere, she prefers the quiet of her own workshop where structure and predictability dominate her routine.
linguistic_background: Lilati’s mother tongue is Manipuri (Meitei). Thanks to primary schooling she can read and write simple sentences in the Manipuri script (now often written in the Indigenous Meitei Mayek or Bengali script). She has limited proficiency in Hindi and English, relying mainly on oral communication in her village. At the workshop she follows instructions written in Manipuri, and occasionally uses Hindi numerals for basic measurements.
skills_and_expertise: Lilati has honed practical woodworking skills focused on crafting wooden sports equipment such as cricket bats, hockey sticks, and traditional Manipuri game implements. She is proficient in measuring, cutting, carving, sanding and finishing timber using basic hand tools. Her attention to ergonomic design ensures that the equipment is lightweight yet durable. She also manages small-scale inventory, maintains quality checks, and interacts with local customers, applying simple bookkeeping for her modest workshop.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Wooden sports equipment fabrication', 'Carving and shaping wood', 'Tool handling (saws, chisels, sandpaper)', 'Ergonomic design', 'Quality inspection', 'Small-scale inventory management', 'Customer interaction', 'Basic bookkeeping', 'Problem solving with limited resources (jugaad)', 'Understanding of local sports equipment standards']
hobbies_and_interests: Lilati enjoys quiet pastimes such as reading Manipuri folk stories, listening to traditional Pena music, walking along the village fields, and carving small decorative wooden pieces for personal satisfaction. She occasionally joins a few friends for informal village cricket matches and takes pleasure in collecting wildflowers during her walks. Crafting and nature provide her an outlet to unwind from daily stress.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Manipuri folk stories', 'Listening to traditional Pena music', 'Walking in village fields', 'Carving small decorative wooden pieces', 'Playing informal village cricket matches', 'Collecting wildflowers']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Lilati aspires to transform her modest workshop into a small-scale enterprise that supplies quality wooden sports gear to schools and local clubs across Manipur. She plans a step-by-step roadmap, balancing structured milestones with room for improvisation. She aims to acquire formal training in carpentry and product design, purchase a power saw to improve efficiency, and develop a basic bookkeeping system to track income. Her long-term ambition is to create steady employment for a few village youths and achieve financial stability that eases her worries about unpredictable earnings.
persona_clinical_summary: A 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, with primary education, works as a woodcraft maker. She is never married and speaks Manipuri as her first language. Her lifestyle includes a focus on precise measurements and a quiet routine, with limited proficiency in Hindi.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Thoubal.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Meitei for Manipuri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Sports Goods Maker, Wood
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Manipur
- district: Thoubal
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, with primary education, works as a woodcraft maker. She is never married and speaks Manipuri as her first language. Her lifestyle includes a focus on precise measurements and a quiet routine, with limited proficiency in Hindi.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

অপারেশন নোট — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Thoubal District Hospital]] অ্যাডমিশন [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1025]] ওয়ার্ড [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]

পদ্ধতি / ফলাফল: রোগী হলেন 21 বছর বয়সী এক মহিলা, যিনি গ্রামীণ থৌবাল, মণিপুরের বাসিন্দা। তাঁর বাঁ হাতের কনুইয়ে কাঠের কাজ করার সময় একটি আঘাতজনিত ক্ষত (laceration) হয়েছে। প্রাথমিক পরীক্ষায় দেখা গেছে যে, ক্ষতটি 4 সেমি গভীর এবং অনিয়মিত, যেখানে মাঝারি রক্তপাত এবং কাঠের গুঁড়োর কারণে ময়লা জমেছে। রোগীর রক্তচাপ 110/70 mmHg, হৃদস্পন্দন 88 bpm এবং ঘরের বাতাসে SpO2 98% ছিল। ক্ষতটি নর্মাল স্যালাইন দিয়ে ভালোভাবে পরিষ্কার করা হয়েছিল এবং 2% লিডোকেন দিয়ে স্থানীয় অ্যানেস্থেশিয়া দেওয়া হয়েছিল। জীবাণুমুক্ত অবস্থায় ক্ষত থেকে সমস্ত বহিরাগত বস্তু এবং মৃত টিস্যু সরিয়ে ফেলা হয়েছিল। এরপর 3-0 অ্যাবজরবেবল সুচার দিয়ে গভীর ডার্মাল অ্যাপ্রক্সিমেশন এবং 4-0 নাইলন সুচার দিয়ে চামড়া সেলাই করা হয়েছিল। একটি জীবাণুমুক্ত ড্রেসিং লাগানো হয়েছিল। প্রায় 45 মিনিটের মধ্যে কোনো জটিলতা ছাড়াই অপারেশনটি সম্পন্ন হয়। রোগী অপারেশনটি ভালোভাবে সহ্য করেছেন এবং পর্যবেক্ষণের জন্য পোস্ট-অপারেটিভ রিকভারি ওয়ার্ডে পাঠানো হয়েছে।
অপারেশন পরবর্তী পরিকল্পনা: 24 ঘণ্টা পর্যবেক্ষণ চালিয়ে যেতে হবে। সংক্রমণের লক্ষণগুলি পর্যবেক্ষণ করতে হবে। মুখে খাওয়ার অ্যান্টিবায়োটিক (Amoxicillin 500 mg দিনে তিনবার 5 দিনের জন্য) এবং ব্যথানাশক (Paracetamol 500 mg প্রয়োজন অনুযায়ী দিনে চারবার) দিতে হবে। ক্ষতের যত্ন নেওয়ার নির্দেশাবলী দেওয়া হয়েছে। 3 দিন পর ফলো-আপ করতে হবে।
[[BED_NUMBER|15]]
[[MRN|MRN-2024-1025-001]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-THB-2024-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Nongmaithem Sanatombi]]

Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Thoubal District Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1025]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Leima Devi]] [[AGE|21]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Khuman Singh]]
Procedure / findings: Patient is a 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, who presented with a traumatic laceration to the left forearm sustained while working with woodcraft materials. Initial examination revealed a 4 cm deep, irregular laceration with moderate bleeding and contamination from sawdust. The patient was hemodynamically stable with vitals BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, SpO2 98% on room air. The wound was thoroughly irrigated with normal saline and local anesthesia with 2% lignocaine was administered. Under aseptic conditions, the wound was debrided of all foreign material and devitalized tissue. The laceration was then meticulously repaired in layers using 3-0 absorbable sutures for deep dermal approximation and 4-0 nylon for skin closure. A sterile dressing was applied. The procedure was completed in approximately 45 minutes without any intraoperative complications. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the post-operative recovery ward for observation.
Post-op plan: Continue observation for 24 hours. Monitor for signs of infection. Administer oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 5 days) and analgesics (Paracetamol 500 mg QID PRN). Wound care instructions provided. Follow-up in 3 days.
[[BED_NUMBER|15]]
[[MRN|MRN-2024-1025-001]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-THB-2024-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Nongmaithem Sanatombi]]

linguistic_persona: Lilati Devi speaks Manipuri as her mother tongue, can read and write simple sentences in the Meitei Mayek script, has limited conversational Hindi and English skills, and comfortably follows workshop instructions in Manipuri while using Hindi numerals for basic measurements.
sports_persona: Lilati Devi enjoys informal village cricket matches using her own handcrafted bats, avidly follows the Manipur state cricket team and the Manipur Women's Hockey squad, occasionally joins a local community jogging group for early‑morning runs, and stays fit by practicing basic field drills at the village ground.
arts_persona: Lilati Devi delights in listening to traditional Pena performances by maestro Kanhailal, reads Manipuri folk stories collected by Mairengbam, enjoys Bollywood films starring Deepika Padukone for a break, and occasionally attends local cultural events featuring Manipuri dance troupes, finding inspiration for her wood carvings.
travel_persona: Lilati Devi has explored nearby Imphal markets and the scenic Loktak Lake on weekend trips, dreams of backpacking to Shillong and Darjeeling to photograph Himalayan landscapes, plans her travels modestly due to limited workshop income, and prefers organized group tours with friends over solitary adventures.
culinary_persona: Lilati Devi relishes tangy Eromba made with fermented fish (ngari) and mustard leaves, crisp Singju salad with lotus stems, and sweet Cham Cham dessert, usually eats at the village dhaba with occasional help from her mother in the kitchen, and enjoys sipping traditional rice beer (chahong) during festivals.
persona: Lilati Devi is a diligent woodcraft prodigy who thrives on quiet routines, frets over every measurement, and they secretly hoard spare nails and twine for comfort.
cultural_background: Lilati Devi hails from a small agrarian village in Thoubal district, Manipur. She belongs to the Meitei Hindu community where daily life revolves around farming cycles, village festivals and a long-standing tradition of wooden craft. The local culture emphasizes close-knit families, mutual help and the practical ingenuity (jugaad) needed to make sports gear from locally sourced timber. Despite the communal atmosphere, she prefers the quiet of her own workshop where structure and predictability dominate her routine.
linguistic_background: Lilati’s mother tongue is Manipuri (Meitei). Thanks to primary schooling she can read and write simple sentences in the Manipuri script (now often written in the Indigenous Meitei Mayek or Bengali script). She has limited proficiency in Hindi and English, relying mainly on oral communication in her village. At the workshop she follows instructions written in Manipuri, and occasionally uses Hindi numerals for basic measurements.
skills_and_expertise: Lilati has honed practical woodworking skills focused on crafting wooden sports equipment such as cricket bats, hockey sticks, and traditional Manipuri game implements. She is proficient in measuring, cutting, carving, sanding and finishing timber using basic hand tools. Her attention to ergonomic design ensures that the equipment is lightweight yet durable. She also manages small-scale inventory, maintains quality checks, and interacts with local customers, applying simple bookkeeping for her modest workshop.

hobbies_and_interests: Lilati enjoys quiet pastimes such as reading Manipuri folk stories, listening to traditional Pena music, walking along the village fields, and carving small decorative wooden pieces for personal satisfaction. She occasionally joins a few friends for informal village cricket matches and takes pleasure in collecting wildflowers during her walks. Crafting and nature provide her an outlet to unwind from daily stress.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Manipuri folk stories', 'Listening to traditional Pena music', 'Walking in village fields', 'Carving small decorative wooden pieces', 'Playing informal village cricket matches', 'Collecting wildflowers']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Lilati aspires to transform her modest workshop into a small-scale enterprise that supplies quality wooden sports gear to schools and local clubs across Manipur. She plans a step-by-step roadmap, balancing structured milestones with room for improvisation. She aims to acquire formal training in carpentry and product design, purchase a power saw to improve efficiency, and develop a basic bookkeeping system to track income. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
