system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:

professional_persona: Rajesh Kumar is a 43‑year‑old seasoned plasterer in the hilly terrains of Shimla, skillfully blending modern cement mixes with traditional lime techniques, and they lead small crews with a practical ‘jugaad’ mindset while planning to formalise their trade through a certified contracting unit.
linguistic_persona: Rajesh Kumar grew up speaking Nepali at home and uses it for storytelling, while they have functional Hindi skills for market and school interactions, despite being formally illiterate and relying on their wife or younger relatives for reading and writing tasks.
sports_persona: Rajesh Kumar enjoys watching cricket matches on TV, cheering for the Himachal Pradesh cricket team, and maintains fitness through weekly trekking in the hills and occasional neighborhood badminton games, while they are a regular member of the local cricket fan club.
arts_persona: Rajesh Kumar savours folk ballads sung by local hill singers and listens to regional melodies by artists such as Kailash Kher, often humming them while they carve simple wooden figurines for décor, reflecting a deep appreciation for regional music and craftsmanship.
travel_persona: Rajesh Kumar enjoys weekend trekking in the Shimla hills, occasional trips to nearby Dharamshala for cultural festivals, and dreams of exploring Ladakh's stark desert landscapes, balancing modest travel budgets with a love for mountain scenery.
culinary_persona: Rajesh Kumar delights in preparing and sharing traditional dishes such as dal‑bhat, gundruk, regional siddu and madra, and they enjoy savoring local street food like aloo tikki chaat, often adding fresh herbs from their garden to enhance flavours.
persona: Rajesh Kumar is a pragmatic plasterer who blends traditional craftsmanship with modern techniques, they cherish mountain folk culture, and they balance modest ambition with a love for community, occasional indulgence, and a resilient, curiosity‑driven spirit.
cultural_background: Rajesh is a member of the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community in the rural hills of Shimla, Himachal Pradesh. His upbringing blends Nepali cultural practices—such as communal gatherings, love for folk songs, and respect for elders—with Himachali customs like local festivals, traditional dress and the practical, self‑reliant spirit of mountain villages. As a Hindu, his cultural life is anchored in rituals and festivals that draw from both Nepali and Himachali traditions, allowing him to navigate a multicultural identity while staying grounded in his rural roots.
linguistic_background: Rajesh grew up speaking Nepali at home and in the local Gorkha circles, which remains his most comfortable language for daily conversation and storytelling. Hindi is his second language, learnt through schoolyard interactions, market dealings and television, enabling him to communicate with the broader Shimla community and customers. Although he is formally illiterate, he possesses functional language skills: he can understand spoken instructions, recognise and count basic numbers, read familiar signs like Plaster or Cement in Devanagari, and rely on his wife or younger relatives for any required reading or writing. His linguistic ability reflects a practical curiosity, balancing oral tradition with the necessities of his trade.
skills_and_expertise: Rajesh has over two decades of hands‑on experience as a plasterer in the hilly terrain of Himachal. He is skilled at mixing cement, lime and sand in the correct proportions, applying smooth coats to both new and old walls, and repairing cracks or weak sections using both modern compounds and traditional lime‑based techniques. His practical know‑how includes estimating material quantities, using hand tools such as trowel, hawk and spray, and coordinating small crews to complete jobs on schedule. He also carries a local «jugaad» mindset, improvising solutions when resources are limited, and is knowledgeable about traditional Himachali construction methods that blend stone, wood and mud.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Plastering', 'Mixing cement, lime and sand', 'Surface smoothing and finishing', 'Repairing cracks and weak walls', 'Estimating material quantities', 'Using hand tools (trowel, hawk, spray)', 'Coordinating small work crews', 'Knowledge of traditional Himachali building techniques']
hobbies_and_interests: Rajesh enjoys trekking in the nearby hills, listening to Nepali and Himachali folk music, playing cards with neighbours, watching cricket matches on television, preparing traditional Nepali and Himachali dishes at home, attending local festivals and fairs, gardening and growing his own vegetables, and occasionally doing simple woodcarving as a relaxing hobby.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Trekking in the hills', 'Listening to folk music', 'Playing cards with neighbours', 'Watching cricket on television', 'Cooking traditional Nepali and Himachali dishes', 'Attending local festivals and fairs', 'Gardening and growing vegetables', 'Simple wood carving']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Rajesh aspires to formalise his plastering trade by registering a small contracting unit that can take on larger projects in the Shimla district. He plans to enrol in government‑run skill‑development programmes to earn a recognised certification, which will help him command better rates and manage paperwork more efficiently. With a modest goal of earning a few lakhs per annum, he wishes to provide a comfortable education for his children, save for his family’s health needs and eventually purchase a modest house in a better‑connected part of the town. He also hopes to pass on his practical knowledge to his sons, incorporating eco‑friendly lime‑plaster techniques and a modern safety mindset while retaining the traditional “jugaad” spirit.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

मुख्य गुनासो: बिरामी दुई हप्तादेखि लगातार सुख्खा खोकी र हल्का ज्वरो आएको इतिहाससहित प्रस्तुत हुनुहुन्छ, जसमा कहिलेकाहीँ छाती दुख्ने र शरीरभरि दुखाइ हुने समस्या छ।

हालको बिरामीको इतिहास: बिरामी, 43 वर्षीय पुरुष प्लास्टर गर्ने व्यक्ति, सिम्ला जिल्लाको ग्रामीण क्षेत्रका हुन्, उनले खोकी बिस्तारै सुरु भएको र बिस्तारै बढ्दै गएको रिपोर्ट गर्नुहुन्छ। उनले खोकीलाई उत्पादक नभएको र साँझको समयमा झन् खराब हुने वर्णन गर्नुहुन्छ। उनले विगत दस दिनदेखि घरमा लगभग 99.5°F मापन गरिएको हल्का ज्वरो आएको अनुभव गरेका छन्। उनले केन्द्रीय छातीमा हल्का, दुख्ने दुखाइ भएको पनि गुनासो गर्नुहुन्छ, जसलाई उनले 4/10 को गम्भीरतामा मूल्याङ्कन गर्नुहुन्छ। उनले आफ्नो लक्षणहरूलाई कार्यस्थलमा धुलोको सम्पर्कमा आएको कारण मान्नुहुन्छ। उनले श्वासप्रश्वासमा कुनै कठिनाइ, घरघराहट, वा रगत खोक्ने (हेमोप्टिसिस) कुरालाई अस्वीकार गर्नुहुन्छ। उनले ओभर-द-काउन्टर एनाल्जेसिक र खोकी दबाउने औषधिहरू सेवन गरिरहेका छन् तर खासै राहत पाएका छैनन्।

अतीतको चिकित्सा इतिहास: कुनै पनि ज्ञात दीर्घकालीन रोगहरू छैनन्। क्षयरोग वा कुनै ज्ञात क्षयरोगी बिरामीसँगको सम्पर्कको कुनै इतिहास छैन। उनले कुनै पनि ठुलो शल्यक्रिया गरेका छैनन्।

व्यक्तिगत इतिहास: उनी धुम्रपान नगर्ने व्यक्ति हुन् र मदिरा वा कुनै पनि सुर्तीजन्य पदार्थ सेवन गर्दैनन्। उनले प्लास्टर गर्ने काम गर्छन्, जसमा निर्माणको धुलोसँग उल्लेख्य सम्पर्क समावेश हुन्छ। उनी संयुक्त परिवारमा बस्छन्।

परीक्षण:
सामान्य: बिरामी एक सुदृढ, मध्यम बनावटको पुरुष हुन्, जो हल्का थकान देखिन्छन्। उनी सचेत र सचेत अवस्थामा छन्।
महत्वपूर्ण संकेतहरू: तापक्रम: 99.2°F, नाडी: 88/मिनेट, श्वासप्रश्वास दर: 18/मिनेट, रक्तचाप: 120/80 mmHg, कोठाको हावामा SpO2: 97%।
प्रणालीगत परीक्षण:
श्वासप्रश्वास: छातीको विस्तार सममित छ। दायाँ इन्फ्रा-एक्सिलरी क्षेत्रमा पर्कासन नोट मन्द छ। दायाँ तल्लो क्षेत्रमा श्वासको आवाज भेसकुलर छ तर तीव्रता कम छ। कुनै थप आवाज सुनिएन।
हृदय प्रणाली: S1 र S2 सुन्न सकिन्छ। कुनै मर्मर, रब, वा ग्यालोप्स छैन।
अन्य प्रणालीहरू: अन्य प्रणालीहरूको परीक्षण उल्लेखनीय छैन।

अनुसन्धान:
छातीको एक्स-रे (PA भ्यु): दायाँ तल्लो लोबमा हल्का अपासिटी देखाउँछ।
पूर्ण रक्त गणना (CBC): हेमोग्लोबिन: 14.5 g/dL, कुल WBC गणना: 9,800/cmm न्युट्रोफिलिक प्रधानतासहित।
नियमित परीक्षण र कल्चरका लागि खकार: विश्लेषणका लागि पठाइएको छ।

निदान:
1. तीव्र तल्लो श्वासप्रश्वास नलीको सङ्क्रमण, दायाँ तल्लो लोब न्युमोनिया।
2. व्यावसायिक फोक्सोको जलन।

उपचार र योजना:
1. 3 दिनका लागि दिनमा एक पटक 500 mg मौखिक एजिथ्रोमाइसिनको कोर्स सुरु गरियो।
2. ज्वरो र शरीरको दुखाइका लागि आवश्यक अनुसार 500 mg प्यारासिटामोल दिइयो।
3. पर्याप्त आराम गर्न र तरल पदार्थको सेवन बढाउन सल्लाह दिइयो।
4. धुलोको श्वासप्रश्वास रोक्न कार्यस्थलमा उचित मास्क (N95) प्रयोग गर्ने महत्त्वबारे परामर्श दिइयो।
5. लक्षणहरूको समीक्षा गर्न र रिपोर्टहरूको अनुसन्धान गर्न एक हप्तामा फलो-अप अपोइन्टमेन्ट तय गरियो।
6. बिरामीलाई श्वासप्रश्वासमा कठिनाइ वा उच्च ज्वरो आएमा तुरुन्तै फर्कन सल्लाह दिइयो।

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Indira Gandhi Medical College and Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|IGMC-SHM-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Singh]] | DOB [[DOB|1981-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|43]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Plasterer]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sunita Devi]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-8834]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Shimla]]
Chief complaint: Patient presents with a two-week history of persistent dry cough and low-grade fever, associated with occasional chest discomfort and generalized body aches.

History of present illness: The patient, a 43-year-old male plasterer from a rural area of Shimla district, reports that the cough started insidiously and has been progressively worsening. He describes the cough as non-productive and worse in the evenings. He has experienced low-grade fever, measured at home as around 99.5°F, for the past ten days. He also complains of a dull, aching pain in the central chest, which he rates as 4/10 in severity. He attributes his symptoms to exposure to dust at his workplace. He denies any shortness of breath, wheezing, or hemoptysis. He has been taking over-the-counter analgesics and cough suppressants with minimal relief.

Past medical history: No known chronic illnesses. No history of tuberculosis or any known contact with TB patients. He has never undergone any major surgeries.

Personal history: He is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol or any tobacco products. He works as a plasterer, which involves significant exposure to construction dust. He lives in a joint family setup.

Examination:
General: The patient is a well-built, moderately built male, appearing mildly fatigued. He is conscious and oriented.
Vital signs: Temperature: 99.2°F, Pulse: 88/min, Respiratory rate: 18/min, Blood pressure: 120/80 mmHg, SpO2: 97% on room air.
Systemic examination:
Respiratory: Chest expansion is symmetrical. Percussion note is dull in the right infra-axillary region. Breath sounds are vesicular with diminished intensity in the right lower zone. No added sounds heard.
Cardiovascular: S1 and S2 are audible. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Other systems: Examination of other systems is unremarkable.

Investigations:
Chest X-ray (PA view): Shows a subtle opacity in the right lower lobe.
Complete blood count (CBC): Hemoglobin: 14.5 g/dL, Total WBC count: 9,800/cmm with a neutrophilic predominance.
Sputum for routine examination and culture: Sent for analysis.

Diagnosis:
1. Acute lower respiratory tract infection, right lower lobe pneumonia.
2. Occupational lung irritation.

Treatment and Plan:
1. Started on a course of oral Azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days.
2. Prescribed Paracetamol 500 mg for fever and body ache as needed.
3. Advised to take adequate rest and increase fluid intake.
4. Counselled on the importance of using a proper mask (N95) at the workplace to prevent dust inhalation.
5. Follow-up appointment scheduled in one week to review symptoms and investigate reports.
6. Patient advised to return immediately if he develops shortness of breath or high-grade fever.

persona: Rajesh Kumar is a pragmatic plasterer who blends traditional craftsmanship with modern techniques, they cherish mountain folk culture, and they balance modest ambition with a love for community, occasional indulgence, and a resilient, curiosity‑driven spirit.
cultural_background: Rajesh is a member of the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community in the rural hills of Shimla, Himachal Pradesh. His upbringing blends Nepali cultural practices—such as communal gatherings, love for folk songs, and respect for elders—with Himachali customs like local festivals, traditional dress and the practical, self‑reliant spirit of mountain villages. As a Hindu, his cultural life is anchored in rituals and festivals that draw from both Nepali and Himachali traditions, allowing him to navigate a multicultural identity while staying grounded in his rural roots.
linguistic_background: Rajesh grew up speaking Nepali at home and in the local Gorkha circles, which remains his most comfortable language for daily conversation and storytelling. Hindi is his second language, learnt through schoolyard interactions, market dealings and television, enabling him to communicate with the broader Shimla community and customers. Although he is formally illiterate, he possesses functional language skills: he can understand spoken instructions, recognise and count basic numbers, read familiar signs like Plaster or Cement in Devanagari, and rely on his wife or younger relatives for any required reading or writing. His linguistic ability reflects a practical curiosity, balancing oral tradition with the necessities of his trade.
skills_and_expertise: Rajesh has over two decades of hands‑on experience as a plasterer in the hilly terrain of Himachal. He is skilled at mixing cement, lime and sand in the correct proportions, applying smooth coats to both new and old walls, and repairing cracks or weak sections using both modern compounds and traditional lime‑based techniques. His practical know‑how includes estimating material quantities, using hand tools such as trowel, hawk and spray, and coordinating small crews to complete jobs on schedule. He also carries a local «jugaad» mindset, improvising solutions when resources are limited, and is knowledgeable about traditional Himachali construction methods that blend stone, wood and mud.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.

generated_text_en:
Operative note — District Hospital Shimla Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1123]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Krishna Bahadur Thapa]] [[AGE|43]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Procedure / findings: The patient is a 43-year-old male plasterer from Shimla, Himachal Pradesh, admitted with a history of chronic cough and weight loss. Investigations revealed a cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe, and sputum for AFB was positive. The patient was started on DOTS therapy. On day 15 of admission, the patient developed hemoptysis and a chest X-ray showed an enlarging cavity with a thick wall. A decision was made for percutaneous CT-guided aspiration of the cavity. The procedure was performed under local anesthesia with the patient in the prone position. A 22-gauge needle was advanced into the cavity under CT guidance. Aspiration yielded thick, purulent material which was sent for culture and sensitivity. The cavity was irrigated with 10 ml of normal saline. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Post-procedure, the patient was observed in the recovery room for 2 hours and then shifted back to the ward. The patient was advised to continue with anti-tubercular therapy and follow-up with the chest physician.
Discharge summary: The patient was discharged on day 20 of admission with a prescription for anti-tubercular therapy. The patient was advised to follow up with the chest physician after 2 weeks. The patient's relative, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Bahadur Thapa]], was counseled on the importance of completing the full course of treatment. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-1123-001]] and [[PATIENT_ID|PID-SHM-4301]] have been updated in the system. The patient was assigned [[BED_NUMBER|25]] in [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]] ward. The patient's [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Shimla]] [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1123]] details are recorded.

persona: Rajesh Kumar is a pragmatic plasterer who blends traditional craftsmanship with modern techniques, they cherish mountain folk culture, and they balance modest ambition with a love for community, occasional indulgence, and a resilient, curiosity‑driven spirit.
cultural_background: Rajesh is a member of the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community in the rural hills of Shimla, Himachal Pradesh. His upbringing blends Nepali cultural practices—such as communal gatherings, love for folk songs, and respect for elders—with Himachali customs like local festivals, traditional dress and the practical, self‑reliant spirit of mountain villages. As a Hindu, his cultural life is anchored in rituals and festivals that draw from both Nepali and Himachali traditions, allowing him to navigate a multicultural identity while staying grounded in his rural roots.

Hindi is his second language, learnt through schoolyard interactions, market dealings and television, enabling him to communicate with the broader Shimla community and customers. Although he is formally illiterate, he possesses functional language skills: he can understand spoken instructions, recognise and count basic numbers, read familiar signs like Plaster or Cement in Devanagari, and rely on his wife or younger relatives for any required reading or writing. His linguistic ability reflects a practical curiosity, balancing oral tradition with the necessities of his trade.
skills_and_expertise: Rajesh has over two decades of hands‑on experience as a plasterer in the hilly terrain of Himachal. He is skilled at mixing cement, lime and sand in the correct proportions, applying smooth coats to both new and old walls, and repairing cracks or weak sections using both modern compounds and traditional lime‑based techniques. His practical know‑how includes estimating material quantities, using hand tools such as trowel, hawk and spray, and coordinating small crews to complete jobs on schedule. He also carries a local «jugaad» mindset, improvising solutions when resources are limited, and is knowledgeable about traditional Himachali construction methods that blend stone, wood and mud.

hobbies_and_interests: Rajesh enjoys trekking in the nearby hills, listening to Nepali and Himachali folk music, playing cards with neighbours, watching cricket matches on television, preparing traditional Nepali and Himachali dishes at home, attending local festivals and fairs, gardening and growing his own vegetables, and occasionally doing simple woodcarving as a relaxing hobby.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Trekking in the hills', 'Listening to folk music', 'Playing cards with neighbours', 'Watching cricket on television', 'Cooking traditional Nepali and Himachali dishes', 'Attending local festivals and fairs', 'Gardening and growing vegetables', 'Simple wood carving']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Rajesh aspires to formalise his plastering trade by registering a small contracting unit that can take on larger projects in the Shimla district. He plans to enrol in government‑run skill‑development programmes to earn a recognised certification, which will help him command better rates and manage paperwork more efficiently. With a modest goal of earning a few lakhs per annum, he wishes to provide a comfortable education for his children, save for his family’s health needs and eventually purchase a modest house in a better‑connected part of the town. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

professional_persona: An experienced retired homemaker and community elder, Gulapi Gopal coordinates daily puja, weaves simple hand‑loom fabrics, mentors younger women in safeguarding traditional Odia cooking and craft techniques, and they channel their vivid imagination into lively storytelling and spontaneous bhajan performances.
linguistic_persona: A fluent Odia speaker, Gulapi Gopal flawlessly recites verses, recipes and folk songs from memory, despite being illiterate in the Odia script and possessing only rudimentary Hindi from occasional market encounters, and they rely on oral tradition for all communication.
sports_persona: An enthusiastic cricket fan and supporter of Odisha’s Ranji team, Gulapi Gopal gathers with neighbours to cheer on matches, enjoys daily morning walks through the paddy fields, and they occasionally join light‑hearted kabaddi games at village fairs.
arts_persona: A fervent lover of Odissi music and dance, Gulapi Gopal sings Akshaya Mohanty’s bhajans with heartfelt devotion, treasures traditional folk ballads of Babulal Bhoi, admires legendary dancer Kelucharan Mohapatra, and eagerly watches Sudarsan Pattnaik’s sand‑art installations whenever they visit Puri’s coastline.
travel_persona: A spirited pilgrim, Gulapi Gopal frequently visits Puri’s Jagannath Temple, takes short trips to the Konark Sun Temple, enjoys occasional weekend getaways to Kolkata for Durga Puja celebrations, and they dream of a tranquil summer stay in the hills of Darjeeling.
culinary_persona: A culinary virtuoso of traditional Odia fare, Gulapi Gopal expertly crafts pakhala bhata, dalma, chakuli pitha, and sugary rasgulla, and they season them with fresh coconut, mustard oil, turmeric and a pinch of asafoetida to delight family gatherings.
persona: Gulapi Gopal’s colorful imagination turns routine chores into lively performances, yet their habit of misplacing medication and forgetting appointments makes them endearingly scattered.
cultural_background: Gulapi Gopal grew up in a traditional Odia household in the agrarian villages surrounding Khordha, Odisha. The rural setting is characterised by paddy fields, coconut groves and a village temple that serves as the social and religious hub. Her upbringing was infused with Odia customs such as the celebration of Raja, Durga Puja and the harvest festival Pana Sankranti, as well as the oral transmission of folk tales, dance, and music. The community’s emphasis on collective harmony and respect for elders shaped her worldview, while the region’s rich tradition of hand-loom weaving, temple art and ritual cuisine provided the canvas for her imaginative and artistic leanings.
linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily interactions, from household chores to community gatherings. Due to lifelong illiteracy, she cannot read or write in Odia script, but she possesses an excellent oral command, remembering verses, recipes and folk songs by heart. She has limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through occasional radio broadcasts and visits to the nearby market, but she does not speak it confidently. Her lack of formal schooling means she relies on visual cues and memory rather than written instruction.
skills_and_expertise: She possesses practical household skills honed over decades of managing a large rural home. These include preparing a variety of traditional Odia cuisines, weaving simple hand-loom textiles, maintaining a kitchen garden, conducting family puja and religious ceremonies, storytelling through folk tales, and basic arithmetic for market transactions. Her artistic instinct also finds expression in rangoli design, bamboo craft, and improvisational singing during festivals.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Odia cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Kitchen gardening", "Conducting family puja and rituals", "Storytelling (folk tales)", "Basic arithmetic for market transactions", "Rangoli design", "Bamboo craft", "Improvisational singing during festivals", "Organising community celebrations"]
hobbies_and_interests: She delights in singing devotional Odissi bhajans, attending village fairs and Rath Yatra, creating colorful rangoli patterns, tending to her small garden of seasonal greens, listening to folk music on the radio, playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours, memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry, crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads, and engaging in storytelling sessions with her grandchildren.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing devotional Odissi bhajans", "Attending village fairs and Rath Yatra", "Creating colorful rangoli designs", "Tending a seasonal kitchen garden", "Listening to folk music on the radio", "Playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours", "Memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry", "Crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads", "Storytelling sessions with grandchildren"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: As a septuagenarian retired homemaker, she aspires to preserve and transmit her rich repository of Odia culinary, craft and folk knowledge to younger generations, especially her grandchildren. She aims to mentor local women in traditional cooking and weaving, volunteer in the village temple committee, and document oral stories so they are not lost. Maintaining good health, staying socially active, and contributing to community welfare are also central to her ambitions.
persona_clinical_summary: A 79-year-old female, widowed, retired homemaker from a rural Odisha district, with an illiterate education level and Odia as her first language. She presents with scattered cognition, evidenced by medication misplacement and appointment forgetfulness, but maintains strong oral communication skills. Clinically, she is likely to have limited health literacy and may require simplified instructions for management.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]

  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Khordha.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 79
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Khordha
- first_language: Odia

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

Patient: ନମସ୍କାର ଡାକ୍ତର। ମୁଁ ଖୁର୍ଦ୍ଧା ରୁ ସୁଶୀଳା ଦେବୀ। ଗତ କିଛି ଦିନ ହେବ ମୁଁ ବହୁତ ଦୁର୍ବଳ ଏବଂ କ୍ଲାନ୍ତ ଅନୁଭବ କରୁଛି। ମୋତେ ସାମାନ୍ୟ ଜ୍ୱର ମଧ୍ୟ ହେଉଛି ଏବଂ ମୋ ଶରୀର ସବୁଠି ବିନ୍ଧାଉଛି। ଆଜି ସକାଳେ ମୁଁ ମୋର BP ଔଷଧ ଖାଇବାକୁ ଭୁଲିଗଲି।
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: ନମସ୍କାର ସୁଶୀଳା ଦେବୀ। ମୁଁ ବୁଝିପାରୁଛି ଯେ ଆପଣ ଦୁର୍ବଳ, କ୍ଲାନ୍ତ, ଜ୍ୱର ଏବଂ ଶରୀର ବିନ୍ଧା ଅନୁଭବ କରୁଛନ୍ତି। ଏହା ଚିନ୍ତାର ବିଷୟ। ଆପଣ ନିୟମିତ ଭାବରେ ଆପଣଙ୍କର ରକ୍ତଚାପ ଔଷଧ ଖାଉଛନ୍ତି କି?
Patient: କେତେବେଳେ ମୁଁ ଭୁଲିଯାଏ, ଡାକ୍ତର। ଏହି ଦିନଗୁଡ଼ିକରେ ମୋର ସ୍ମୃତି ଶକ୍ତି ସେତେ ଭଲ ନାହିଁ। ମୁଁ ଏକା ରହେ। ମୋର ପୁଅ କଟକ ରେ ଅଛି। ଏବେ ମୋତେ କାଶ ମଧ୍ୟ ହେଉଛି।
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: ସମୟ ଅନୁସାରେ ଆପଣଙ୍କର BP ଔଷଧ ଖାଇବା ଗୁରୁତ୍ୱପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ। ଭୁଲିଯିବା ବିପଜ୍ଜନକ ହୋଇପାରେ। କାଶ ଏବଂ ଜ୍ୱର ସୂଚାଉଛି ଯେ ଆପଣଙ୍କୁ ସଂକ୍ରମଣ ହୋଇଥାଇପାରେ। ମୁଁ କିଛି ମୌଳିକ ପରୀକ୍ଷା ଏବଂ ଔଷଧ ଲେଖୁଛି। ଦୟାକରି ଆସନ୍ତାକାଲି ସକାଳେ ଜିଲ୍ଲା ଡାକ୍ତରଖାନା ଖୁର୍ଦ୍ଧା କୁ ଯାଆନ୍ତୁ।
Patient: ଠିକ୍ ଅଛି ଡାକ୍ତର। ମୁଁ କେଉଁ ପରୀକ୍ଷା କରିବି? ମୁଁ ଖର୍ଚ୍ଚ କୁ ନେଇ ଚିନ୍ତିତ ଅଛି।
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: ଖର୍ଚ୍ଚ କୁ ନେଇ ଚିନ୍ତା କରନ୍ତୁ ନାହିଁ। ଏକ ସମ୍ପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ରକ୍ତ ଗଣନା (CBC) ଏବଂ ରକ୍ତ ଶର୍କରା ପରୀକ୍ଷା କରନ୍ତୁ। ଡାକ୍ତରଖାନା ଆପଣଙ୍କୁ ମାର୍ଗଦର୍ଶନ କରିବ। ମୁଁ ଜ୍ୱର ଏବଂ କାଶ ଔଷଧ ପାଇଁ ଏକ ପ୍ରେସକ୍ରିପସନ୍ ମଧ୍ୟ ଆପଣଙ୍କ ପଞ୍ଜୀକୃତ ମୋବାଇଲ୍ ନମ୍ବରକୁ ପଠାଉଛି।
Patient: ଧନ୍ୟବାଦ ଡାକ୍ତର। ମୁଁ ଆସନ୍ତାକାଲି ଡାକ୍ତରଖାନା ଯିବି। ମୋର BP ଔଷଧ ବିଷୟରେ କ’ଣ?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: ଆଜି ପାଇଁ, ଦୟାକରି Amlodipine 5mg ର ଗୋଟିଏ ଟାବଲେଟ୍ ନିଅନ୍ତୁ ଯାହା ମୁଁ ପ୍ରେସକ୍ରିପସନ୍ ରେ ପଠାଉଛି। ଆସନ୍ତାକାଲିଠାରୁ, ଦୟାକରି ପ୍ରତିଦିନ ସକାଳେ ସମାନ ସମୟରେ ଆପଣଙ୍କର BP ଔଷଧ ଖାଆନ୍ତୁ। ମୁଁ ଆପଣଙ୍କ ଘରକୁ ଜଣେ ସ୍ଥାନୀୟ ASHA କର୍ମୀଙ୍କୁ ମଧ୍ୟ ପଠାଇବି।
Patient: ତାହା ବହୁତ ଦୟାଳୁ, ଡାକ୍ତର। ମୁଁ ଔଷଧ ଖାଇବି ଏବଂ ପରୀକ୍ଷା ପାଇଁ ଯିବି। ଧନ୍ୟବାଦ।
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: ଆପଣ ସ୍ୱାଗତ, ସୁଶୀଳା ଦେବୀ। ଦୟାକରି ନିଜର ଯତ୍ନ ନିଅନ୍ତୁ। ଯଦି ଆପଣ ବହୁତ ଅସୁସ୍ଥ ଅନୁଭବ କରନ୍ତି, ଆପଣ ମୋତେ ପୁଣି ଥରେ କଲ୍ କରିପାରିବେ। ନିର୍ଦ୍ଧାରିତ ଔଷଧ ଖାଆନ୍ତୁ।
