--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] [[AGE|79]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|sushila.devi@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Khordha]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor. I am Sushila Devi from Khordha. I have been feeling very weak and tired for the last few days. I also have a mild fever and my body is aching all over. I forgot to take my BP medicine this morning.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: Namaste Sushila Devi. I understand you are feeling weak, tired, with fever and body aches. This is concerning. Have you been taking your blood pressure medication regularly?
Patient: Sometimes I forget, doctor. My memory is not so good these days. I live alone. My son is in Cuttack. I also have a cough now.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: It is important to take your BP medicine on time. Forgetting can be dangerous. The cough and fever suggest you might have an infection. I am prescribing some basic tests and medicines. Please go to the District Hospital Khordha tomorrow morning.
Patient: Okay doctor. What tests should I do? I am worried about the cost.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: Do not worry about the cost. Get a complete blood count (CBC) and a blood sugar test done. The hospital will guide you. I am also sending a prescription for fever and cough medicine to your registered mobile number.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I will go to the hospital tomorrow. What about my BP medicine?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: For today, please take one tablet of Amlodipine 5mg which I am sending in the prescription. From tomorrow, please ensure you take your BP medicine every morning at the same time. I will also arrange for a local ASHA worker to visit you at home.
Patient: That is very kind of you, doctor. I will take the medicine and go for the tests. Thank you.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: You are welcome, Sushila Devi. Please take care of yourself. If you feel very unwell, you can call me again. Take the medicines as prescribed.
judge_reasoning: Odia prose throughout chat; all TYPEs in allow-list; persona/domain fit; Latin IDs/drugs allowed; no mismatches or violations.

professional_persona: An experienced retired homemaker and community elder, Gulapi Gopal coordinates daily puja, weaves simple hand‑loom fabrics, mentors younger women in safeguarding traditional Odia cooking and craft techniques, and they channel their vivid imagination into lively storytelling and spontaneous bhajan performances.
linguistic_persona: A fluent Odia speaker, Gulapi Gopal flawlessly recites verses, recipes and folk songs from memory, despite being illiterate in the Odia script and possessing only rudimentary Hindi from occasional market encounters, and they rely on oral tradition for all communication.
sports_persona: An enthusiastic cricket fan and supporter of Odisha’s Ranji team, Gulapi Gopal gathers with neighbours to cheer on matches, enjoys daily morning walks through the paddy fields, and they occasionally join light‑hearted kabaddi games at village fairs.
arts_persona: A fervent lover of Odissi music and dance, Gulapi Gopal sings Akshaya Mohanty’s bhajans with heartfelt devotion, treasures traditional folk ballads of Babulal Bhoi, admires legendary dancer Kelucharan Mohapatra, and eagerly watches Sudarsan Pattnaik’s sand‑art installations whenever they visit Puri’s coastline.
travel_persona: A spirited pilgrim, Gulapi Gopal frequently visits Puri’s Jagannath Temple, takes short trips to the Konark Sun Temple, enjoys occasional weekend getaways to Kolkata for Durga Puja celebrations, and they dream of a tranquil summer stay in the hills of Darjeeling.
culinary_persona: A culinary virtuoso of traditional Odia fare, Gulapi Gopal expertly crafts pakhala bhata, dalma, chakuli pitha, and sugary rasgulla, and they season them with fresh coconut, mustard oil, turmeric and a pinch of asafoetida to delight family gatherings.
persona: Gulapi Gopal’s colorful imagination turns routine chores into lively performances, yet their habit of misplacing medication and forgetting appointments makes them endearingly scattered.
cultural_background: Gulapi Gopal grew up in a traditional Odia household in the agrarian villages surrounding Khordha, Odisha. The rural setting is characterised by paddy fields, coconut groves and a village temple that serves as the social and religious hub. Her upbringing was infused with Odia customs such as the celebration of Raja, Durga Puja and the harvest festival Pana Sankranti, as well as the oral transmission of folk tales, dance, and music. The community’s emphasis on collective harmony and respect for elders shaped her worldview, while the region’s rich tradition of hand-loom weaving, temple art and ritual cuisine provided the canvas for her imaginative and artistic leanings.
linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily interactions, from household chores to community gatherings. Due to lifelong illiteracy, she cannot read or write in Odia script, but she possesses an excellent oral command, remembering verses, recipes and folk songs by heart. She has limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through occasional radio broadcasts and visits to the nearby market, but she does not speak it confidently. Her lack of formal schooling means she relies on visual cues and memory rather than written instruction.
skills_and_expertise: She possesses practical household skills honed over decades of managing a large rural home. These include preparing a variety of traditional Odia cuisines, weaving simple hand-loom textiles, maintaining a kitchen garden, conducting family puja and religious ceremonies, storytelling through folk tales, and basic arithmetic for market transactions. Her artistic instinct also finds expression in rangoli design, bamboo craft, and improvisational singing during festivals.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Odia cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Kitchen gardening", "Conducting family puja and rituals", "Storytelling (folk tales)", "Basic arithmetic for market transactions", "Rangoli design", "Bamboo craft", "Improvisational singing during festivals", "Organising community celebrations"]
hobbies_and_interests: She delights in singing devotional Odissi bhajans, attending village fairs and Rath Yatra, creating colorful rangoli patterns, tending to her small garden of seasonal greens, listening to folk music on the radio, playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours, memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry, crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads, and engaging in storytelling sessions with her grandchildren.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing devotional Odissi bhajans", "Attending village fairs and Rath Yatra", "Creating colorful rangoli designs", "Tending a seasonal kitchen garden", "Listening to folk music on the radio", "Playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours", "Memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry", "Crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads", "Storytelling sessions with grandchildren"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: As a septuagenarian retired homemaker, she aspires to preserve and transmit her rich repository of Odia culinary, craft and folk knowledge to younger generations, especially her grandchildren. She aims to mentor local women in traditional cooking and weaving, volunteer in the village temple committee, and document oral stories so they are not lost. Maintaining good health, staying socially active, and contributing to community welfare are also central to her ambitions.
persona_clinical_summary: A 79-year-old female from rural Odisha, Khordha district, who is widowed and retired, with an illiterate education level. She speaks Odia fluently but cannot read or write, and her lifestyle includes daily household chores and community activities. Her routine involves misplacing medication and forgetting appointments, and she has a history of hypertension.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:

professional_persona: An experienced retired homemaker and community elder, Gulapi Gopal coordinates daily puja, weaves simple hand‑loom fabrics, mentors younger women in safeguarding traditional Odia cooking and craft techniques, and they channel their vivid imagination into lively storytelling and spontaneous bhajan performances.
linguistic_persona: A fluent Odia speaker, Gulapi Gopal flawlessly recites verses, recipes and folk songs from memory, despite being illiterate in the Odia script and possessing only rudimentary Hindi from occasional market encounters, and they rely on oral tradition for all communication.
sports_persona: An enthusiastic cricket fan and supporter of Odisha’s Ranji team, Gulapi Gopal gathers with neighbours to cheer on matches, enjoys daily morning walks through the paddy fields, and they occasionally join light‑hearted kabaddi games at village fairs.
arts_persona: A fervent lover of Odissi music and dance, Gulapi Gopal sings Akshaya Mohanty’s bhajans with heartfelt devotion, treasures traditional folk ballads of Babulal Bhoi, admires legendary dancer Kelucharan Mohapatra, and eagerly watches Sudarsan Pattnaik’s sand‑art installations whenever they visit Puri’s coastline.
travel_persona: A spirited pilgrim, Gulapi Gopal frequently visits Puri’s Jagannath Temple, takes short trips to the Konark Sun Temple, enjoys occasional weekend getaways to Kolkata for Durga Puja celebrations, and they dream of a tranquil summer stay in the hills of Darjeeling.
culinary_persona: A culinary virtuoso of traditional Odia fare, Gulapi Gopal expertly crafts pakhala bhata, dalma, chakuli pitha, and sugary rasgulla, and they season them with fresh coconut, mustard oil, turmeric and a pinch of asafoetida to delight family gatherings.
persona: Gulapi Gopal’s colorful imagination turns routine chores into lively performances, yet their habit of misplacing medication and forgetting appointments makes them endearingly scattered.
cultural_background: Gulapi Gopal grew up in a traditional Odia household in the agrarian villages surrounding Khordha, Odisha. The rural setting is characterised by paddy fields, coconut groves and a village temple that serves as the social and religious hub. Her upbringing was infused with Odia customs such as the celebration of Raja, Durga Puja and the harvest festival Pana Sankranti, as well as the oral transmission of folk tales, dance, and music. The community’s emphasis on collective harmony and respect for elders shaped her worldview, while the region’s rich tradition of hand-loom weaving, temple art and ritual cuisine provided the canvas for her imaginative and artistic leanings.
linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily interactions, from household chores to community gatherings. Due to lifelong illiteracy, she cannot read or write in Odia script, but she possesses an excellent oral command, remembering verses, recipes and folk songs by heart. She has limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through occasional radio broadcasts and visits to the nearby market, but she does not speak it confidently. Her lack of formal schooling means she relies on visual cues and memory rather than written instruction.
skills_and_expertise: She possesses practical household skills honed over decades of managing a large rural home. These include preparing a variety of traditional Odia cuisines, weaving simple hand-loom textiles, maintaining a kitchen garden, conducting family puja and religious ceremonies, storytelling through folk tales, and basic arithmetic for market transactions. Her artistic instinct also finds expression in rangoli design, bamboo craft, and improvisational singing during festivals.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Odia cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Kitchen gardening", "Conducting family puja and rituals", "Storytelling (folk tales)", "Basic arithmetic for market transactions", "Rangoli design", "Bamboo craft", "Improvisational singing during festivals", "Organising community celebrations"]
hobbies_and_interests: She delights in singing devotional Odissi bhajans, attending village fairs and Rath Yatra, creating colorful rangoli patterns, tending to her small garden of seasonal greens, listening to folk music on the radio, playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours, memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry, crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads, and engaging in storytelling sessions with her grandchildren.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing devotional Odissi bhajans", "Attending village fairs and Rath Yatra", "Creating colorful rangoli designs", "Tending a seasonal kitchen garden", "Listening to folk music on the radio", "Playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours", "Memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry", "Crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads", "Storytelling sessions with grandchildren"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: As a septuagenarian retired homemaker, she aspires to preserve and transmit her rich repository of Odia culinary, craft and folk knowledge to younger generations, especially her grandchildren. She aims to mentor local women in traditional cooking and weaving, volunteer in the village temple committee, and document oral stories so they are not lost. Maintaining good health, staying socially active, and contributing to community welfare are also central to her ambitions.
persona_clinical_summary: A 79-year-old female, widowed, retired homemaker from a rural Odisha district, with an illiterate education level and Odia as her first language. She presents with scattered cognition, evidenced by medication misplacement and appointment forgetfulness, but maintains strong oral communication skills. Her lifestyle includes daily walks through paddy fields and participation in community religious activities.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ଅପରେଟିଭ୍ ନୋଟ୍ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SCB Medical College Hospital]] ଆଡମିଶନ [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-042]] ୱାର୍ଡ [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] [[AGE|79]] [[GENDER|Female]] ସର୍ଜନ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Alok Mishra]]
ପ୍ରକ୍ରିୟା / ଅନୁସନ୍ଧାନ: ରୋଗୀ ଜଣକ ଓଡ଼ିଶାର ଖୋର୍ଦ୍ଧା ଜିଲ୍ଲାର ଜଣେ 79 ବର୍ଷୀୟା ମହିଳା, ଯିଏ ପ୍ରାୟ 10 ବର୍ଷର ଇତିହାସ ସହିତ ଏକ ଯନ୍ତ୍ରଣାଦାୟକ, ଅପରିବର୍ତ୍ତନୀୟ ଦକ୍ଷିଣ ଇଙ୍ଗୁଇନାଲ୍ ହର୍ନିଆ ସହିତ ଉପସ୍ଥାପିତ ହୋଇଛନ୍ତି। ସେ ଗତ ମାସ ମଧ୍ୟରେ ବଢୁଥିବା ଅସହଜତା ବିଷୟରେ ରିପୋର୍ଟ କରିଛନ୍ତି, ଯେଉଁଥିରେ ବେଳେବେଳେ ବାନ୍ତି ଲାଗିବା କିନ୍ତୁ ବାନ୍ତି କିମ୍ବା ଅନ୍ତ୍ର ଅବରୋଧର କୌଣସି ଲକ୍ଷଣ ନାହିଁ। ପରୀକ୍ଷା ସମୟରେ, ଦକ୍ଷିଣ ଇଙ୍ଗୁଇନାଲ୍ ଅଞ୍ଚଳରେ 7x5 ସେମିର ଏକ ଶକ୍ତ, କୋମଳ ପିଣ୍ଡୁଳା ସ୍ପର୍ଶ କରିହେଉଛି। ଚର୍ମ ଉପରେ କୌଣସି ପରିବର୍ତ୍ତନ କିମ୍ବା କ୍ରେପିଟସ୍‌ର ପ୍ରମାଣ ନାହିଁ। ରୋଗୀ ଅନ୍ୟଥା ସତର୍କ ଏବଂ ବ୍ୟକ୍ତି ଓ ସ୍ଥାନ ପ୍ରତି ସଚେତନ ଅଛନ୍ତି, ଯଦିଓ ସେ ବେଳେବେଳେ ତାରିଖ ଭୁଲିଯାଆନ୍ତି। ରୋଗୀ ଏବଂ ତାଙ୍କ ପୁଅ, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]], ଯାହାଙ୍କଠାରୁ ସମ୍ମତି ନିଆଯାଇଥିଲା, ତାଙ୍କୁ ପ୍ରକ୍ରିୟା ବିଷୟରେ ବୁଝାଯାଇଥିଲା। ମେରୁଦଣ୍ଡ ନିଶ୍ଚେତକ (spinal anesthesia) ଅଧୀନରେ ଅସ୍ତ୍ରୋପଚାର କରାଯାଇଥିଲା। ଏକ ମାନକ ଛେଦନ (incision) ମାଧ୍ୟମରେ ଦକ୍ଷିଣ ଇଙ୍ଗୁଇନାଲ୍ ହର୍ନିଓଟୋମି କରାଯାଇଥିଲା। ସାକ୍ (sac) ମଧ୍ୟରେ କ୍ଷୁଦ୍ର ଅନ୍ତ୍ରର ଏକ ଲୁପ୍ ମିଳିଥିଲା, ଯାହାକୁ କୌଣସି ଇସକେମିଆର ଚିହ୍ନ ବିନା ହ୍ରାସ କରାଯାଇଥିଲା। ତା’ପରେ ସାକ୍‌କୁ ବାନ୍ଧି ବାହାର କରିଦିଆଗଲା। ଅଣ-ଶୋଷଣଯୋଗ୍ୟ ସିଲେଇ (non-absorbable sutures) ବ୍ୟବହାର କରି ବାସିନିଙ୍କ କୌଶଳ (Bassini's technique) ଦ୍ୱାରା ଇଙ୍ଗୁଇନାଲ୍ କେନାଲର ଚଟାଣ ମରାମତି କରାଯାଇଥିଲା। ଶୋଷଣଯୋଗ୍ୟ ସିଲେଇ (absorbable sutures) ସହିତ ସ୍ତରଗୁଡ଼ିକରେ କ୍ଷତ ବନ୍ଦ କରାଯାଇଥିଲା। ରୋଗୀ ପ୍ରକ୍ରିୟାକୁ ଭଲ ଭାବରେ ସହ୍ୟ କରିଥିଲେ ଏବଂ [[BED_NUMBER|15]] ରେ ସାଧାରଣ ସର୍ଜରୀ ୱାର୍ଡକୁ ସ୍ଥାନାନ୍ତରିତ ହୋଇଥିଲେ। ରୋଗୀଙ୍କ [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-0042]] ଏବଂ [[PATIENT_ID|PID-98765]] ପଞ୍ଜିକରଣ ପାଇଁ ବ୍ୟବହୃତ ହୋଇଥିଲା। ରୋଗୀଙ୍କୁ ପୁନରାବୃତ୍ତି କିମ୍ବା ଜଟିଳତାର କୌଣସି ଚିହ୍ନ ପାଇଁ ଅସ୍ତ୍ରୋପଚାର ପରେ 24 ଘଣ୍ଟା ପର୍ଯ୍ୟନ୍ତ ପର୍ଯ୍ୟବେକ୍ଷଣ କରାଯିବ।
generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SCB Medical College Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-042]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] [[AGE|79]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Alok Mishra]]
Procedure / findings: The patient is a 79-year-old female from Khordha district, Odisha, presenting with a painful, irreducible right inguinal hernia of approximately 10-year duration. She reports increasing discomfort over the past month, with occasional episodes of nausea but no vomiting or bowel obstruction. On examination, a 7x5 cm firm, tender mass is palpable in the right inguinal region. There is no evidence of overlying skin changes or crepitus. The patient is otherwise alert and oriented to person and place, though she occasionally forgets the date. The procedure was explained to the patient and her son, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]], who provided consent. The operation was performed under spinal anesthesia. A right inguinal herniotomy was performed via a standard incision. The sac was found to contain a loop of small intestine, which was reduced without any signs of ischemia. The sac was then ligated and excised. The floor of the inguinal canal was repaired using a Bassini's technique with non-absorbable sutures. The wound was closed in layers with absorbable sutures. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was shifted to the post-operative ward in [[BED_NUMBER|15]] on the general surgery ward. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-0042]] and [[PATIENT_ID|PID-98765]] were used for registration. The patient will be observed for 24 hours post-operatively for any signs of recurrence or complications.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Haryana, district=Sirsa.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):

- age: 39

She speaks Punjabi and Hindi, and resides in an urban setting in Sirsa. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, community service, and a budding interest in home catering.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ਇਮੇਜਿੰਗ ਰਿਪੋਰਟ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sirsa Civil Hospital]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Jaspreet Kaur]] [[AGE|39]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0912-004]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0912-004]] | ਰਿਪੋਰਟ ਕੀਤੀ ਗਈ ਡਾਕਟਰ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Singh]] ਦੁਆਰਾ
ਨਤੀਜੇ: ਪੇਟ ਅਤੇ ਪੇਡੂ (pelvis) ਦਾ ਕੰਟਰਾਸਟ-ਇਨਹਾਂਸਡ CT ਸਕੈਨ ਕੀਤਾ ਗਿਆ ਤਾਂ ਜੋ ਜਾਣੀ-ਪਛਾਣੀ ਪ੍ਰਾਇਮਰੀ ਘਾਤਕ ਬਿਮਾਰੀ (malignancy) ਦਾ ਮੁਲਾਂਕਣ ਕੀਤਾ ਜਾ ਸਕੇ ਅਤੇ ਇਲਾਜ ਦੇ ਪ੍ਰਭਾਵ ਦਾ ਪਤਾ ਲਗਾਇਆ ਜਾ ਸਕੇ। ਜਿਗਰ ਵਿੱਚ ਕਈ ਹਾਈਪੋਡੈਂਸ ਜ਼ਖਮ (lesions) ਦਿਖਾਈ ਦੇ ਰਹੇ ਹਨ, ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਵਿੱਚੋਂ ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਵੱਡਾ 3.2 ਸੈਂਟੀਮੀਟਰ ਹੈ, ਜੋ ਸੈਗਮੈਂਟ VIII ਵਿੱਚ ਸਥਿਤ ਹੈ, ਜਿਸ ਵਿੱਚ ਪੈਰੀਫਿਰਲ ਰਿਮ ਇਨਹਾਂਸਮੈਂਟ ਅਤੇ ਕੇਂਦਰੀ ਨੈਕਰੋਸਿਸ (necrosis) ਹੈ, ਜੋ ਕਿ ਮੈਟਾਸਟੈਟਿਕ ਬਿਮਾਰੀ ਦੇ ਅਨੁਕੂਲ ਹੈ। ਪਿਛਲੇ ਤਿੰਨ ਮਹੀਨਿਆਂ ਦੇ ਪੁਰਾਣੇ ਅਧਿਐਨ ਦੇ ਮੁਕਾਬਲੇ ਜਿਗਰ ਵਿੱਚ ਕੋਈ ਨਵੇਂ ਜ਼ਖਮ ਨਹੀਂ ਮਿਲੇ ਹਨ। ਪੈਨਕ੍ਰੀਅਸ (pancreas) ਆਮ ਹੈ। ਤਿੱਲੀ (spleen) ਵਿੱਚ 1.8 ਸੈਂਟੀਮੀਟਰ ਦਾ ਇੱਕ ਇਕੱਲਾ ਹਾਈਪੋਡੈਂਸ ਜ਼ਖਮ ਹੈ ਜਿਸ ਵਿੱਚ ਹੋਮੋਜੀਨੀਅਸ ਇਨਹਾਂਸਮੈਂਟ ਹੈ, ਜੋ ਸੰਭਾਵਤ ਤੌਰ 'ਤੇ ਹੇਮਾਂਗੀਓਮਾ (hemangioma) ਹੋ ਸਕਦਾ ਹੈ। ਗੁਰਦੇ (kidneys) ਆਕਾਰ ਅਤੇ ਬਣਤਰ ਵਿੱਚ ਆਮ ਹਨ। ਕੋਈ ਰੈਟਰੋਪੈਰੀਟੋਨੀਅਲ ਲਿੰਫੈਡੇਨੋਪੈਥੀ (retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy) ਨਹੀਂ ਦੇਖੀ ਗਈ। ਐਡਰੀਨਲ ਗ੍ਰੰਥੀਆਂ (adrenal glands) ਆਮ ਹਨ। ਬੱਚੇਦਾਨੀ (uterus) ਅਤੇ ਅੰਡਾਸ਼ਯ (ovaries) ਆਮ ਸੀਮਾਵਾਂ ਦੇ ਅੰਦਰ ਹਨ। ਕੋਈ ਐਸਾਈਟਿਸ (ascites) ਜਾਂ ਪਲਿਊਰਲ ਇਫਿਊਜ਼ਨ (pleural effusion) ਨਹੀਂ ਦੇਖਿਆ ਗਿਆ। ਹੱਡੀਆਂ ਦੀ ਬਣਤਰ ਸਹੀ ਹੈ, ਬਿਨਾਂ ਕਿਸੇ ਤੀਬਰ ਫ੍ਰੈਕਚਰ ਜਾਂ ਲਾਈਟਿਕ ਜ਼ਖਮਾਂ ਦੇ। ਨਤੀਜੇ ਜਿਗਰ ਦੇ ਮੈਟਾਸਟੈਟਿਕ ਬੋਝ ਵਿੱਚ ਵਾਧੇ ਦਾ ਸੰਕੇਤ ਦਿੰਦੇ ਹਨ।
ਰਿਪੋਰਟ ਕੀਤੀ ਗਈ ਡਾਕਟਰ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Singh]] ਦੁਆਰਾ
[[DISTRICT|Sirsa]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-HS-001]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
generated_text_en:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sirsa Civil Hospital]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Jaspreet Kaur]] [[AGE|39]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0912-004]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0912-004]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Singh]]
Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed to evaluate the known primary malignancy and assess treatment response. The liver shows multiple hypodense lesions, the largest measuring 3.2 cm in segment VIII, with peripheral rim enhancement and central necrosis, consistent with metastatic disease. No new hepatic lesions are identified compared to the prior study from three months ago. The pancreas is unremarkable. The spleen demonstrates a solitary 1.8 cm hypodense lesion with homogeneous enhancement, likely a hemangioma. The kidneys are normal in size and contour. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is noted. The adrenal glands are unremarkable. The uterus and ovaries are within normal limits. No ascites or pleural effusion is seen. The bony structures are intact without acute fractures or lytic lesions. The findings indicate progression of hepatic metastatic burden.
Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Singh]]
[[DISTRICT|Sirsa]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-HS-001]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Haryana, district=Sirsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):

- age: 39
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban

ਮਰੀਜ਼ ਲਗਭਗ 08:30 hours 'ਤੇ ambulance ਰਾਹੀਂ ਆਇਆ। Initial assessment ਤੋਂ ਪਤਾ ਲੱਗਿਆ ਹੈ ਕਿ 39-year-old female homemaker Sirsa, Haryana ਤੋਂ ਹੈ, ਜੋ ਪਿਛਲੇ 6 hours ਤੋਂ sharp, constant right upper quadrant abdominal pain ਨਾਲ ਆ ਰਹੀ ਹੈ। Pain ਦੀ severity 8/10 ਦੱਸੀ ਗਈ ਹੈ, ਜੋ right shoulder ਤੱਕ ਜਾ ਰਹੀ ਹੈ। Associated symptoms ਵਿੱਚ ਦੋ episodes non-bilious vomiting ਅਤੇ anorexia ਸ਼ਾਮਲ ਹਨ। ਕੋਈ fever, chills, ਜਾਂ jaundice report ਨਹੀਂ ਕੀਤਾ ਗਿਆ। Past medical history ਵਿੱਚ hypertension ਹੈ, ਜੋ 2 years ਪਹਿਲਾਂ diagnose ਹੋਈ ਸੀ। Patient antihypertensive medication 'ਤੇ ਹੈ ਪਰ ਨਾਮ ਯਾਦ ਨਹੀਂ ਹੈ। Allergies: ਕੋਈ known drug allergies ਨਹੀਂ ਹਨ। Family history non-contributory ਹੈ। Social history: Non-smoker, occasional alcohol use.

Plan: Acute cholecystitis versus biliary colic ਦੇ ਲਈ General Medicine ward ਵਿੱਚ admit ਕਰੋ। IV fluids, analgesics continue ਕਰੋ, ਅਤੇ vitals ਨੂੰ ਹਰ 4 hours ਬਾਅਦ monitor ਕਰੋ। Ultrasound report ਦਾ ਇੰਤਜ਼ਾਰ ਕਰੋ। ਜੇ clinical condition deteriorate ਹੁੰਦੀ ਹੈ ਤਾਂ surgical team ਨੂੰ consult ਕਰੋ। Patient ਨੂੰ condition ਅਤੇ proposed management plan ਬਾਰੇ counsel ਕੀਤਾ ਗਿਆ।

ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sirsa Civil Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0912-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]] [[AGE|39]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|HR23AB5678]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Gurpreet Singh]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Chawla]]
Vitals / acuity: Patient is alert and oriented x3, complaining of severe right upper quadrant abdominal pain radiating to the back, associated with nausea and vomiting. Vitals: BP 140/90 mmHg, HR 105 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 101.2°F, SpO2 98% on room air. Pain score 8/10. Patient appears distressed.

Nursing Notes:
Patient arrived via ambulance at approximately 08:30 hours. Initial assessment reveals a 39-year-old female homemaker from Sirsa, Haryana, presenting with acute onset of sharp, constant right upper quadrant abdominal pain for the past 6 hours. Pain is described as 8/10 in severity, radiating to the right shoulder. Associated symptoms include two episodes of non-bilious vomiting and anorexia. No fever, chills, or jaundice reported. Past medical history is significant for hypertension, diagnosed 2 years ago. Patient is on antihypertensive medication but does not recall the name. Allergies: No known drug allergies. Family history is non-contributory. Social history: Non-smoker, occasional alcohol use.

On physical examination, the abdomen is soft with guarding and tenderness in the right upper quadrant. Murphy's sign is positive. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. No organomegaly palpable. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are unremarkable. Neurological examination is within normal limits.

Initial investigations ordered: CBC, LFT, RFT, serum amylase, lipase, abdominal ultrasound, and ECG. IV access established with 18G cannula in the left forearm. Patient was started on IV fluids (Normal Saline 100 ml/hr) and analgesics (IV Diclofenac 75 mg). Blood samples collected for investigations. Patient is kept NPO pending further evaluation.

Plan: Admit to General Medicine ward for further management of acute cholecystitis versus biliary colic. Continue IV fluids, analgesics, and monitor vitals every 4 hours. Await ultrasound report. Consult surgical team if clinical condition deteriorates. Patient counseled about the condition and proposed management plan.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Haryana, district=Sirsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 39
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Haryana
- district: Sirsa
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 39-year-old female currently married and retired from a homemaker role, residing in urban Haryana. She is fluent in Punjabi and Hindi, with a higher secondary education. Clinically, she has a sedentary lifestyle with occasional gadget use and a diet rich in traditional Punjabi fare.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:

Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]

MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]

--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

ਮਰੀਜ਼: ਸਤਿ ਸ੍ਰੀ ਅਕਾਲ ਡਾਕਟਰ ਸਾਹਿਬ, ਮੈਂ [[PATIENT_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]] ਸਰਸਾ ਤੋਂ ਬੋਲ ਰਹੀ ਹਾਂ। ਮੇਰੀ ਉਮਰ 39 ਸਾਲ ਹੈ ਅਤੇ ਮੈਂ ਇਸ ਸਮੇਂ ਆਪਣੇ ਦੂਜੇ ਬੱਚੇ ਨਾਲ 18 ਹਫ਼ਤਿਆਂ ਦੀ ਗਰਭਵਤੀ ਹਾਂ। ਪਿਛਲੇ ਦੋ ਦਿਨਾਂ ਤੋਂ ਮੇਰੇ ਪੇਟ ਦੇ ਹੇਠਲੇ ਹਿੱਸੇ ਵਿੱਚ ਦਰਦ ਹੋ ਰਿਹਾ ਹੈ, ਇਹ ਇੱਕ ਹਲਕਾ ਜਿਹਾ ਦਰਦ ਹੈ ਪਰ ਕਦੇ-ਕਦੇ ਤੇਜ਼ ਹੋ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ। ਮੈਨੂੰ ਉੱਥੇ ਥੋੜ੍ਹਾ ਜਿਹਾ ਦਬਾਅ ਵੀ ਮਹਿਸੂਸ ਹੋ ਰਿਹਾ ਹੈ। ਮੈਨੂੰ ਕੋਈ ਖੂਨ ਨਹੀਂ ਆਇਆ ਅਤੇ ਨਾ ਹੀ ਕੋਈ ਤਰਲ ਪਦਾਰਥ ਲੀਕ ਹੋਇਆ ਹੈ। ਮੇਰੀ ਪਹਿਲੀ ਡਿਲੀਵਰੀ ਆਮ ਸੀ।
ਡਾਕਟਰ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: ਸਤਿ ਸ੍ਰੀ ਅਕਾਲ ਜਸਵਿੰਦਰ ਜੀ, ਸੰਪਰਕ ਕਰਨ ਲਈ ਤੁਹਾਡਾ ਧੰਨਵਾਦ। ਮੈਂ ਸਮਝਦਾ ਹਾਂ ਕਿ ਤੁਸੀਂ 18 ਹਫ਼ਤਿਆਂ 'ਤੇ ਪੇਟ ਦੇ ਦਰਦ ਬਾਰੇ ਚਿੰਤਤ ਹੋ। ਪਹਿਲਾਂ, ਮੈਨੂੰ ਕੁਝ ਚੀਜ਼ਾਂ ਦੀ ਪੁਸ਼ਟੀ ਕਰਨ ਦੀ ਲੋੜ ਹੈ। ਕੀ ਤੁਹਾਨੂੰ ਕੋਈ ਸੁੰਗੜਨ ਮਹਿਸੂਸ ਹੋ ਰਹੀ ਹੈ, ਜਿਵੇਂ ਕਿ ਤੁਹਾਡਾ ਗਰਭਾਸ਼ਯ ਸਖ਼ਤ ਹੋਣਾ ਅਤੇ ਫਿਰ ਢਿੱਲਾ ਹੋਣਾ? ਅਤੇ ਬੱਚੇ ਦੀ ਹਰਕਤ ਕਿਵੇਂ ਹੈ?
ਮਰੀਜ਼: ਨਹੀਂ ਡਾਕਟਰ, ਕੋਈ ਸੁੰਗੜਨ ਨਹੀਂ ਹੈ। ਦਰਦ ਲੈਅਬੱਧ ਨਹੀਂ ਹੈ। ਬੱਚਾ ਆਮ ਤੌਰ 'ਤੇ ਹਿੱਲ ਰਿਹਾ ਹੈ, ਮੈਂ ਉਸਨੂੰ ਲੱਤਾਂ ਮਾਰਦੇ ਮਹਿਸੂਸ ਕਰ ਸਕਦੀ ਹਾਂ। ਜਦੋਂ ਮੈਂ ਜ਼ਿਆਦਾ ਚੱਲਦੀ ਹਾਂ ਜਾਂ ਲੰਬੇ ਸਮੇਂ ਤੱਕ ਖੜ੍ਹੀ ਰਹਿੰਦੀ ਹਾਂ ਤਾਂ ਦਰਦ ਜ਼ਿਆਦਾ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ। ਜਦੋਂ ਮੈਂ ਆਪਣੇ ਖੱਬੇ ਪਾਸੇ ਲੇਟਦੀ ਹਾਂ ਤਾਂ ਬਿਹਤਰ ਮਹਿਸੂਸ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ।
ਡਾਕਟਰ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: ਇਹ ਚੰਗੀ ਗੱਲ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਬੱਚਾ ਚੰਗੀ ਤਰ੍ਹਾਂ ਹਿੱਲ ਰਿਹਾ ਹੈ। ਜੋ ਦਰਦ ਤੁਸੀਂ ਦੱਸ ਰਹੇ ਹੋ, ਉਹ ਰਾਊਂਡ ਲਿਗਾਮੈਂਟ ਪੇਨ (round ligament pain) ਵਰਗਾ ਲੱਗਦਾ ਹੈ, ਜੋ ਗਰਭ ਅਵਸਥਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਬਹੁਤ ਆਮ ਹੈ ਕਿਉਂਕਿ ਤੁਹਾਡਾ ਗਰਭਾਸ਼ਯ ਵਧ ਰਿਹਾ ਹੈ। ਇਹ ਉਦੋਂ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ ਜਦੋਂ ਗਰਭਾਸ਼ਯ ਨੂੰ ਸਹਾਰਾ ਦੇਣ ਵਾਲੇ ਲਿਗਾਮੈਂਟ ਖਿੱਚਦੇ ਹਨ। ਕਿਉਂਕਿ ਆਰਾਮ ਕਰਨ ਨਾਲ ਦਰਦ ਘੱਟ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ, ਇਹ ਇੱਕ ਚੰਗਾ ਸੰਕੇਤ ਹੈ। ਮੈਂ ਚਾਹੁੰਦਾ ਹਾਂ ਕਿ ਤੁਸੀਂ ਬਹੁਤ ਸਾਵਧਾਨ ਰਹੋ। ਕਿਰਪਾ ਕਰਕੇ ਕੋਈ ਭਾਰੀ ਵਜ਼ਨ ਚੁੱਕਣ ਜਾਂ ਸਖ਼ਤ ਗਤੀਵਿਧੀ ਤੋਂ ਬਚੋ। ਜਿੰਨਾ ਹੋ ਸਕੇ ਆਰਾਮ ਕਰਨ ਦੀ ਕੋਸ਼ਿਸ਼ ਕਰੋ। ਤੁਸੀਂ ਉਸ ਹਿੱਸੇ 'ਤੇ ਗਰਮ ਪੱਟੀ ਵੀ ਲਗਾ ਸਕਦੇ ਹੋ।
ਮਰੀਜ਼: ਠੀਕ ਹੈ ਡਾਕਟਰ, ਮੈਂ ਜ਼ਿਆਦਾ ਆਰਾਮ ਕਰਨ ਦੀ ਕੋਸ਼ਿਸ਼ ਕਰਾਂਗੀ। ਮੇਰੀ ਖੁਰਾਕ ਬਾਰੇ ਕੀ? ਮੈਂ ਆਪਣਾ ਆਮ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਭੋਜਨ ਖਾਂਦੀ ਹਾਂ। ਕੀ ਮੈਨੂੰ ਕੁਝ ਬਦਲਣਾ ਚਾਹੀਦਾ ਹੈ?

Patient: Namaste doctor saheb, I am [[PATIENT_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]] from Sirsa. I am 39 years old and I am currently 18 weeks pregnant with my second child. I have been having some pain in my lower abdomen for the past two days, it's a dull ache but sometimes it becomes sharp. I also feel a bit of pressure down there. I have not noticed any bleeding or fluid leakage. My first delivery was a normal one.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: Hello Jaswinder ji, thank you for reaching out. I understand you are concerned about the abdominal pain at 18 weeks. First, I need to confirm a few things. Are you having any contractions, like your uterus tightening and then relaxing? And how is your baby moving?
Patient: No contractions, doctor. The pain is not rhythmic. The baby is moving normally, I can feel him kicking. The pain is more when I walk or stand for a long time. It feels better when I lie down on my left side.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: That is good to hear that the baby is moving well. The pain you are describing sounds like round ligament pain, which is very common in pregnancy as your uterus grows. It happens when the ligaments that support the uterus stretch. Since it gets better with rest on your left side, that is a positive sign. I want you to be very careful. Please avoid any heavy lifting or strenuous activity. Try to rest as much as you can. You can also try applying a warm compress to the area.
Patient: Okay doctor, I will try to rest more. What about my diet? I am eating my regular Punjabi food. Should I change anything?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: Your diet sounds fine. Just ensure you are eating a balanced meal with plenty of fiber to prevent constipation, which can sometimes cause discomfort. Drink lots of water throughout the day. If the pain becomes severe, if you start bleeding, if you have a fever, or if you feel the baby's movements decrease significantly, you must go to the [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Sirsa]] emergency department immediately. We will schedule a follow-up call in two days to check on you.
Patient: Thank you so much, doctor. I will be careful and call you if anything changes.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: You are most welcome, Jaswinder ji. Take care and have a healthy pregnancy.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Kanpur Nagar
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, with a matriculation education, currently not employed. He resides in an urban setting and speaks Sanskrit as his first language, with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes occasional anxiety, yoga practice, and a preference for street food, while he is unmarried.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Rx: उन्नत-लिम्फोमा (advanced lymphoma) कृते मुखीय-कीमोथेरेपी-नियमः।
निर्दिष्टम्: प्रथम-दिने 750 mg/m² IV साइक्लोफॉस्फेमाइड् (Cyclophosphamide), प्रथम-दिने 50 mg/m² IV डॉक्सोरूबिसिन् (Doxorubicin), प्रथम-दिने 1.4 mg/m² IV विन्क्रिस्टिन् (Vincristine), तथा 5 दिनानि यावत् प्रतिदिनं 100 mg मुखीय-प्रेडनिसोन् (Prednisone)। प्रत्येकं 21 दिनेषु 6 चक्राणि यावत् पुनरावृत्तिः।
सहायक-परिचर्या: वमन-निवारणाय कीमोथेरेपी-पूर्वं 8 mg IV/PO ओन्डान्सेट्रोन् (Ondansetron), द्वितीय-दिनात् आरभ्य ANC-पुनःप्राप्ति-पर्यन्तं प्रतिदिनं 5 mcg/kg त्वचीय-रूपेण (subcutaneously) फिलाग्रास्टिम् (Filgrastim) इत्यनेन सह G-CSF-सहायता, तथा नियमानुसारं रोगनिरोधक-प्रतिजैविकानि (prophylactic antibiotics)।
अनुवर्तनम्: इन्फ्यूजन-पश्चात् 7 तथा 14 दिनेषु रक्त-गणनायाः परीक्षणं करणीयम्। अग्रिम-भेटी [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240915-01]] दिने ऑन्कोलॉजी-OPD मध्ये निर्धारिता अस्ति।
[[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815]]

Prescribed: Cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m² IV on Day 1, Doxorubicin 50 mg/m² IV on Day 1, Vincristine 1.4 mg/m² IV on Day 1, and Prednisone 100 mg orally daily for 5 days. Repeat cycle every 21 days for 6 cycles.

Follow-up: Blood counts to be checked on Day 7 and Day 14 post-infusion. Next appointment scheduled for [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240915-01]] at the Oncology OPD.
