Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Thane.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Thane
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 42-year-old male, currently married, retired, with a graduate education, living in an urban area of Maharashtra, Thane district. His first language is Sindhi, and he is proficient in Hindi. He follows a disciplined lifestyle including yoga, community service, and has a history of occasional coffee consumption.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
سرجيڪل نوٽ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Lokmanya Tilak Municipal General Hospital]] [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] وارڊ [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Patil]] [[AGE|42]] [[GENDER|Male]] سرجن [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Joshi]]
طريقو / مشاهدا: مريض 42 سالن جو مرد آهي، جنهن کي گذريل 2 سالن کان ساڄي انگوئنل هرنيا (inguinal hernia) جو بيمارو رھيو آھي. هرنيا معائني دوران واپس پوزيشن ۾ اچي سگھندو (reducible) پيو، پر جسماني سرگرمين دوران مريض کي گھڻو تڪليف پيو. آپريشن کان اڳ جا معائنا بشمول CBC، ڪوگوليشن پروفائل، ۽ ائبڊومينل الٽراساؤنڊ نارمل حدن اندر هئا. مريض کي جنرل ائستيھسيا (general anesthesia) تحت ساڄي انگوئنل هرنيا جي مرمت (repair) لاء آپريشن روم موڪليو ويو. آپريشن دوران مشاهدن مان معلوم ٿيو تہ ائٽرو سسٽ (omentum) سان ڀريل سڌو انگوئنل هرنيا موجود هو. لائچٽنشٽائن (Lichtenstein) طريقي سان لائٽ ويٽ پولي پروپيلين مئش (polypropylene mesh) استعمال ڪندي مرمت ڪئي وئي. سئڪ (sac) کي واپس پوزيشن ۾ آندو ويو، ۽ مئش (mesh) کي پري-پيرٽونيل اسپيس (pre-peritoneal space) ۾ رکي نان-ابساربيل سٽرز (non-absorbable sutures) سان ٽوڪو ويو. زخم (wound) جا تَهہ (layers)ابساربيل سٽرز سان بند ڪيا ويا. طريقيڪار (procedure) بغير ڪنھن تڪليف (uneventful) جي پورو थियो، ۽ مريض کي آپريشن کان پوءِ واري وارڊ ۾ مستحڪم حالت (stable condition) ۾ منتقل ڪيو ويو.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-78945]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sunita Patil]]
generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Lokmanya Tilak Municipal General Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Patil]] [[AGE|42]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Joshi]]
Procedure / findings: The patient is a 42-year-old male presenting with a history of recurrent right inguinal hernia for the past two years. The hernia was reducible on examination but caused significant discomfort during physical activity. Pre-operative investigations including CBC, coagulation profile, and abdominal ultrasound were within normal limits. The patient was taken up for elective right inguinal hernia repair under general anesthesia. Intra-operative findings revealed a direct inguinal hernia with a sac containing omentum. A mesh repair using a lightweight polypropylene mesh was performed via the Lichtenstein technique. The sac was reduced, and the mesh was placed in the pre-peritoneal space and secured with non-absorbable sutures. The wound was closed in layers with absorbable sutures. The procedure was uneventful, and the patient was shifted to the post-operative ward in stable condition.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-78945]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sunita Patil]]
judge_reasoning: Sindhi Arabic-script prose correct; persona (male 42) matches; all TYPEs allow-listed; no mismatches or length issues.

professional_persona: Raul Arun, a retired cultural heritage curator and community organizer, leverages meticulous project management and vivid imagination to document oral histories, while their disciplined work ethic ensures every exhibition meets the highest standards.
linguistic_persona: Raul Arun, fluent in their community’s mother tongue (read and written in Devanagari), near‑native Hindi, advanced English, and functional Marathi, seamlessly switches among them for writing, media, and daily interactions.
sports_persona: Raul Arun follows cricket passionately, cheer for the Mumbai Indians in the IPL, plays occasional tennis at a local club, and includes daily morning walks in the Thane neighbourhood as part of his fitness routine.
arts_persona: Raul Arun paints watercolour scenes inspired by folk tales, curates exhibitions featuring works of M.F. Husain and Raja Ravi Varma, enjoys concerts of Pandit Jasraj and sitar maestro Ravi Shankar, and often listens to Lata Mangeshkar’s timeless ghazals.
travel_persona: Raul Arun loves heritage tourism, has explored Ajanta & Ellora caves, roamed the pink city of Jaipur, trekked around Hampi’s ruins, and plans a leisurely trip to Goa’s beaches to unwind after the heritage centre launch.
culinary_persona: Raul Arun enjoys cooking traditional sai bhaji and koki, often adds quinoa or kale for a modern twist, loves the tangy taste of tamarind and the aroma of mustard seeds, and prefers dining out at local dhabas for authentic flour‑based rotis and tea.
persona: Raul Arun is a disciplined heritage enthusiast who blends artistic imagination with meticulous organization, occasionally splurging on art books while juggling yoga, community service, and a pinch of coffee‑addiction.
cultural_background: Raul Arun grew up in the vibrant Sindhi Hindu community of Thane, Maharashtra. Being part of a diaspora that migrated from Sindh after Partition, his family preserved Sindhi customs such as Cheti Chand, traditional folk music, and a strong emphasis on entrepreneurship and hospitality. The urban setting of Thane exposed him to a mix of Marathi culture, cosmopolitan lifestyles and modern amenities, while his household remained rooted in Sindhi language, cuisine, and inter‑generational storytelling. This blend of Sindhi heritage and Maharashtrian urban culture shapes his worldview and social interactions.
linguistic_background: Raul's mother tongue is Sindhi, which he reads and writes fluently in the Devanagari script, a skill inherited from his family’s emphasis on preserving their linguistic heritage. He is also highly proficient in Hindi, the lingua franca of Maharashtra, and uses it comfortably in daily interactions, media consumption, and formal writing. His graduate education, completed in English, has equipped him with strong reading and writing abilities in English, enabling him to access academic literature and engage in online communities. Living in Thane, he has acquired basic conversational Marathi, allowing him to navigate local services and social settings. Overall, his multilingual repertoire includes Sindhi (native fluency), Hindi (near‑native fluency), English (advanced proficiency), and functional Marathi.
skills_and_expertise: Raul possesses a blend of cultural, analytical and creative skills honed through decades of community involvement and personal study. He is adept at cultural heritage preservation, especially documenting Sindhi traditions, oral histories, and folk art. His organizational talent shines in planning community festivals and managing volunteer teams. He also has strong financial planning abilities, having overseen family budgeting and charitable fund allocations. Additionally, he is skilled in creative writing, photography, and art appreciation, allowing him to curate exhibitions and author articles on heritage topics. His disciplined mindset supports effective project management and meticulous research.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Cultural Heritage Preservation", "Community Festival Planning", "Financial Planning and Budget Management", "Creative Writing", "Photography", "Art Curation and Appreciation", "Research and Documentation", "Mentoring and Volunteer Coordination", "Multilingual Communication"]
hobbies_and_interests: Raul pursues a range of creative and intellectual hobbies that reflect his curiosity and disciplined nature. He enjoys painting watercolors inspired by Sindhi folklore, reading classical Sindhi poetry and contemporary Indian literature, and practicing photography to capture the everyday vibrancy of Thane’s streets. He is fond of cooking traditional Sindhi dishes such as sai bhaji and koki, often experimenting with modern twists. His interest in history leads him to explore heritage sites across India, while chess and puzzle solving keep his mind sharp. He also participates in community volunteer activities, attends classical music concerts, and practices yoga for physical and mental balance.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Watercolor Painting", "Reading Sindhi Poetry", "Photography", "Cooking Traditional Sindhi Cuisine", "Traveling to Heritage Sites", "Chess", "Puzzle Solving", "Community Volunteering", "Classical Music Concerts", "Yoga"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Though retired, Raul aspires to leave a lasting cultural legacy. He plans to establish a Sindhi Heritage Centre in Thane that will archive oral histories, host workshops on Sindhi arts, and provide a platform for younger Sindhi youth to learn about their roots. He also aims to author a book compiling his research on traditional Sindhi cuisine and folk narratives, and to mentor emerging community leaders. Additionally, he wishes to volunteer as a senior advisor for local NGOs focusing on heritage preservation and social welfare, thereby contributing his organizational expertise and disciplined work ethic to broader societal goals.
persona_clinical_summary: A 42-year-old male, currently married, retired, and graduate-educated, resides in urban Maharashtra, Thane district. He is Sindhi-speaking and proficient in Hindi, with a lifestyle that includes community service and a disciplined routine. His clinical profile is enriched by a heritage-focused background and a preference for traditional yet modern dietary habits.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Thane.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Thane
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 42-year-old male, currently married, retired, and graduate-educated, resides in urban Maharashtra, Thane district. He is Sindhi-speaking and proficient in Hindi, with a lifestyle that includes community service and a disciplined routine. His clinical profile is enriched by a heritage-focused background and a preference for traditional yet modern dietary habits.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: An experienced community storyteller and cultural facilitator, Naveen Gopal blends vivid oral narratives, temple chant leadership, and meticulous event coordination, balancing careful planning with spontaneous creativity, and they draw energy from being the centre of attention at every local gathering.
linguistic_persona: Naveen Gopal, fluent in his native South Indian Dravidian tongue, communicates effortlessly through storytelling, song and daily conversation, yet remains illiterate in any script and has no functional command of the national or colonial languages, making oral expression their primary tool and they rely on it for all communication.
sports_persona: Naveen Gopal follows cricket fervently, cheering for the regional state team and the Chennai Super Kings, enjoys watching badminton on television, and participates in gentle morning walks around the temple courtyard to stay active, and they consider this routine essential for their wellbeing.
arts_persona: Naveen Gopal, an avid lover of Carnatic music, regularly listens to recordings of M.S. Subbulakshmi and appreciates the compositions of Ilaiyaraaja, delights in classic South Indian films starring Rajinikanth and Sivaji Ganesan, and spends his evenings sketching the ornate gopurams of his local temple, and they find these artistic pursuits a source of inner peace.
travel_persona: Naveen Gopal favors modest pilgrimages to nearby sacred towns like Kumbakonam and Rameshwaram, occasional hill‑station retreats to Ooty, and short road trips with friends, planning each journey carefully to fit his modest pension and health considerations, and they view travel as a way to stay connected with tradition.
culinary_persona: Naveen Gopal delights in preparing idli, dosa, sambar and festive sweets such as payasam, favoring ingredients like urad dal, rice, tamarind, jaggery and coconut, though they rarely cook beyond traditional South Indian fare, preferring to enjoy meals at local eateries during festivals.
persona: Naveen Gopal is a charismatic, talkative elder who weaves folk tales, leads temple chants, worries about health and finances, but they find joy in cooking, music, and community gatherings.
cultural_background: Naveen Gopal is a 60‑year‑old Tamil Hindu man who has spent his entire life in the bustling city of Salem, Tamil Nadu. He belongs to the Tamil cultural sphere, characterised by rich Dravidian traditions, vibrant temple festivals, and a strong emphasis on family and community. Growing up in a joint‑family setting, he absorbed values of respect for elders, communal harmony and devotion to Hindu rituals. Although he never married, his social life revolves around neighbours, extended relatives and local temple circles, where his charismatic presence often makes him the centre of attention. The urban environment of Salem provides him with access to busy markets, temples and cultural gatherings, shaping his worldview with a blend of tradition and modern city life.
linguistic_background: Naveen's mother tongue is Tamil, which he uses fluently for everyday conversation, storytelling, and reciting devotional hymns. He lacks formal literacy, so he cannot read or write Tamil or any other script. He also does not possess functional knowledge of Hindi or English, as he never learned these languages in school. Consequently, his communication is primarily oral, relying on his vivid imagination and expressive voice.
skills_and_expertise: Naveen has honed a range of practical and cultural skills over his lifetime. He is an adept oral storyteller, capable of weaving traditional Tamil folklore and mythological tales with vivid detail. He possesses a deep knowledge of Tamil devotional music and can sing bhajans with a resonant voice. His cooking abilities include preparing authentic South Indian dishes such as idli, dosa, sambar, and traditional festive sweets. He is also experienced in organizing community gatherings, coordinating temple festivals, and managing household tasks efficiently. His sociable nature makes him a natural facilitator in group conversations and informal gatherings.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Oral storytelling", "Tamil devotional music", "South Indian cooking", "Community event coordination", "Household management"]
hobbies_and_interests: Naveen enjoys immersing himself in the rich tapestry of Tamil culture. He spends his leisure time listening to Carnatic concerts and Tamil devotional songs, watching classic Tamil movies, tending a small vegetable garden, experimenting with new recipes, and sketching temple architecture. Social gatherings, especially temple festivals and community tea‑breaks, delight him, and he often engages in lively conversations with friends and neighbours, sharing anecdotes and jokes.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Carnatic music", "Watching classic Tamil movies", "Vegetable gardening", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Sketching temple architecture", "Attending temple festivals", "Socializing with friends and neighbours", "Storytelling and sharing anecdotes"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Looking ahead, Naveen aspires to deepen his contribution to the cultural life of his neighbourhood. He wishes to conduct regular storytelling workshops for schoolchildren, share his culinary expertise by hosting community feasts during festivals, and volunteer as an assistant in temple rituals, helping priests with preparations and recitations. While he does not seek formal employment, his ambition is to preserve Tamil heritage, mentor the younger generation, and find personal fulfillment through active community service and creative expression.
persona_clinical_summary: A 60-year-old male from Salem, Tamil Nadu, who is never married, retired, and illiterate, speaks Tamil. He resides in an urban zone and reports concerns about health and finances. His lifestyle includes community engagement, temple activities, and traditional cooking.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

முக்கிய அறிகுறிகள் / தீவிரத்தன்மை: நோயாளி விழிப்புடன், தெளிவான மனநிலையுடன், மூன்று நிலைகளிலும் (x3) விழிப்புணர்வுடன் உள்ளார். முக்கிய அறிகுறிகள்: இரத்த அழுத்தம் 150/90 mmHg, இதயத் துடிப்பு 95 bpm, சுவாச விகிதம் 18/நிமிடம், அறை காற்றில் SpO2 96%, வெப்பநிலை 37.2°C. நோயாளிக்கு திடீரென கடுமையான மார்பு வலி ஏற்பட்டு, இடது கையில் பரவுகிறது; அதனுடன் மூச்சுத்திணறல் மற்றும் வியர்வை (diaphoresis) காணப்படுகிறது. வலி 8/10 தீவிரத்தில் இருப்பதாக விவரிக்கப்பட்டுள்ளது. நோயாளி பதற்றமாக உள்ளார். ஆரம்பகட்ட ECG சோதனையில் V2-V4 லீட்களில் ST உயர்வு (ST elevation) காணப்படுகிறது. அவசர நரம்பு வழி (IV) அணுகல் ஏற்படுத்தப்பட்டது, ஆஸ்பிரின் 300 மி.கி. மெல்ல வழங்கப்பட்டது, குளோபிடோக்ரல் 300 மி.கி. ஏற்றப்பட்டது. இதயக் கண்காணிப்புக் கருவி இணைக்கப்பட்டது. அவசர ஆய்வகப் பரிசோதனைகள் மற்றும் இதய என்சைம்கள் (cardiac enzymes) உத்தரவிடப்பட்டன. அவசர இதய சிகிச்சை அறை (cath lab) செயல்பாட்டிற்கு நோயாளி தயார் செய்யப்படுகிறார்.

professional_persona: An experienced community storyteller and cultural facilitator, Naveen Gopal blends vivid oral narratives, temple chant leadership, and meticulous event coordination, balancing careful planning with spontaneous creativity, and they draw energy from being the centre of attention at every local gathering.
linguistic_persona: Naveen Gopal, fluent in his native South Indian Dravidian tongue, communicates effortlessly through storytelling, song and daily conversation, yet remains illiterate in any script and has no functional command of the national or colonial languages, making oral expression their primary tool and they rely on it for all communication.
sports_persona: Naveen Gopal follows cricket fervently, cheering for the regional state team and the Chennai Super Kings, enjoys watching badminton on television, and participates in gentle morning walks around the temple courtyard to stay active, and they consider this routine essential for their wellbeing.
arts_persona: Naveen Gopal, an avid lover of Carnatic music, regularly listens to recordings of M.S. Subbulakshmi and appreciates the compositions of Ilaiyaraaja, delights in classic South Indian films starring Rajinikanth and Sivaji Ganesan, and spends his evenings sketching the ornate gopurams of his local temple, and they find these artistic pursuits a source of inner peace.
travel_persona: Naveen Gopal favors modest pilgrimages to nearby sacred towns like Kumbakonam and Rameshwaram, occasional hill‑station retreats to Ooty, and short road trips with friends, planning each journey carefully to fit his modest pension and health considerations, and they view travel as a way to stay connected with tradition.
culinary_persona: Naveen Gopal delights in preparing idli, dosa, sambar and festive sweets such as payasam, favoring ingredients like urad dal, rice, tamarind, jaggery and coconut, though they rarely cook beyond traditional South Indian fare, preferring to enjoy meals at local eateries during festivals.
persona: Naveen Gopal is a charismatic, talkative elder who weaves folk tales, leads temple chants, worries about health and finances, but they find joy in cooking, music, and community gatherings.
cultural_background: Naveen Gopal is a 60‑year‑old Tamil Hindu man who has spent his entire life in the bustling city of Salem, Tamil Nadu. He belongs to the Tamil cultural sphere, characterised by rich Dravidian traditions, vibrant temple festivals, and a strong emphasis on family and community. Growing up in a joint‑family setting, he absorbed values of respect for elders, communal harmony and devotion to Hindu rituals. Although he never married, his social life revolves around neighbours, extended relatives and local temple circles, where his charismatic presence often makes him the centre of attention. The urban environment of Salem provides him with access to busy markets, temples and cultural gatherings, shaping his worldview with a blend of tradition and modern city life.
linguistic_background: Naveen's mother tongue is Tamil, which he uses fluently for everyday conversation, storytelling, and reciting devotional hymns. He lacks formal literacy, so he cannot read or write Tamil or any other script. He also does not possess functional knowledge of Hindi or English, as he never learned these languages in school. Consequently, his communication is primarily oral, relying on his vivid imagination and expressive voice.
skills_and_expertise: Naveen has honed a range of practical and cultural skills over his lifetime. He is an adept oral storyteller, capable of weaving traditional Tamil folklore and mythological tales with vivid detail. He possesses a deep knowledge of Tamil devotional music and can sing bhajans with a resonant voice. His cooking abilities include preparing authentic South Indian dishes such as idli, dosa, sambar, and traditional festive sweets. He is also experienced in organizing community gatherings, coordinating temple festivals, and managing household tasks efficiently. His sociable nature makes him a natural facilitator in group conversations and informal gatherings.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Oral storytelling", "Tamil devotional music", "South Indian cooking", "Community event coordination", "Household management"]
hobbies_and_interests: Naveen enjoys immersing himself in the rich tapestry of Tamil culture. He spends his leisure time listening to Carnatic concerts and Tamil devotional songs, watching classic Tamil movies, tending a small vegetable garden, experimenting with new recipes, and sketching temple architecture. Social gatherings, especially temple festivals and community tea‑breaks, delight him, and he often engages in lively conversations with friends and neighbours, sharing anecdotes and jokes.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Carnatic music", "Watching classic Tamil movies", "Vegetable gardening", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Sketching temple architecture", "Attending temple festivals", "Socializing with friends and neighbours", "Storytelling and sharing anecdotes"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Looking ahead, Naveen aspires to deepen his contribution to the cultural life of his neighbourhood. He wishes to conduct regular storytelling workshops for schoolchildren, share his culinary expertise by hosting community feasts during festivals, and volunteer as an assistant in temple rituals, helping priests with preparations and recitations. While he does not seek formal employment, his ambition is to preserve Tamil heritage, mentor the younger generation, and find personal fulfillment through active community service and creative expression.
persona_clinical_summary: A 60-year-old male from Salem, Tamil Nadu, who is never married, retired, and illiterate, speaks Tamil as his first language and resides in an urban zone. He presents with lifestyle-related concerns regarding health and finances, though he maintains a routine of community gatherings, temple activities, and traditional cooking. His daily life includes gentle morning walks, participation in local festivals, and engagement with oral storytelling and music.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]

  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Tamil (code=ta, script=Tamil). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Salem.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Tamil for Tamil.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 60
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Tamil Nadu
- district: Salem
- first_language: Tamil
- clinical_persona_summary: A 60-year-old male from Salem, Tamil Nadu, who is never married, retired, and illiterate, speaks Tamil as his first language and resides in an urban zone. He presents with lifestyle-related concerns regarding health and finances, though he maintains a routine of community gatherings, temple activities, and traditional cooking. His daily life includes gentle morning walks, participation in local festivals, and engagement with oral storytelling and music.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:

நோயாளி [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramanathan]] [[AGE|60]] [[GENDER|Male]]
பணி [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-001]] இணையதளம் [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
வாடிக்கையாளர் IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] சாதன IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] தொலைபேசி [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
மின்னஞ்சல் [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|ramanathan.s@example.com]] மருத்துவமனை [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Hospital Salem]]
--- உரையாடல் ---
நோயாளி: மருத்துவரே, கடந்த இரண்டு நாட்களாக எனது வலது கீழ் வயிற்றுப் பகுதியில் கடுமையான வலி ஏற்படுகிறது. நடக்கும்போது வலி அதிகமாகிறது. எனக்கு குமட்டல் உணர்வும் ஏற்பட்டுள்ளது, வாயுவை வெளியேற்ற முடியவில்லை.
மருத்துவர் [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: ராமநாதன், உங்கள் கவலை எனக்குப் புரிகிறது. உங்கள் அறிகுறிகளான வயிற்று வலி, குமட்டல் மற்றும் வாயு வெளியேற இயலாமை ஆகியவற்றைக் கருத்தில் கொண்டு, இது குடல் அடைப்பு அல்லது குடல்வால் அழற்சியின் அறிகுறியாக இருக்கலாம். நான் உடனடியாக இரத்தப் பரிசோதனை மற்றும் வயிற்றுப் பகுதி அல்ட்ராசவுண்ட் செய்ய ஏற்பாடு செய்கிறேன். இன்று சேலம் அரசு மருத்துவமனைக்குச் சென்று அவசர சிகிச்சைப் பிரிவில் கேளுங்கள்.
நோயாளி: சரி மருத்துவரே. பரிசோதனைகள் எதைக் காட்டும்?
மருத்துவர் [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: இரத்தப் பரிசோதனை தொற்று அல்லது அழற்சி அறிகுறிகளைச் சரிபார்க்கும். அல்ட்ராசவுண்ட் உங்கள் குடல் மற்றும் குடல்வால் ஆகியவற்றின் படங்களை உருவாக்கி, அடைப்பு அல்லது வீக்கம் உள்ளதா என்று பார்க்கும். முடிவுகள் நோயறிதலை உறுதிப்படுத்த உதவும்.
நோயாளி: செலவு குறித்து நான் மிகவும் கவலைப்படுகிறேன். நான் ஓய்வு பெற்ற நபர், வருமானம் இல்லை.
மருத்துவர் [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: நிதி குறித்த உங்கள் கவலை எனக்குப் புரிகிறது. சேலம் அரசு மருத்துவமனையில் செய்யப்படும் பரிசோதனைகள் மற்றும் ஆலோசனை, முதலமைச்சர் சுகாதாரத் திட்டத்தின் கீழ் அனைத்து முதிய குடிமக்களுக்கும் இலவசம். நீங்கள் எதையும் செலுத்த வேண்டியதில்லை. இப்போது மருத்துவமனைக்குச் செல்லுங்கள்.
நோயாளி: நன்றி மருத்துவரே. நான் உடனடியாகச் செல்கிறேன்.
மருத்துவர் [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: நீங்கள் வரவேற்கப்படுகிறீர்கள், ராமநாதன். மருத்துவரைப் பார்க்கும் வரை எதையும் சாப்பிடவோ குடிக்கவோ வேண்டாம். நாங்கள் உங்களைக் கவனித்துக் கொள்வோம்.

--- session ---

Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|ramanathan.s@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Hospital Salem]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Doctor, I am having severe pain in my right lower abdomen for the past two days. It is a sharp, constant pain that gets worse when I walk. I also feel a bit nauseous and have not been able to pass gas.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: Ramanathan, I understand your concern. Based on your symptoms of localized lower abdominal pain, nausea, and inability to pass gas, this could be a sign of an intestinal obstruction or appendicitis. I am arranging for an immediate blood test and an ultrasound of your abdomen. Please go to the Government Hospital Salem today and ask for the emergency department.
Patient: Okay doctor. What will the tests show?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: The blood test will check for signs of infection or inflammation. The ultrasound will create images of your intestines and appendix to see if there is a blockage or swelling. The results will help us confirm the diagnosis.
Patient: I am very worried about the cost. I am a retired person with no income.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: I understand your concern about finances. The tests and consultation at the Government Hospital Salem are free for all senior citizens under the Chief Minister's Health Scheme. You do not need to pay anything. Please go to the hospital now.
Patient: Thank you doctor. I will go immediately.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Kumar]]: You are welcome, Ramanathan. Do not eat or drink anything until you see the doctor. We will take care of you.
judge_reasoning: Tamil prose, valid allow-listed entities, strong domain-persona fit, no script or length issues.

professional_persona: Enkateshwarlu Atloori, a hands-on tobacco curing specialist who manages drying chambers, moisture sensors and budgeting, blends curiosity for low-cost tech with a relaxed, sometimes disorganized work style, using practical know-how to keep their family’s operations running smoothly.
linguistic_persona: Enkateshwarlu Atloori, a native Telugu speaker fluent in reading, writing and speaking his mother tongue, possesses functional English for everyday tasks but lacks Hindi literacy, relying on visual cues from movies and signs when needed, and they often switch between languages with ease in family gatherings.
sports_persona: Enkateshwarlu Atloori, a devoted cricket fan who never misses a Sunrisers Hyderabad game on TV, plays occasional friendly card tournaments, stays fit with casual walks along the Godavari banks, but they do not belong to any formal sports club.
arts_persona: Enkateshwarlu Atloori, an avid listener of Telugu folk songs and bhajans by legends like Ghantasala and S. P. Balasubrahmanyam, enjoys evenings watching classic Telugu films starring Chiranjeevi, and they often hum these melodies while experimenting with traditional recipes, letting music shape his mood and creativity.
travel_persona: Enkateshwarlu Atloori, prefers low-key family road trips to nearby pilgrim spots like Tirupati and coastal getaways at Visakhapatnam, plans journeys at the last minute using his phone’s map apps, and balances his love for exploring with the need to manage farm duties and limited savings, and they often travel with a small group of close relatives.
culinary_persona: Enkateshwarlu Atloori, relishes coastal Andhra cuisine such as spicy chilichintha (chili pickle), crisp pesarattu, tangy pulihora (tamarind rice) and creamy coconut rice pudding, cooks with fresh coconut, tamarind and mustard seeds, often experiments by adding millet flour to traditional dishes, yet usually relies on their mother’s recipes for everyday meals.
persona: Enkateshwarlu Atloori is a practical, curiosity-driven tobacco curer juggling family duties, modest savings, occasional health worries and a love for folk music, often forgetting to file paperwork on time.
cultural_background: Enkateshwarlu belongs to a Telugu-speaking community in the East Godavari region of Andhra Pradesh. His family follows the traditional joint family system, celebrates festivals like Ugadi, Sankranti, and Bathukamma, enjoys coastal cuisine featuring rice, coconut and spicy pickles, and participates in community gatherings at local temples. Living in an urban centre of East Godavari exposes him to modern amenities while retaining the agrarian vibe of the Godavari delta.
linguistic_background: Telugu is his mother tongue and he is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing it. His formal schooling up to matriculation gave him basic literacy in English, enabling him to understand simple instructions and fill out work-related forms, though his proficiency remains functional rather than academic. He does not speak or read Hindi, but he can recognise common Hindi words from movies and signage.
skills_and_expertise: He has hands‑on expertise in curing tobacco, including knowledge of air‑curing, flue‑curing, and moisture control techniques. Over the years he has acquired skills in operating drying chambers, maintaining equipment, and ensuring product quality. He also manages basic business operations such as procurement of raw leaves, budgeting, and record‑keeping. His practical experience extends to agricultural awareness, basic carpentry for workshop repairs, and familiarity with local market dynamics.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Tobacco curing (air and flue curing)', 'Moisture control and quality assessment', 'Operation and maintenance of drying equipment', 'Basic procurement and inventory management', 'Simple bookkeeping and budgeting', 'Basic carpentry for workshop repairs', 'Understanding of local market dynamics', 'Knowledge of agricultural practices relevant to tobacco']
hobbies_and_interests: In his spare time Enkateshwarlu enjoys listening to Telugu folk music and bhajans, watching cricket matches on television, and reading simple magazines on agriculture and DIY projects. He likes taking occasional walks along the Godavari River banks, tending to a small vegetable garden at home, and experimenting with traditional recipes in the kitchen. Occasionally he joins friends for informal card games, but prefers quiet evenings to unwind.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Telugu folk music and bhajans', 'Watching cricket on TV', 'Reading agriculture and DIY magazines', 'Walking along the Godavari River', 'Home vegetable gardening', 'Cooking traditional Telugu recipes', 'Playing informal card games with friends']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Enkateshwarlu aims to upgrade his tobacco curing operations by incorporating low‑cost, energy‑efficient technologies such as solar drying racks or digital moisture sensors, which would boost productivity while keeping costs manageable. He also dreams of diversifying his livelihood by venturing into supplementary agricultural ventures like millets or spice processing, thereby reducing dependence on a single crop. To achieve these goals, he plans to attend short‑term vocational training in agri‑technology and basic entrepreneurship, balancing his learning with family responsibilities.
persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Enkateshwarlu Atloori, 32, is a male tobacco curer in East Godavari, Andhra Pradesh, married with a matriculation education. He resides in an urban zone and speaks Telugu as his first language, with basic English literacy. His lifestyle includes occasional health concerns and a love for folk music.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=East Godavari.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Tobacco Curer
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: East Godavari
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Enkateshwarlu Atloori, 32, is a male tobacco curer in East Godavari, Andhra Pradesh, married with a matriculation education. He resides in an urban zone and speaks Telugu as his first language, with basic English literacy. His lifestyle includes occasional health concerns and a love for folk music.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
